game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with six players. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their whole endowment (20) to the public pot or to keep it (0 contributed). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally among all six players. Individually, keeping your 20 always gives you 20 more than contributing, holding others' contributions fixed; defecting (keeping 20) strictly dominates cooperating (contributing 20) in one-shot incentives. Socially, however, everyone is best off if all contribute.

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect early rounds to include some experimentation and signalling: a few players may contribute to test cooperation or to signal a cooperative norm, while others will free-ride to exploit those contributions. Over time, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior — a small group may cooperate, others free-ride.
- Middle rounds: some reciprocity or attempts to build cooperation may appear (conditional cooperators join when they see some contributions), possibly raising contributions for short runs.
- Later rounds: without strong enforcement, contributions tend to decline as defectors earn higher round payoffs and cooperators withdraw, especially because there is no final-round known to trigger a last-minute cooperative surge.

Chat is usually sparse and tactical: brief promises, requests for cooperation, or short reactions to payoffs. I'll simulate that pattern: a few players occasionally commit to contribute, others respond (or not), and contributions fluctuate with an overall downward tendency.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a classic N-person public goods game with six players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute all 20 coins or contribute nothing. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly across all six players. Individually, any coin you keep yields 1 to you, while any coin you contribute yields 3.7/6 ≈ 0.617 back to you. So the group is best off if everyone contributes, but the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because each contributed coin gives less than 1 back to the contributor.

Typical dynamics: many groups start with some cooperation (pledges or unconditional contributions) but, as the rounds proceed and players observe payoffs and others' behavior, free-riding tends to increase. Sparse messages can either coordinate continued cooperation (promises, calls for reciprocity) or express frustration after being exploited, accelerating defection. Uncertainty about the total number of rounds means there's no guaranteed end-game collapse, but without strong enforcement or reliable reciprocity, contributions usually decline over time.

I will simulate a plausible path across 12 rounds: an initially cooperative first round, some exploitation and mixed contributions in middle rounds, a period of widespread defection, occasional attempts to re-start cooperation, and a modest final push by a couple players in the last round. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (pledges, brief acknowledgements, complaints).

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with 6 players and a per-round endowment of 20 coins. Each player either contributes all 20 or keeps all 20; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. The private return from contributing is smaller than the personal cost (your contribution raises everyone’s share by about 12.33 coins but costs you 20), so the dominant short-term incentive is to free-ride. However, because players do not know when the game will end, repeated interaction and communication can sustain cooperation early on: players may test contribute and try to coordinate, and some will reciprocate or retaliate based on observed history.

Typical dynamics: initial rounds often show some cooperation as players test each other and try to coordinate (especially with messages). If some players free-ride and earn more, contributors may reduce contributions in later rounds, producing a decline. Occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation can appear if a few players use chat to promise contributions, but each successful contribution still makes contributors worse off than free-riders that round, so cooperation tends to be fragile and often declines or fluctuates. Because players see each round’s payoffs, they can infer who contributed (contributors get the lower of two payoff levels), and that information shapes subsequent rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, 20-coin endowment each round, and a pot multiplier of 3.7. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and payoffs are split equally, any individual who defects in a round gets a private advantage over contributors in that same round. Historically, such games often start with some willingness to contribute (trust/experimentation), but contributions tend to decline over time as players respond to observed payoffs and free-riding. Communication (cheap talk) can sometimes sustain coordination if enough people signal and follow through, but because the game length is unknown and choices are anonymous, cooperation typically decays with occasional attempts to restart cooperation by a few players. I therefore predict an early moderate level of contributions, followed by a steady decline, with a few isolated attempts to re-establish cooperation via chat and unilateral contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with 6 players who each choose either 0 or 20 coins to contribute. Each contributed coin is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all 6 players. Individually, contributing is costly: a player who contributes 20 loses 20 but only recovers 74/6 ≈ 12.33 of that contribution, so contributing reduces a player's own round payoff relative to free-riding. Group payoffs are highest if everyone contributes (each gets 74), but the dominant individual action is to free-ride.

Because players do not know how many rounds the game will last, there's uncertainty about whether cooperation might be sustained: without a known final round, some players may try to establish cooperation early via chat, while others will test by free-riding to gain immediate advantage. Typical dynamics: some early cooperation attempts (especially if a few promise to contribute), followed by partial unraveling as free-riders earn more and punish contributors' payoffs. Occasional short-lived attempts to restore cooperation via messages can occur, but by mid-to-late rounds many groups tend toward low contributions or all non-contribution. Chat is sparse and mainly used to promise, complain, or explain choices.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with uncertainty about length. Each round everyone chooses between full contribution (20) or no contribution (0). Contributing helps the group because each contributed 20 yields 3.7*20 = 74 added to the pot, which is split across all six players (~12.33 per player per 20 contributed). Individually, contributing costs 20 private coins while returning only ~12.33 to the contributor — a personal net loss. That creates a strong short-term incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) while still making group-level cooperation beneficial.

Given these incentives and the unknown horizon, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: some players try cooperating to test reciprocity; others may free-ride to exploit those cooperators.
- Middle rounds: contributions tend to fall as conditional cooperators respond to being exploited; occasional attempts at revival of cooperation occur via messages.
- Late rounds: without knowledge of the end, some players may still try to coordinate, but overall average contributions often decline. Chat is sparse and used for pledges, complaints, or short coordination attempts.

I will simulate plausible behavior across 12 rounds. Chats are sparse and purposeful (pledges, reactions, short coordination). At each round I show a brief explanation of expected behavior, then the chat and contributions, then the round summary listing each player's net payoff for that round (their private remainder plus share from the multiplied pot).

game_explanation:
This is a standard one-shot repeated public goods game with 6 players each round. Each player privately gets 20 coins and must choose either to contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the 6 players. Individually, contributing is costly: your contribution raises everyone's share but returns only (20 * 3.7) / 6 = 12.333... coins to you, so each contribution reduces your private payoff by about 7.67 compared with free-riding. Socially, however, more contributors raise the total group payoff and can make everyone better off if enough people contribute.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often show some cooperation (promises, a few contributors) because the horizon is unknown and people try to build mutual benefit.
- Free-riding is individually dominant, so defectors tend to do better in mixed groups; after seeing payoffs, contributors may become discouraged and reduce cooperation.
- Sparse, purposeful chat can sometimes coordinate cooperation (e.g., agreements to all contribute), producing high-payoff rounds; but a single defector can quickly undermine cooperation.
- Because players do not know how many rounds remain, cooperation may persist longer than in a known finite-horizon game, but it will likely fluctuate: spurts of cooperation and reversion to defection.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a classic public goods game with a 3.7 multiplier and six players. Each round every player can either keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) or put the entire 20 into the group pot. The group return is relatively high (one full round of contributions of 20 by everyone yields 74 per player), so full cooperation is collectively best. But individual incentive is to free-ride: if the five others contribute and you keep your 20, you earn more than if you contributed. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), behavior tends to be stark: some players repeatedly contribute (conditional cooperators or committed cooperators), some free-ride repeatedly, and some switch depending on what they see in past rounds.

Uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- Messages can be used to coordinate cooperation, warn free-riders, or signal punishment. Chat is sparse in real play, so short, strategic messages are most likely.
- Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we should expect less systematic “endgame” collapse; cooperation can persist throughout as long as people believe cooperation might continue.
- Conditional cooperation and targeted punishment are likely: a few cooperators may try to sustain high contributions by messaging, while others (especially if they get repeated high payoffs from free-riding) may keep defecting. Occasional rounds of mass defection can occur as punishment or out of frustration.
- Over repeated rounds a plausible pattern is: some stable cooperators (contribute most rounds), one or two persistent free-riders, intermittent retaliatory or restorative rounds, and sparse chat trying to restore cooperation.

The transcript below simulates a plausible 12-round run with those dynamics: one player (SLOTH) often free-rides; several players try to sustain cooperation (PARROT, GORILLA, DUCK, DOG), with occasional punishments and a short period of full defection. Chat is limited and purposeful.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a classic public goods game with a strong social optimum (everyone contributing yields the highest group payoff) but a clear individual incentive to free-ride each round. Each player must choose 0 or 20; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players. Contributing increases the group's pot but, because the benefit is shared, a single contributor is usually worse off than a defector in the same round. Players don't know the total number of rounds, so we can expect:
- Early rounds: experimentation and signaling (short messages like "I'll contribute") as players test cooperation.
- Middle rounds: occasional waves of cooperation if a few players try to coordinate, but successful coordination invites profitable one-shot defections (temptation to free-ride).
- Later rounds: cooperation often unravels after visible exploitation; intermittent attempts to re-establish cooperation via messages are possible.
Chat will be sparse and strategic: promises, complaints when someone benefits from deviating, and occasional coordination attempts.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players, binary contributions (0 or 20) and a fairly large multiplication factor (3.7) that is shared equally. Two tensions will shape play. First, when most players contribute, defecting yields a higher round payoff than cooperating (you save 20 and still benefit from the multiplied pot), so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Second, because the game repeats (players don't know when it will end), there is room for conditional cooperation: some players may try to build patterns of mutual contributions by signaling in chat or reciprocating previous contributors. Expect initial experimentation and a few attempts at coordination through short messages, but also repeated temptation to defect. Over 12 rounds we typically see mixed dynamics: spikes of partial cooperation after explicit proposals or promises, but a general drift toward non-contribution because defecting typically gives higher individual round payoffs and there is limited enforcement. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to propose or explain choices; payoffs will show that free-riders often earn more in rounds when others contribute.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard one-shot-style public goods game repeated for an unknown number of rounds. Each round each player can either contribute all 20 coins or keep them. The pot multiplies contributions by 3.7 and splits equally across the six players. Individually, keeping your 20 is always a private gain (you keep 20 plus you still receive your share of the public pot), so free-riding is the dominant strategy in any single round. Collectively, however, more contributors always raise total group payoff and average payoff.

Likely dynamics: early rounds often see a mix — some players try contributing to build a norm, others free-ride and earn more in the short run. Once players see they were exploited (they contributed while others kept theirs), they usually stop contributing. Because players observe peers' net payoffs at summary (from which contributions can be inferred), attempts at cooperation are quickly punished if not reciprocated. That tends to produce an initial small level of cooperation that decays over time, with occasional brief attempts to restart cooperation via messages but little sustained recovery. Chat messages will be sparse and mainly promises, brief coordination attempts, and short reactions to payoffs.

game_explanation: This is a repeated binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players decide whether to contribute all 20 coins to a shared pot (cooperate) or keep them (defect). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally, so contributors raise the group's return but defectors get a larger private payoff in the short run. Because players don't know the total number of rounds, we expect some cooperation to emerge early (conditional cooperators) and punishment to be used sparingly to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and harsher to the target (5 coins per unit), so players will typically punish only when defection is visible and when they think it will promote future cooperation. Chat messages will be infrequent and purposeful (warnings, requests to cooperate, or short reactions to punishment). Overall dynamics: initial mixed contributions, a few targeted punishments to deter free-riding, gradual stabilization toward cooperation, occasional experiments/defections, and selective punishments (sometimes leading to brief retaliations).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players. Each round each player must either keep their 20-coin endowment or contribute it to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so the group gets more if many contribute, but each individual's private return from contributing is smaller than the private cost: a single player's contribution helps everyone but is individually costly compared to free-riding. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, cooperation can be sustained by reciprocity, reputation, and punishment. Punishment is costly to deliver (2 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger penalty on the target (5 coins per unit), so occasional targeted punishment can deter free-riding without widespread escalation.

Typical dynamics: early rounds see some experimentation—some players contribute, some test free-riding. If cooperators can coordinate (through sparse messages) and threaten punishment, contributions tend to rise. Punishment is usually used sparingly and targeted at repeated or blatant defectors. Retaliation is possible (players can see who punished them), which can lead to short punishment cycles, but because punishment is costly, most players revert to cooperation after a short exchange. Messages are used rarely to call for cooperation or to signal intentions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and evenly shared, so if many contribute the group payoff rises, but any single defector who keeps 20 benefits from others' contributions. Because players can punish others after seeing revealed contributions, a common dynamic is:
- Early rounds: some players test the waters (defect) while others contribute. Visible defection invites targeted punishment because punishers prefer to establish cooperation norms despite the personal cost of punishing.
- Middle rounds: punishments reduce the advantage of defecting, and cooperation tends to increase as defectors switch to contributing. Punishment is used sparingly — usually a few low-intensity punishments targeted at clear defectors.
- Later rounds: with repeated interactions and identity-based punishment information, cooperation is largely sustained; occasional tests (one-off defections) are met with small punishments. Because players do not know when the game ends, there's no guaranteed final-round collapse; norms established by punishment can persist.

Across rounds I will predict a plausible path: initial mixed contributions, targeted punishment of obvious free-riders, convergence toward high cooperation with occasional isolated defections that are punished lightly. Chat messages will be few and purposeful (warnings, complaints, short acknowledgements). Punishments will be sparse and targeted; only non-zero actions will be listed.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players decide whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are socially beneficial (multiplied by 3.7 and shared) but individually costly, so the dominant strategy in a one-shot choice is to free-ride. Because players can punish after seeing contributions, we should expect a tension: some players will contribute and use punishment sparingly to discipline free-riders; others will try to free-ride and may retaliate if punished. Chats are possible and often used to signal intentions or complain. Early rounds typically show a mix of cooperation and defection; timely, modest punishments (1–2 units) can sustain cooperation for many rounds. Excessive or retaliatory punishment can trigger cycles of retaliation and brief drops in cooperation. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, end-game collapse is less likely; we thus predict generally high contributions after the first few rounds, punctuated by occasional defections and limited punishments to enforce norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) to a common pot. The pot is multiplied (3.7×) and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can spend private coins to punish others; each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 5 coins. Players can also send chat messages at any time. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, early rounds typically establish behavioral norms: some players test others by free-riding, and cooperators may use punishment sparingly to enforce contribution. Punishment is costly to administer, so it is used mainly to curb repeated or blatant free-riding rather than occasional deviations. Expect cooperation to rise after punishments, with occasional defections and targeted, limited punishments. Chats tend to be few and focused (requests, threats, apologies).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with a 3.7 multiplier and six players. Each round every player privately chooses either to keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) or to contribute the full 20 to the group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and then split equally, so each coin contributed returns only about 0.617 coins to each player. That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride (keep your 20) while harming group payoff. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds typically show some cooperation driven by reciprocity and social norms; anonymous free-riding often provokes targeted, costly punishment to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) but harsher to the target (5 coins deducted per unit), so a few punishments can be effective at deterring repeated defection. Messages are sparse and usually used to coordinate or to call out defectors. In these simulated 12 rounds I predict a pattern of mostly cooperative behavior maintained by occasional low-level punishments directed at repeat defectors or one-off “tests,” with chat used slightly to request cooperation or to note punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game among six players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone chooses either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the public pot or to keep it (0). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. The marginal per-capita return (3.7/6 ≈ 0.62) is less than 1, so the short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because any individual loses 20 by contributing but gains only about 12.33 (if everyone else contributes 20) from that action; however, the group as a whole gets more if many contribute. Punishment lets players deter free-riding by paying 2 coins per punishment unit to reduce a target’s earnings by 5 coins; punishment is visible and identified, which enables targeted retaliation or reputation effects.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often show a mix of cooperation and free-riding as players test each other. Cooperators may punish clear defectors to enforce contributions; punishers pay a cost and so use punishment sparingly. If punishment is concentrated on repeat defectors, cooperation tends to rise. Occasional defections (testing or exploitation) and modest punishments reappear across rounds; retaliation can occur but is also costly, so cycles stabilize toward substantial cooperation with occasional deviations. Chat messages tend to be few and aimed at coordination or calling out defectors.

game_explanation:
This 6-player public goods game gives each player 20 coins per round and a binary choice: contribute 20 (cooperate) or 0 (defect). Contributions are multiplied (×3.7) and split equally; thus a cooperative act benefits the group but is individually costly. Because punishments are available (cost 2 per punishment unit to source, 5 deducted per unit from target), players can enforce norms by paying to reduce a free-rider's payoff. Players do not know how many rounds will be played, so they have an incentive to maintain cooperation to earn future returns from mutual contributions — and to punish occasional defectors to deter them.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often test cooperation — a few players may defect to gain immediate benefit. Other players then punish defectors sparingly (1–2 units) to signal intolerance. Punishment is costly but can stabilize cooperation; conditional cooperators respond to sanctions by contributing in later rounds. Retaliatory punishments can appear but are usually limited because punishment is costly and identification of punishers makes cycles of revenge risky. Chat is used sparsely to coordinate norms or express frustration. Because players don’t know the horizon, cooperation tends to persist across rounds, with occasional defection and targeted, modest punishments.

game_explanation: This public goods game sets up a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and group incentives (contribute 20 so the pot grows and everyone benefits). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are stark: contributing costs you 20 immediately but yields a share of the multiplied pot; free-riding (0) yields immediate private benefit but reduces the group's total payoff. The punishment stage lets players punish defectors at personal cost (2 per unit) that reduces the target's earnings (5 per unit), so punishment is an enforcement device: it can sustain cooperation if used selectively. Because punishment is costly, players typically punish sparingly and target clear defectors to deter future free-riding. Communication is possible but limited and observable, so short normative messages (requests to contribute, warnings after defection) are common. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so we should see stable cooperation maintained via occasional targeted punishment of deviators, with sparse chat: a few warnings, apologies, and occasional retaliation or testing. Below is a plausible 12-round transcript that follows those patterns.

game_explanation:
This is a standard voluntary contribution public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and costly punishment. Individually, contributing 20 yields a personal loss (you give up 20 but each player gains only 12.33 from that contribution), so the selfish best response is to free-ride. But free-riding harms group welfare, and costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit for sender, 5 coins deducted per unit from the target) allows cooperative players to discipline defectors. Because players can identify who punished them, punishment can deter free-riding but can also provoke retaliation. Communication is allowed but typically sparse and used to coordinate or call out defections. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which typically supports continued cooperation if punishment is credible. I therefore expect:
- Some players to initially test or free-ride, others to signal cooperation.
- A small number of targeted punishments by a few enforcement-oriented players (not blanket punishment).
- Occasional retaliatory punishments in response to being penalized, but not sustained cycles because punishment is costly.
- Over rounds cooperation tends to increase after punishment enforces norms, with occasional tests/defections later on and small corrective punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with costly rewards. Individually, contributing to the public pot is dominated (you keep more privately by withholding), but the group gets more if many contribute because the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared. Expect a mixture: some stable cooperators who contribute a lot, some persistent free-riders (they contribute little or nothing), and many conditional cooperators who respond to others' behavior and to rewards. Because players can reward after outcomes, modest positive rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce contributors early and mid-game; rewards are costly to give, so only a few units will be sent to signal appreciation. Over time, as the end approaches, cooperation typically declines (backward induction / end-game effect) and reward activity falls off. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to state intent, praise, or thank others.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds. Each round players decide simultaneously how many of their 20 private coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so each player's private payoff from the public good depends on the sum of everyone's contributions. After contributions are revealed and redistributed, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins per unit) to give small positive transfers (2 coins per unit) to specific others. Players see private round summaries (their net payoff, how much they spent rewarding, and how much they received) and can identify who rewarded them. Players may also chat at any time.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The marginal per-player return from the public pot is 4.5 / 16 = 0.28125 per coin contributed by any single player. Because 0.28125 < 1, contributing privately costs more than the personal return; the one-shot incentive is to free-ride.
- However, because the game is repeated and people can reward others, cooperative contributors can be supported by positive rewards. Rewards are costly to send (4 coins) and give small benefit to recipients (2 coins), so reward behavior is selective and sparse in practice.
- Expect initial moderate cooperation by many players (10–12 coins), with a few persistent low contributors (free-riders). Rewarding will be used sparingly to selectively encourage or thank cooperators rather than to punish defectors. Over time contributions may drift down in response to free-riding, then sometimes rebound if a few players use rewards and messages to sustain cooperation, especially later rounds when end-game effects occur.
- Chat will be sparse and generally used to propose cooperation or to call out/encourage behavior. Because rewards are observable to recipients, there will be occasional reciprocation or selective reinforcement.

game_explanation:
This public goods game with 16 players and 17 rounds creates a tension between private incentives (keep the 20 coins) and group gains (multiply contributions by 4.5 and redistribute). Early rounds typically see a modest-to-strong push for cooperation as players try to establish norms and signal trustworthiness. Because rewards are costly (4 coins to give 1 reward unit that gives 2 coins to the recipient), they will be used sparingly—mostly by players who care about sustaining cooperation or who want to signal that they will support cooperators. Free-riding tendencies will erode average contributions over time, and with a known finite horizon (17 rounds) there is usually a decline toward the end as backward induction and short-term incentives dominate. Still, a few consistent cooperators and occasional rewarding can sustain above-minimum contributions in many intermediate rounds, while the final few rounds tend to see lower contributions and fewer rewards.

Below I simulate a plausible, coherent play-by-play from round 1 through round 17. Each round begins with a short explanation of why players act as they do, followed by any chat, the contribution vector (in the exact avatar order provided), optional short chat, the list of reward actions (only non-zero), and then a short summary-stage chat.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with costly positive rewards. Each round players choose how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, then players may spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 2 coins to another player. Because contributions are multiplied more than 1 and then split, contributing yields a social return but not always a full private return; rewards give a narrow instrument to endorse cooperative contributors (they cannot directly punish, only reward). Typical dynamics: many start by contributing moderately to highly; some free-ride from the start. Sparse, targeted rewards can sustain cooperation by signaling appreciation and creating small private incentives for cooperators. Because the game has a known finite horizon (17 rounds) there is pressure toward endgame defection, but visible rewards and a culture of reciprocity can mitigate decline. Chats tend to be rare and pragmatic (announcements, thanks), and rewards are used sparingly to recognize contributors.

In this simulated run I predict a mostly cooperative group with many players contributing fairly high amounts (often 10–15), a few consistent low contributors (0–5), and a handful of players using sparse 1-unit rewards to encourage cooperators. Cooperation is broadly sustained through the game by those small positive reinforcements; chats appear occasionally (promises, thanks), and there is a modest downward drift only very late in the game.

Because contributions are multiplied more than 1 and then split, contributing yields a social return but not always a full private return; rewards give a narrow instrument to endorse cooperative contributors (they cannot directly punish, only reward). Sparse, targeted rewards can sustain cooperation by signaling appreciation and creating small private incentives for cooperators. Because the game has a known finite horizon (17 rounds) there is pressure toward endgame defection, but visible rewards and a culture of reciprocity can mitigate decline. Chats tend to be rare and pragmatic (announcements, thanks), and rewards are used sparingly to recognize contributors.

In this simulated run I predict a mostly cooperative group with many players contributing fairly high amounts (often 10–15), a few consistent low contributors (0–5), and a handful of players using sparse 1-unit rewards to encourage cooperators. Cooperation is broadly sustained through the game by those small positive reinforcements; chats appear occasionally (promises, thanks), and there is a modest downward drift only very late in the game.

This 17-round public goods game combines contribution decisions with an inexpensive positive-reward stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the shared pot: each coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally, so social return per coin is 4.5/16 = 0.28125 < 1. Individual incentive is to free-ride (keep the coin) unless motivated by reciprocity, norms, or reward.
- The reward stage allows players to pay 4 coins to give 2 coins to a peer (per reward unit). Rewards are costly and therefore will be used sparingly — mainly to recognize steady cooperators or to incentivize conditional cooperators, not to correct many defections.
- Because players observe who rewarded them, small reputational effects can support cooperation early on but weaken over time as the end approaches (endgame effect).
- Uncertainties: types of players (some are reliably cooperative, some are free-riders, most respond conditionally), how many will use rewards (they are scarce), and whether small early rewards are enough to sustain contributions. Expect moderate-to-high contributions early, gradual decline through the middle, and a clear drop in the final rounds. Rewarding will be concentrated on a few consistently cooperative players (e.g., OWL, RABBIT, COW), used rarely and with small units (mostly 1 unit).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 16 players against the classic tension: each coin kept privately yields 1 to the player, while each coin contributed to the public pot is multiplied by 4.5 and then split equally among all players (so each contributed coin returns 4.5/16 ≈ 0.28125 to its contributor). The individual incentive is to keep money while the group optimum is for everyone to contribute everything. Because the game lasts a known finite 17 rounds, we expect cooperation to be highest early on (players experiment and try to establish cooperative norms), stabilize in the middle rounds if reward is used to reinforce contributors, and then decline toward the end as players anticipate the final rounds (endgame effect). The available reward mechanism (each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver) is costly for the sender and inefficient socially, so rewards will be used sparingly — mainly by cooperators to recognize other cooperators, often reciprocally. Players who consistently free-ride are unlikely to be rewarded and may be socially isolated. Chat will be rare and mainly for coordination or signaling. Over 17 rounds I predict moderate initial contributions with selective rewarding, some sustained cooperation among a subset, and a gradual unraveling in the final few rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round, everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses how much (0–20) to put into a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins each) to give small benefits (2 coins each unit) to particular others. Players can also send short messages. Crucially, the game length is common knowledge, players see who rewarded them, and summaries show only each player's own round payoff details.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will try to signal cooperation (high contributions) while a few test for free-riding. Conditional cooperators respond to observed high contributions by increasing theirs; persistent free-riders earn fewer rewards.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce contributors and to reciprocate kindness. A few “prosocial” players (e.g., GORILLA, MOOSE, OWL) will reward high contributors; defectors (SNAKE, CROCODILE, SLOTH early on) will rarely reward.
- Mid-game (rounds ~4–10): cooperation tends to strengthen as reciprocal rewards and praise stabilize behavior; average contributions rise.
- Late game (rounds ~11–17): finite horizon effects start to appear. Some players reduce contributions as the end approaches; conditional cooperators drift down. Rewards remain but become less effective as the incentive to build future reputation weakens. By the final round, many will reduce contributions sharply (some to 0), though a few continue to contribute or send last-round rewards/praise.

I will simulate a plausible sequence consistent with these tendencies: sparse, targeted rewards and occasional short chat messages; cooperation rises midgame then erodes toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, an endowment of 20 each round, a public multiplier (4.5) that makes full contribution socially optimal but individually costly, and a costly positive reward instrument (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives 2 coins to the recipient). Players cannot see others' contributions before choosing, but they can reward after seeing results and identify who rewarded them. The game lasts 17 rounds, so early and mid rounds generally favor some cooperation sustained by reciprocity and selective rewarding; late rounds (especially the final few) usually see contributions fall as the horizon approaches and incentives to invest in others (via rewards) decline.

Expected dynamics:
- Early rounds: testing and signaling. Some players will contribute moderately-high to signal cooperativeness; others will free ride or contribute modestly. A few selective rewards will be used to reinforce visible cooperators.
- Middle rounds (rounds ~4–10): If rewards are targeted to contributors, cooperation often increases as reciprocity emerges. Reward use remains sparse because each reward is expensive for the giver.
- Late rounds (rounds ~11–16): Cooperation slowly erodes as the end approaches; rewards continue but less frequently and more selectively.
- Final round (17): Most players reduce contributions; reward use typically drops sharply since future reciprocity is no longer possible. Occasional small tokens may be given as thanks or norm signaling.

Now the full round-by-round transcript follows. I use the exact avatar order for contributions and list only non-zero reward actions. Chat is kept sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a finite 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone receives 20 coins, chooses a contribution (0–20) to a shared pot that is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. Individually, contributing yields less return than keeping coins (MPCR = 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281), so purely selfish incentives push toward zero contribution. However, social motives (conditional cooperation), the visibility of contributions after decisions, and the ability to punish (costly but severe) or reward (costly and mild benefit to target) can sustain cooperation early on.

Typical dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: uncertainty and mixture of strategies. Some players will test cooperation (mid-to-high contributions) while some free-ride (low or zero). A few players respond to very low contributors with occasional punishments; a few deliver small rewards to high contributors. This can create a norm toward moderate cooperation.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward signals are consistent, contributions can stabilize at moderate-to-high levels among many players. Punishment remains sparse because it's costly; single-unit punishments are used to signal disapproval.
- Late rounds: with a known finite horizon, backward induction pressures produce a decline in cooperation. Even with prior norms, contributions tend to fall in the final rounds. Punishment and reward activity also declines; some isolated punitive acts or final spiteful moves can appear but are typically rare.

I will simulate a plausible path: contributions start mixed, rise into a cooperative phase supported by occasional sanctions and rewards, then gradually decay toward the end game. Chat is sparse and purposeful — calls for cooperation, a few justifications for punishment, and short reactions when players are sanctioned.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 16 players against the classic tension: contributing to the group fund increases total payoffs (each coin contributed becomes 4.5 coins to the pot, shared equally), but any single player keeps more by free-riding (each coin returned to the contributor only ~0.281 of a coin). Expect initial heterogeneity: some players try cooperation, others test defections. Because punishment is available (costly to the punisher but heavy on the punished) and rewards are available (also costly but smaller effect), we'll likely see a pattern where early low contributors get occasional punishments and late contributors get occasional rewards. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher 4 coins per unit and also because identification after rounds makes retaliation and reputation possible. Over repeated rounds, punishments and rewards can stabilize midgame cooperation; however, due to the finite 17-round horizon, contributions tend to decline toward the end (rational backward induction and waning enforcement). Chat will be sparse and purposeful: brief coordination attempts early, short protests when someone is punished, thanks when rewarded.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. By itself the private return to each contributed coin is less than 1, so the individual incentive is to free-ride, but group payoffs are higher when many contribute. The punishment option (cost 4 per unit to sender, -15 per unit to target) is a strong deterrent to free-riding for targets but is costly to the punisher; rewards (cost 4 per unit to sender, +2 per unit to target) are relatively weak and costly, so they are used rarely. Real groups often start with modest cooperation, react to clear free-riding with a small number of punishments, and then either stabilize cooperation or trend down toward the end as the finite horizon becomes salient. Chat is typically sparse and focused on coordination or sanctioning. I predict mostly moderate-to-high contributions across most of the game, a few isolated free-riding incidents that prompt limited punishment, occasional apologies, and some unraveling in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG </PLAYERS>

This Public Goods Game (PGG) is a repeated 17-round setting where each round players choose how much of a 20-coin endowment to contribute to a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally among all 16 players, so individual contributions produce a return to the group but a smaller private return than the cost of contributing. After contributions are revealed each round, players may punish (costly for the sender, very costly for the recipient) or reward (also costly for the sender, modest benefit to the recipient) others. Messages can be sent at any time.

Likely overall dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players will "test" cooperation levels. Expect moderate contributions (around half to three-quarters of endowment) as players signal willingness to cooperate.
- Mid game: players who value group outcomes or who are willing to pay to enforce norms may occasionally punish clear free-riders. Because punishment is expensive to the punisher (4 coins per unit) but very damaging to the target (15 coins per unit), punishments will be used sparingly and typically only against persistent or stark free-riding. Rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior (also used sparingly because they cost 4 per unit).
- Convergence: If a few players reliably punish free-riding, average contributions can stay moderately high (10–14). If punishers are absent or ineffective, contributions drift downward as selfish incentives dominate.
- Endgame (last few rounds): standard backward-looking incentives predict declining contributions as the possibility of future retaliation or reciprocity disappears. Punishment and rewards taper off near the very end.

Uncertainties:
- Who will act as enforcer(s)? A few players may shoulder the cost of punishing to sustain cooperation. If not, cooperation will decay.
- How tolerant are players of costs from being punished? A single 1-unit punishment is very costly to the target (−15), so even a single punishment can change behavior dramatically.
- Chat can coordinate norms, but messages are sparse in most real experiments; expect a few short normative messages and occasional complaints after punishment.

The simulated transcript below follows these patterns: modest initial cooperation, sparse but decisive punishment towards low contributors, occasional small rewards for steady cooperators, and a gradual decline toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds, private endowments of 20 each round, a group-sized multiplier (4.5) and a post-decision punishment/reward stage. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Contributing benefits the group: each coin contributed returns 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins to each player, so a coin gives 4.5 total to the pot but only 0.28125 back to each contributor directly. Private incentive is to contribute less than others; group incentive is to contribute more.
- Punishment is very powerful (each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and subtracts 15 from the target). Rewards are inexpensive for the recipient (each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 2 to the recipient). Because punishment causes big immediate harm, it is used sparingly in real games — typically only targeted at obvious free-riders.
- Early rounds often see moderate, cautious contributions (10–15) and sparse messages. Small experiments (a few people contributing 0 or 20) tend to occur; peers may punish persistent free-riders, and occasionally reward high contributors.
- With punishment available, we expect cooperation to stabilize at a moderate level after a few rounds: defectors get targeted punishment; some players reward unusually generous contributors. Late in the game (final rounds) some players may defect more (backward induction), so contributions may drop in the last round.
- Chat tends to be sparse and functional — a short justification, a call for cooperation, or a protest if punished.

I will simulate plausible play from round 1 through round 17, showing round-level explanations, the contributions (in the exact avatar order given), any chat, punishment/reward tuples, and each player's summary (their own net payoff, amounts they spent on punishing/rewarding, amount deducted from punishments they received, and amount received from rewards).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds. Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can spend coins to punish or reward others. Punishment is costly for the punisher and harsh for the target; rewards are costly for the sender but modestly benefit targets. Players also can send chat messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players test the waters — a mix of cooperation and free-riding. A few committed cooperators (and at least one enforcer) will try to steer behavior with messages, occasional rewards, and rare punishments targeted at obvious defectors.
- Middle rounds: if punish/reward enforcement works, cooperation tends to rise as defectors are nudged to contribute more and cooperators are rewarded. Rewards are more common than punishments (since punishments are costly and can provoke retaliation).
- Late rounds: the well-known endgame effect appears. As the horizon nears, incentives to maintain cooperation weaken and contributions drift downward. Punishment becomes rarer late in the game because it is costly and has limited future benefit.
- Chat is sparse and typically used to announce intentions, encourage cooperation, or express approval/disapproval after a round.

I will simulate plausible contributions, sparse chat, and a few targeted punishments and rewards that follow these patterns across rounds 1–17.

game_explanation:
This public goods game with 16 players and 17 rounds sets up a tension between individual short-term incentives (keep your 20 coins) and group benefits (contribute to the pot, which is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally). Because contributions are private before decisions, players tend to match perceived norms: if many contribute fairly high amounts early, conditional cooperators will follow; if a few free-ride, others may reduce their contributions next round.

Two institutional features will shape behavior strongly here:
- Punishment is very powerful: each punishment unit costs 4 coins for the punisher but subtracts 15 coins from the punished player. That creates a strong deterrent against extreme free-riding, and players who value reciprocity or reputation will sometimes pay the small cost to impose a heavy penalty.
- Rewards are cheap to deliver relative to their effect: each reward unit costs 4 to the giver and adds 2 to the recipient. Rewards are less efficient at shaping payoffs than punishment is at deterring bad behavior, but they are useful as positive signals to encourage cooperators.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: modest-to-high contributions as players test each other; sparse chat with a few normative statements ("please contribute", "I'll punish free-riders").
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly. Punishment tends to be targeted at obvious free-riders (very low contributions relative to the group) and usually in small units (1 unit). Rewards get used occasionally to reinforce high cooperators.
- Middle rounds: contributions can stabilize if punishment/reward sustain cooperation, or start drifting down if some players exploit the public good without immediate consequences.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to decline (backward induction and diminishing future). Punishment becomes rarer because the deterrent effect is weaker when there are few remaining rounds to influence behavior; occasional final-round spite or gratitude messages/rewards may appear.

I will simulate a plausible sequence across 17 rounds following these tendencies: initial moderate-high cooperation, sparse use of punishment targeted at low contributors, occasional rewards, short messages (promises, threats, apologies), and gradual decline in contributions toward the end with fewer punitive acts.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round every player privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so any coin contributed returns only about 0.28 coins to its contributor but yields 4.5 coins total to the group. That creates a tension: full cooperation (high contributions) maximizes group payoff, but individually there's an incentive to free-ride.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some players will “test” cooperation with moderate contributions (5–12), while a few free-ride (0–2). Sparse chat may set intentions.
- Enforcement and reciprocity: because players can punish (costly to giver but very costly to target) or reward (costly to giver but small benefit to receiver), a few players may use punishment sparingly to discipline clear free-riders. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperators. These tools can stabilize higher contributions mid-game.
- Mid-game (rounds ~3–11): if punishment/reward is used effectively, many players will increase contributions (10–15). Punishment remains rare but targeted.
- End-game (last few rounds): forward-looking incentives collapse; contributions usually fall as the horizon approaches. Expect a decline in the final 3–4 rounds.
- Chat will be sparse and functional: short commitments, calls-out after seeing contributions, occasional apologies or promises.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and optional punishment and reward after contributions. Each coin a player contributes is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so each coin contributed increases everyone's payoff but yields only 0.28125 coins back to the contributor herself — beneficial for the group but costly privately. Punishment is powerful (each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and removes 15 coins from the target) while reward is relatively weak (cost 4 to the sender, adds 2 to the recipient). Because players can identify who punished or rewarded them, targeted social sanctions and praise can be used to enforce norms.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: contributions often start moderately as players test each other. A few low contributions typically trigger selective punishment or targeted rewards to encourage cooperation.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward are used effectively, cooperation can stabilize at a moderate level (many contributing well above 0). Rewards reinforce high contributors; punishments deter freeloaders.
- Late rounds (endgame): with a known finite horizon, contributions tend to decline as the incentive to punish future behavior disappears. Punishment becomes rarer (less payoff to invest in costly sanctions when there's little future), and many players reduce contributions toward the end.
- Chat is typically sparse and mostly used for coordination, calls for fairness, or to complain after being punished.

With those tendencies in mind, the transcript below simulates plausible contributions, a few targeted punishments and occasional rewards, and a gradual decline in cooperation as the end approaches.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players privately decide how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied and split equally. Because the private return on each contributed coin (4.5/16 ≈ 0.28) is less than 1, purely selfish incentives favor contributing 0. But subjects commonly start by contributing moderately (hoping others will reciprocate), and contributions often drift downward unless there are sanctions or positive reinforcement. The punishment (costly to punishers, sharply costly to targets) and reward (costly to givers, small benefit to receivers) stages allow players to enforce norms: rare but targeted punishments can deter free-riding; occasional rewards can sustain cooperation. Communication is possible but typically sparse and used for brief coordination or calling out behavior. Over 17 rounds we expect an initial wave of cooperation, some targeted punishments for clear free-riders that raise contributions for a while, occasional rewards to reinforce leaders, and a gradual erosion of cooperation toward the very last rounds (when future retaliation no longer applies).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds. Each round each player privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to the group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally, so collective payoffs are maximized by everyone contributing everything; individual incentives push toward free-riding (contribute 0) because you keep private coins while still getting a share of others' contributions. The punishment/reward stage lets players impose costly sanctions or give costly rewards after seeing contributions. Punishment is costly to the punisher but extremely costly to the punished (source pays 4 per unit, target loses 15 per unit), so even single punishment units are strong signals. Rewards are also costly to the source but give modest benefit to the target (source pays 4 per unit, target gains 2 per unit). Because the game has a known finite horizon (17 rounds), expect a tension: early- and mid-game cooperation can be fostered by conditional reciprocity and occasional punishments of defectors; in later rounds, backward induction typically reduces cooperation as players anticipate that sanctions become less credible near the end. Messages will be sparse but used to praise cooperation or call out low contributors. Overall I predict:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions, some free-riding, a few targeted punishments and rewards to shape behavior.
- Middle rounds: higher average contributions as sanctions and rewards enforce norms.
- Late rounds: gradual decline in contributions as end-game effects appear; punishments drop off because they’re costly and less effective at the very end.

reflection:

Because the multiplier (1.8) creates a positive group return but individual incentives favor keeping coins, expect some cooperation early on, occasional targeted rewards to reinforce contributors, and gradual unraveling toward the end as the horizon approaches. Rewards will be sparse and used mostly to acknowledge cooperative behavior or to nudge a laggard back toward contribution; they cannot directly punish (no negative rewards), so non-cooperators are usually "punished" by omission of rewards rather than by negative transfers. Chat is likely to be limited — occasional calls to cooperate, short acknowledgements, or short complaints after surprising defections.

I predict:
- Early rounds: high contributions by most players and a few small, targeted rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: occasional defections or experimentation (one or two players reduce contributions), followed by selective rewarding of cooperators.
- Late rounds: increased defection as the end approaches, with few or no rewards in the final rounds; round 19 will likely see everyone withdraw everything (no contribution).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds, so players face a tension between short-term private gain (withdrawing coins) and long-term group benefit (contributing to the shared pot). Early rounds typically see some cooperation as players try to build norms and test others. Because contributions are revealed each round, good contributors are visible and can be rewarded; that creates an incentive to contribute if other players use rewards selectively to support cooperators.

However, rewards are costly (2 coins per reward unit to the giver, 2 coins gained by the recipient), so they will be used sparingly. Expect a few players to emerge as consistent cooperators (and to be rewarded occasionally), while at least one player may free-ride or contribute little. Over time, contributions will tend to drift downward as the endgame approaches and incentives to sustain cooperation weaken. Chats will be sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, thanks, or calls-out), and rewards will be concentrated on those who contribute more, used intermittently to sustain cooperation in the middle rounds and largely disappear near the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates tension between private incentive (withdraw coins for yourself) and group benefit (keep coins in the pot so the multiplied public good is shared). Key forces likely to shape play here:

- Free-riding temptation: withdrawing a lot raises your private payoff while others bear the cost of maintaining the pot; rational short-term incentives push toward lower contributions.
- Conditional cooperation: many people respond to others' contributions — if they expect others to give, they contribute more; if they see free-riding, they reduce contributions.
- Rewarding as enforcement: the reward stage lets players transfer value to others at a personal cost; players typically use rewards sparingly to encourage cooperators (and indirectly discourage free-riders by not rewarding them). Because rewards are anonymous to recipients, direct reciprocal signaling is weakened but senders can still selectively support cooperators.
- Uncertainty & limited reputation: players see only their own round payoff and how much they spent/received in rewards, not who did what. This reduces the clarity of who is being reinforced or punished, making sustained, targeted enforcement harder.
- End-game decline: with a known finite horizon (19 rounds), cooperation often decays near the end as future enforcement becomes irrelevant — expect contributions and rewards to drop in late rounds.

Overall, I expect some initial cooperation with a few high contributors (Gorilla, Chicken), a cluster of medium cooperators (Duck, Parrot occasionally), and a persistent low contributor (Sloth). Several players will use small, occasional rewards to sustain cooperation early on; rewards will become scarcer as the game progresses and cooperation drifts downward, with a noticeable end-game decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players for 19 rounds. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to withdraw (private) so contribution = 20 - withdrawn. The group return is 1.8× total contributions, split equally (equivalently each player gets 0.3× the round's total contributions). Rewards are costly (2 coins per reward unit sent) and give 2 coins per unit to the target. Players see only their own round payoff and how much they spent/received on rewards; they do not see who rewarded them.

Likely dynamics: early rounds typically show relatively high cooperation as players attempt to build a norm. Occasional unilateral low contributions (testing or opportunism) prompt sparse chat and small rewards aimed at encouraging return to cooperation. Because rewards are costly, they are used sparingly — mostly to reinforce cooperators, occasionally to "reconcile" a previously low contributor. Midgame tends to see some drift down in contributions as fear of being exploited grows. Near the end (last few rounds), standard game-theoretic incentives push toward lower contributions (endgame unraveling), but some players still send a few last rewards or messages to sustain norms or signal reciprocity. Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests, calls out, thanks). Reward actions are likewise small units (1 or 2), targeted and infrequent.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds, anonymity for contributions and reward sources, and an explicit reward stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing more to the public pot raises the group payout (multiplied by 1.8 and split equally), so contributing is socially beneficial.
- Withdrawing is privately beneficial in the short run (you keep more immediately), so free-riding is tempting.
- Rewards let players transfer value to encourage cooperators, but rewards cost the giver (2 coins per reward unit) and are anonymous to recipients (they see amounts received but not who paid).
- Because the end is known (19 rounds), we expect some endgame decline in contributions. Early rounds often feature moderate-to-high contributions and some promises/messages. Middle rounds may see conditional cooperators trying to sustain cooperation via occasional rewards to high contributors. Late rounds typically show falling contributions as strategic motives to free-ride dominate.

Uncertainties:
- Because contributions and reward sources are anonymous, it's hard to identify and target consistent free-riders, so rewards will be used sparingly and often for visible signals (e.g., reward after receiving a good payoff).
- Individual tendencies (some players may be staunch cooperators, others opportunistic) will shape the group average. Expect a modest level of mutual reinforcement early, more drift toward defection late, and sparse but targeted reward use across mid-rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players against a repeated temptation: keep coins privately or contribute to a shared pot that benefits everyone. With 19 rounds known in advance, early rounds typically see moderate cooperation (players try to build a norm), rewarded by a small number of costly transfers to reinforce contributors. Because contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, the social return on a coin contributed (1.8/6 = 0.3 to each player) is lower than the private return (1 to the withdrawer), so individual incentives favor withholding. Rewards provide a way to indirectly support cooperators, but they are costly to give (2 coins out per unit) so they are used sparingly.

Uncertainties and dynamics to watch:
- Conditional cooperators will give more when they expect others to contribute; a few early free-riders can erode cooperation.
- Rewards are private transfers (source pays cost) and not fully visible in summaries to others, so they signal willingness to support cooperators but are limited in disciplinary power.
- With a known finite horizon (19 rounds), contributions typically drift down toward the end as the incentive to build future cooperation weakens (endgame effect).
- Occasional chats can encourage cooperation; persistent small rewards may sustain moderate contributions midgame, but overall trend tends to be gradual decline.

I therefore predict a start with modest-to-high contributions and sparse rewarding of high contributors, attempts to sustain cooperation midgame through a few targeted rewards and messages, and a steady falloff toward low contributions in the final rounds.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits private incentives (withdrawing coins) against a shared benefit: each coin left in the common pot returns only 0.3 to each individual, so contributing is costly for the individual but beneficial for the group as a whole. Because rewards are costly to give (2 coins taken from the sender to give 2 coins to a recipient), players will typically reward sparingly — usually to signal appreciation for high contributions or to try to sustain cooperation. Early rounds often see some cooperation driven by optimism and reciprocity; targeted rewards can stabilize cooperation for a while. As the finite horizon approaches, classical endgame effects reduce incentives to cooperate and to reward, so contributions and rewards usually decay toward free-riding. Messages are sparse and used to praise, shame, or coordinate. There is uncertainty about others' contributions each round (players only observe their own payoff and reward exchanges involving themselves), so players infer others' behavior indirectly from rewards and chats; that makes conditional cooperation imperfect and noisy.

game_explanation:
This is a finite-horizon public goods game with 6 players for 19 rounds. Each round players decide how many of 20 coins to withdraw (private use) so that the remainder is contributed. Total contributions get multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.3 to each player (1.8/6). Because the private withdrawal is fully retained by the choosing player whereas contributions benefit everyone only partially, there is a temptation to withdraw more (free-ride). However, players can reward others after seeing contributions; rewards cost 2 coins to the sender per reward unit and give 2 coins to the recipient. Rewards are anonymous to recipients in their summaries, but players can use them to signal appreciation and sustain cooperation.

Likely dynamics: early rounds tend to show moderate cooperation as players try to establish norms; some players will promise or use small rewards to sustain high contributors. Over time, because the horizon is known, contributions will slowly decline as end-game effects and opportunistic withholding appear. Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce contributors; they become rarer late in the game as contributions fall and the incentive to invest in rewards weakens. Messages are infrequent and strategic (praise, mild threats to stop rewarding, or conditional commitments).

game_explanation:
This is a finite-horizon public goods game with 19 rounds, 6 players, and a reward stage. Each player effectively decides how much of their 20 coins to keep privately (by withdrawing) and how much to contribute (20 minus withdrawal). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.8, and split equally. Because returns from the public pot are shared, individual incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding) while group payoffs favor contributing. The reward stage lets players transfer positive “reward units” (each costing 2 coins to the giver and giving 2 coins to the recipient) to reinforce cooperative behavior, but such transfers are individually costly.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: Players typically start fairly cooperative or at least test cooperation. Some players (conditional cooperators) match others' generosity; a few may test free-riding. Players who care about sustaining cooperation (or who want to signal norms) will use small rewards to encourage high contributors.
- Mid game: The picture often diverges. If some players persistently free-ride (low contributions), cooperators will reduce their contributions in response unless rewards successfully incentivize contributions. Rewards are used sparingly because they are costly, usually targeted at consistently high contributors.
- End game (last few rounds): Because the game has a known finite horizon, cooperation often unravels toward the end — especially when players expect others to stop cooperating. Rewarding typically declines as players conserve coins.

Uncertainties:
- How many players are strong conditional cooperators vs. persistent free-riders.
- Whether reward senders can offset decline in contributions enough to sustain cooperation midgame.
- Individual differences in how costly rewarding is perceived; repeated anonymity of reward sources in summaries slightly blurs reciprocity dynamics.

Given these tendencies, expect some initial cooperation with sparse targeted rewards, then gradual decline in contributions and rewards toward the final rounds, with occasional last-minute attempts to prop up cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players, a marginal per-coin group return of 1.8 and a private return of 1.0. Individually, contributing is costly (you get 0.3 back per coin you contribute), so one-shot incentives push toward free-riding. But because the game repeats (19 rounds) and players can reward others (at private cost), reputational considerations and reciprocity can sustain cooperation early on.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: partial cooperation as players try to establish a cooperative norm, with a few small positive rewards to signal approval.
- Middle rounds: noises—occasional defections (especially by a habitual low contributor like DOG) lower the perceived norm; rewards become more targeted and sparser.
- Late rounds (end-game): cooperation tends to unravel as the horizon approaches; contributions fall, rewards largely stop. A few players may try a last-minute cooperative push, but it's usually small and short-lived.

Uncertainties:
- Whether a few generous players will persist in rewarding high contributors enough to sustain cooperation is uncertain.
- One or two persistent defectors can accelerate decline because they lower average return from the public pot and attract little reward in response.
- Chat messages can coordinate cooperation early but are less effective late in the game.

Below is a round-by-round transcript predicting likely contributions, sparse chat, and targeted small rewards that reflect evolving norms.

game_explanation:
In this public goods game (PGG) with 19 rounds, players each decide how much of the 20-coin pot to withdraw for private use; the remainder is contributed to the public pot, multiplied by 1.8, and split equally. After seeing their own round payoff and reward transfers (but not who did what), players may pay to reward others (2 coins cost per 2-coin benefit). Because play is repeated and the horizon is known, we expect the following broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' choices leads to moderate contributions; conditional cooperators (those who reward or respond to rewards) try to encourage cooperation with small, targeted rewards and positive chat. Free riders may withdraw more, but the public multiplication still provides a benefit to contributors.
- Middle rounds: reward use tends to be sparse but strategic—players reward those they believe are cooperating (often signaled via chat or consistent payoff patterns). This can sustain higher contributions for several rounds.
- Late rounds: as the end approaches, classical end-game effects reduce cooperation. Contributions decline toward the final rounds because there are fewer future consequences; reward activity also diminishes. Chat messages become sparse and more resigned.
- Uncertainty and anonymity about who rewarded whom makes social signaling noisier: players may claim cooperative intent, but claims are unverifiable. As a result, rewards are used sparingly and typically only to support clear patterns of contribution.

The transcript below shows a plausible unfolding: some players start relatively cooperative (GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT), others are more stingy (SLOTH, DOG sometimes). Rewards are modest and aimed at reinforcing cooperative behavior, especially in early-to-mid rounds; by the final rounds contributions largely collapse.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round each player effectively decides how much of 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribute) versus withdraw privately; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Because each coin contributed returns only 0.3 to the contributor (1.8/6), the individual incentive is to withdraw (contribute 0) even though the group would do best if everyone contributed most or all. That creates a classic tension: conditional cooperators may start contributing out of goodwill or expectation of reciprocity, while free-riders can exploit them.

The reward stage allows targeted rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Rewards themselves are payoff-neutral at the group level (coins merely move between players), but they can sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors. Because rewards are costly to the sender, they tend to be used sparingly and strategically. Also, because the game has a known finite horizon (19 rounds), "endgame" effects usually appear: cooperation and reward tend to decline toward the later rounds as incentives to invest in future reciprocity vanish.

Given typical PGG behavior, I predict:
- Moderate-to-high contributions early (some players contribute a lot, others less).
- A few players act as "rewarders" early — they reward visible cooperators or publicly announced contributors — which helps sustain contributions for several rounds.
- Conditional cooperators (who reward or reduce contributions) will gradually lower contributions when they perceive others aren't matching their effort; free-riders stay low.
- As the game approaches the final rounds, both contributions and rewards decay toward zero (endgame collapse).
- Chat messages will be sparse and purposeful: initial greetings, occasional promises or calls for cooperation, and occasional expressions of thanks or disappointment.
