game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players over 19 rounds. Each round players choose how much of their 20 coins to keep (withdraw) and how much to leave as contribution to the public pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoffs are revealed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target per unit) others. Players see only their own summary each round and can send chat messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often feature moderate cooperation as players test each other. High contributors may be rewarded; low contributors may be punished once identified.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly (they're costly and noisy — punishments cost the sender and also remove even more from the punished).
- Because players cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, retaliation is imprecise; punishment is primarily a social enforcement tool rather than exact tit-for-tat.
- Mid-game cooperation can rise if punishments/rewards enforce norms, but some free-riding persists.
- As the finite horizon approaches, the "endgame" logic pushes contributions down (players withdraw more in later rounds), so we expect a gradual decline toward lower contributions and fewer social transfers. In the final round, most players are likely to withdraw everything.

Below is a round-by-round transcript consistent with these incentives, with sparse, purposeful chat and limited punishment/reward actions.

- Mid-game cooperation can rise if punishments/rewards enforce norms, but some free-riding persists.
- As the finite horizon approaches, the "endgame" logic pushes contributions down (players withdraw more in later rounds), so we expect a gradual decline toward lower contributions and fewer social transfers. In the final round, most players are likely to withdraw everything.

Below is a round-by-round transcript consistent with these incentives, with sparse, purposeful chat and limited punishment/reward actions.

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (the contribution) and keep the rest privately; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, then players can punish or reward others at a cost. With a finite horizon (19 rounds), we expect early rounds to show moderate-to-high cooperation maintained by occasional punishments and selective rewards. Punishment will be used sparingly to discourage clear free-riders; small positive rewards will occasionally reinforce high contributors. As the end approaches (final few rounds), incentive to cooperate falls and contributions will drift downward (the classic end-game decline), with fewer punishments and rewards because retaliation or reputation concerns weaken. Chats will be sparse — a few calls to cooperate, apologies, thanks, and short comments — and punishment/reward actions will be limited and targeted rather than widespread.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 known rounds, six players, and an intermediate redistribution (total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally). Each round players choose a contribution (0–20) simultaneously; lower contribution increases private payoff that round but reduces the group return. After seeing contributions, players can punish (costly to sender, hurts target) or reward (costly to sender, helps target). Punishment is 2 coins to sender per unit and removes 3 coins from the target per unit; reward costs 2 coins to sender and gives 2 coins to the target per unit. Players cannot identify who targeted them later.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (most contribute a fair amount). People test trust; a few small rewards may be used to signal appreciation.
- Middle rounds: a free rider or two appears, provoking sparing punishments from some cooperators to enforce norms. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperators.
- Late rounds (toward the known end): a predictable decline in contributions (endgame effect). Punishment and reward become rarer because enforcement is costly and less effective when the game will end soon.
- Messages (chat) will be sparse and mostly normative: calls for fairness, short explanations of punishments or promises to encourage cooperation.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript (contributions listed in the exact avatar order). For each round I give a short explanation then the actions, punishment/reward tuples, and the summary each player would see (their net payoff, coins spent on punish/reward, coins deducted by punishment, coins received from reward). Numbers are rounded to one decimal where needed.

game_explanation:
This public goods game sets up a tension every round between private use (withdrawing coins) and contributing to a shared pot that is multiplied and shared. Each player gets 0.3 times the group total contribution added to whatever they keep privately, so every coin contributed gives the group 0.3 to each member (0.3*6 = 1.8 total return). Private withdrawal gives an immediate +1 to the chooser, but it lowers the shared pot and so everyone’s return. Because returns are shared, there's an incentive to free-ride (withhold contribution) and still collect the group benefit.

Two features change typical dynamics: (1) costly punishment (cost 2 to punisher, -3 to target) and costly reward (cost 2 to giver, +2 to recipient). These allow informal enforcement: cooperators can punish low contributors to deter free-riding, or reward cooperators to encourage it. (2) The game lasts 19 rounds with anonymous punish/reward (players see effects on themselves but not who acted). That encourages conditional strategies (punish repeated free-riders, reward consistent cooperators), but anonymity limits direct retaliation.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: players experiment; many start moderately cooperative (around half the endowment) and send sparse messages encouraging cooperation.
- When clear free-riding appears (one player repeatedly contributing little), a small coalition of cooperators will occasionally punish that player to deter freeloading; punishment is costly, so it will be applied sparingly.
- Mutual small rewards appear intermittently to reinforce leadership or reciprocity.
- Over the middle rounds, punishment and occasional rewards help sustain modest cooperation though not perfect; defecting players are often targeted but not eliminated.
- Toward the final rounds, a mild end-game decline in contributions is typical; some players reduce contributions slightly as the incentive to invest in long-term reputation falls.
- Chat is sparse and strategic: initial appeals to cooperate, occasional calls-out or promises to punish, and short encouragements.

Now the simulated transcript round-by-round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Each round players choose how much of the 20-coin pot to keep for themselves (withdraw) and how much to leave in the shared pot as a contribution. The group return to each player is 1.8 times the sum of contributions split equally across all six players — equivalently each player's share equals 0.3 times the total contributions. Withdrawing gives an immediate private gain but reduces the group return; contributions increase the common pie but benefit everyone.

Likely dynamics: early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as players test each other. If someone free-rides (withdraws a lot), a few players may punish to signal that free-riding is costly; punishments are usually sparse because each punishment is costly to the punisher. Rewards are also used sparingly to encourage contributors. Over time, conditional cooperators adjust their contributions in response to punishment and rewards — punished free-riders increase contributions, rewarded cooperators reinforce their behavior. Toward the final rounds (end-game), cooperation often declines because future retaliation or reciprocity is limited, so many players reduce contributions. Chats are expected to be infrequent and mostly short requests, reprimands, or thanks.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players and 19 rounds. Each round each player chooses how much to withdraw (private payoff) leaving the remainder as their public contribution; the total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Punishment (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target) and reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target) follow. With 1.8/6 = 0.30 marginal return per coin to the group, purely selfish play favors withdrawing everything (contribute 0). But when punishment and reward are available, players can try to sustain cooperation: modest contributions can be enforced by occasional punishments and small rewards. Typical dynamics in such games: some initial experimentation and mixed contributions, a drift toward higher cooperation if punishments/rewards target low contributors, and then a decay near the final rounds (end‑game) as incentives to cooperate disappear. Chat is typically sparse and used to signal intentions or register displeasure. Punishment tends to be used sparingly because it's costly to the sender; rewards are used sometimes to reinforce cooperators.

Below I simulate a plausible full transcript from round 1 through round 19 with sparse chat, modest use of punishment/reward to sustain cooperation in the middle rounds, and gradual unraveling near the end. Contribution lists always follow the avatar order given (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Punishment/Reward tuples list only non-zero actions; positive units are rewards, negative units are punishments.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (withdraw coins for oneself) and collective benefit (leave coins in the shared pot). Each coin left in the pot yields 1.8 coins total, but the return is split across six players, so the private return per contributed coin is only 1.8/6 = 0.3 — much less than keeping the coin privately (1.0). That makes full free-riding individually attractive, but if everyone defects the group loses the multiplication benefit.

Because rounds are repeated (19 rounds known in advance) and players can punish or reward others after seeing contributions, we expect:
- Early rounds often show relatively high contributions as players attempt to signal cooperation.
- Some players will test the group by lowering contributions; those free-riders typically provoke a small amount of punishment (costly to senders), which can temporarily increase cooperation.
- Punishments are costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and remove more from the target (3 per unit), so they are used sparingly; rewards are also costly (2 per unit) but add 2 to the target.
- Over the long run, absent strong and consistent punishment, cooperation usually drifts downward (the “decline in contributions” typical in PGGs). Toward the final rounds you'll often see an "end-game" drop as incentives to sustain cooperation vanish.
- Chats are sparse and often used to call out defectors, thank cooperators, or signal intent.

I will simulate a plausible path: a cooperative start with a few episodes of punishment against clear free-riders, intermittent small rewards, and a gradual decline as the game approaches round 19, ending in low contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 known rounds, six players, multiplication factor 1.8 and symmetric sharing. Each round players choose privately how much of the 20 coins to withdraw (i.e., keep); the remainder is the contribution. Because the multiplication factor yields 0.3 coins to each player per contributed coin (1.8/6 = 0.3), an individual gains 0.3 from each coin they (or anyone) contributes to the pot but keeps 1 coin if they withdraw it. That creates an individual incentive to free-ride (withdraw coins) while the group is better off with high contributions. Uncertainties: players don't observe others' choices before deciding and can't link punishments/rewards to identities, so social enforcement is noisy.

Likely dynamics: early rounds typically show modest to fairly high contributions as players test each other's cooperativeness. Sparse rewards are used to reinforce consistently high contributors and a few (not too many) punishments may be used by norm-enforcers to target clear free-riders. If enforcement is effective, contributions can rise into the middle rounds. However, because the horizon is finite and known, contributions usually start to decline as the end approaches (the “end-game”), with many players withdrawing most coins in the final rounds. Chat is typically sparse and used to encourage cooperation or warn about punishment; punishments and rewards remain infrequent but visible enough to affect behavior early to mid-game.

Players see only their own round summary (net payoff, what they spent on punish/reward, how much was deducted from them, how much reward they received), not who did what.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (above zero contributions), with occasional punishments to deter persistent low contributors and occasional small rewards to reinforce cooperators. Messages are sparse and used for coordination or complaints.
- Midgame: punishments are used sparingly to sustain cooperation; some players (DUCK, perhaps DOG) exploit occasionally and get sanctioned, which can bring them back toward higher contributions.
- Late rounds: because the horizon is finite, contributions gradually fall. Punishment becomes less effective (costly for the punisher and with diminishing future repercussions), so cooperation tends to collapse toward the final rounds. By the last round most players withdraw everything.
- Overall: punish/reward actions are infrequent and targeted. Chats are sparse and usually reactive.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 14 known rounds. Each round players choose whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, which here simplifies: if k players contribute, every player receives 4k coins from the pot. Because a contributor pays 20 and a non-contributor keeps 20, defecting yields a direct private advantage of 20 coins each round (before rewards). Rewarding is costly to the sender (3 coins per reward unit) and beneficial to the receiver (+4 coins per unit) and happens after redistribution, so it can be used to reward cooperators or compensate for exploitation.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: some players try cooperation to build the public good. A few players (often those who contribute) will spend small amounts to reward cooperators to encourage them. Rewards are sparse because they are costly.
- Middle rounds: as players see how many others actually contributed in earlier rounds, cooperation may erode because defectors capture a private benefit each round. Occasional rewarders try to sustain cooperation, leading to intermittent cooperation revivals.
- Late rounds and endgame: with only a few rounds left, forward-looking players stop investing in sustaining cooperation; contributions and rewards drop. The last round often collapses to universal defection since there's no future to incentivize cooperation.

Uncertainties:
- Which players will consistently cooperate or defect is unknown, so conditional strategies (rewarding cooperators, punishing defectors) may be attempted but with limited and costly rewards.
- Small, sporadic revivals of cooperation are possible if a few actors try to re-establish cooperation with small rewards, but these are fragile.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, finite-horizon public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player chooses either to keep 20 coins privately or contribute the full 20 to a group pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. After redistribution, players may spend costly reward units (cost 3 to giver, gives 4 to receiver) to encourage others. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- The social optimum is that many players contribute, since the multiplied pot benefits everyone. The private incentive in any single round is to free-ride (keep 20) because your private return from contributing is less than the private cost.
- Rewarding creates a second-stage mechanism to sustain cooperation: contributors who are recognized as beneficial targets may receive positive transfers, offsetting the private cost of contribution.
- Uncertainty and anonymity (no pre-decision knowledge of others' choices and rewards are anonymous to recipients) make initial rounds exploratory — some players test contributing, some test free-riding.
- Because the game has a known end (14 rounds), backward induction predicts declining contributions toward the final rounds. Rewarding can slow but usually not completely prevent decline; we expect some cooperation early and mid-game, with gradual erosion in later rounds.
- In practice players reward sparingly (costly and only partial compensation), so rewards will typically be few and directed at observable cooperative behavior. As rounds progress players will reduce rewards and contributions, particularly in the last few rounds.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 14 rounds. Each round players choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so a lone cooperator subsidizes others; full cooperation improves everyone’s group payoff but is individually costly if others defect. Because choices are simultaneous and private, conditional cooperation (matching others) and occasional free-riding are likely. Early rounds tend to show higher cooperation as norms form; without strong incentives, cooperation often drifts downward as players exploit others or anticipate endgame defection. The reward stage provides a private way to reinforce cooperation: sending reward units is costly to the sender but benefits the recipient, so we expect sparse, targeted rewards typically directed at cooperators (or to reciprocate). Rewards can stabilize cooperation temporarily, but because they’re costly and players cannot identify who rewarded them, their use is usually limited. Toward the final rounds, reciprocity and rewards tend to decline and defection becomes more common (endgame effect).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with 13 players, 20 coins each round, and binary contribution choices (0 or 20). Contributing increases the shared pot which is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all players, so the private benefit of contributing is typically lower than the private return from keeping the endowment (defectors usually earn more that round unless they are compensated). After redistribution, players can pay 3 coins per reward unit to give 4 coins to another player — a modest, targeted way to compensate cooperators or reward preferred peers.

Typical dynamics: early rounds are uncertain — some players test cooperation hoping to trigger reciprocal behaviour or rewards; others free-ride because defection pays better immediately. Occasional, modest rewards to contributors can sustain cooperation for a few rounds among reciprocity-minded players, but with a known finite horizon (14 rounds), cooperation tends to decline toward the end as incentives to maintain reputation or future reciprocity weaken. Rewards are usually sparse and directed toward visible cooperators; because players cannot see choices before acting and cannot identify reward sources in summaries, reward use is conservative and mostly symbolic (1-unit rewards are common).

Given these incentives and uncertainties, I predict an initial moderate level of cooperation with a few small, targeted rewards. Cooperation drifts downward in the middle rounds as defection looks better, with a few cooperators and occasional rewards persisting. Near the final rounds most players defect (no contribution), and rewards largely disappear.

# GAME STARTS

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players over 14 rounds. Each round each player chooses whether to contribute their whole endowment (20) or keep it (0). Every contributed 20 is multiplied by 2.6 and then shared equally, which in this setup means each single 20 contribution yields 52 to the pot which gives 4 to every player (52/13 = 4). So a contribution reduces a player's private holding by 20 but gives 4 to everyone; personally the immediate monetary return per 20 contributed is 4 to oneself (less than 20), so the individual incentive is to free-ride, but group payoffs are higher when more people contribute.

After redistribution players can spend resources to reward others: each reward unit costs 3 to the sender and gives 4 to the recipient. That cost-benefit means rewarding is slightly net-positive for group payoff (+1 to group per unit), and rewards are a mechanism players can use to encourage cooperators. However, rewards are costly to send and players only see the aggregate reward amounts received (not who sent them), so rewards will tend to be sparse and targeted at visible cooperators when players want to reinforce contributions.

Uncertainties here include private choices (decisions are simultaneous) and the inability to identify reward senders. Expectation: early rounds usually show some mixture of cooperation and free-riding. Sparse positive rewards will appear to reinforce contributors, possibly raising cooperation in middle rounds. Toward the final rounds (endgame), cooperation typically drifts downward as the strategic motive to invest in future reciprocity weakens. Throughout, reward use remains cautious (a few 1-unit tokens rather than large transfers).

game_explanation: This repeated public goods game (14 rounds) pits private incentives to free-ride against group gains from contributing. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and the pot is multiplied by 2.6 then split equally, a lone contributor helps the group but bears the private cost; mutual cooperation yields highest group payoffs. Uncertainty about others’ choices and the short horizon encourage a typical pattern: some players start cooperatively or experiment, conditional cooperators respond to visible contributions in summaries and reward selectively to reinforce cooperation, and free-riders exploit high contributors. Rewards (costly to give, beneficial to recipients) are used sparingly to promote cooperation early and midgame. Toward the end (final rounds) the incentive to maintain costly cooperation weakens (backward induction), so contributions drift down and rewarding fades. I therefore predict moderate-to-high cooperation early, a peak in the middle rounds as conditional cooperators coordinate, then a gradual decline to mostly defection by the final round, with only occasional, targeted rewards throughout.

game_explanation:
This is a finite-horizon public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds. Each round players privately choose either to contribute their entire endowment (20) to a group pot or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, then players may spend coins to reward others (cost 3 per reward unit to sender, benefit 4 per unit to receiver). Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Social dilemma: contributing increases group payoff but is individually costly; free-riding (contributing 0) maximizes immediate private payoff if others contribute.
- Conditional cooperation and uncertainty: because choices are private during decision, players infer others’ behavior from previous rounds and from rewards received. Many players start as conditional cooperators—willing to contribute early if they expect reciprocity.
- Rewards as incentive: rewards can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors. They are costly, so they will be used sparingly and selectively (typically 1 unit, occasionally 2).
- End-game effect: with a known finite horizon (14 rounds), cooperation tends to unravel toward the final rounds because future reciprocation is limited; players often defect more in the last few rounds and stop rewarding.
- Uncertainty and heterogeneity: players differ (some are steady cooperators, some are defectors, some fluctuate). We therefore expect a mix: moderate cooperation early, reinforced by a small number of targeted rewards, peaking mid-game, then gradual decline and near-universal defection in the last round(s). Rewards will be sparse throughout, concentrated on those who contribute and occasionally given as tokens to surprising contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, finite public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player chooses between contributing all 20 coins to the group pot or keeping all 20 privately. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the 13 players, so a single 20-coin contribution increases the group pot by 52 coins but yields only 52/13 = 4 coins back to each player. Individually, contributing is costly (you forgo 20 to get 4 back), so free-riding (contributing 0) strictly dominates in a one-shot sense. However, because players play 14 repeated rounds and can reward others after seeing the round’s contributions, conditional cooperation and positive reciprocity can sustain some contributions early on.

The reward stage lets players spend 3 coins to give 4 coins to another player per reward unit. This is costly for the giver but can selectively boost cooperators’ payoffs, creating incentives to reward contributors and discourage free-riding (through lack of reward). Because players do not know others’ choices before contributing, contributions are tentative early on; reward feedback after each round shapes subsequent behavior. Because the horizon is known (14 rounds), there is strategic uncertainty about how quickly cooperation unravels: we expect moderate cooperation early as players test norms and reward cooperators, then gradual decline as some begin to free-ride and the incentive to sustain cooperation weakens. Near the final rounds, backward induction and the absence of future reciprocity push contributions toward zero; rewards are used sparingly throughout, mainly to thank or reciprocate visible cooperators.

game_explanation: This 14-round public goods game pits private incentives against group welfare. Each round, players decide whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all 13 players, so each contributing player helps the group (each extra contributor raises everyone's share) but is personally worse off than a non-contributor in the same round. Because players see only aggregated outcomes and not others' choices before deciding, early rounds usually show some cooperation (trust/experimentation), reinforced if cooperators are rewarded. Rewarding is costly (3 coins per unit) but increases the recipient's payoff by 4 coins, so rewards can sustain cooperation at the group level when used sparingly and targeted at cooperators. Over time, with a known finite horizon (14 rounds), we typically see cooperation fall as the end approaches (end-game defection). Rewards are used conservatively and decline with cooperation, since they are individually costly and less effective near the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a simultaneous-move public goods game with 13 players. Each player has 20 coins and must either contribute all 20 or keep all 20. Contributions go to a common pot multiplied by 2.6 and divided equally. Each 20 contributed creates 20 * 2.6 = 52 in the pot, which gives every player 52/13 = 4 coins back. So a contributing player sacrifices 20 private coins but only recovers 4 from the group return — a personal net loss of 16 compared to keeping the 20. Individually, free-riding (contributing 0) strictly dominates contributing, but if many contribute the group payoff is higher.

Because moves are simultaneous and players cannot identify who rewarded them, incentives for contribution rely on social preferences and indirect reciprocity: players may use the reward stage to encourage cooperators by spending 3 coins to give 4 to someone else. Rewards can partially offset the private loss of contributing, but reward-givers also pay a cost. The known finite horizon (14 rounds) creates an “endgame” incentive: backward induction predicts declining cooperation toward zero in later rounds. In practice, we expect an initial phase with moderate cooperation (some players contribute out of fairness or hoping for reciprocity), sparse targeted rewards to sustain cooperation, and then a gradual erosion of contributions and reward use as the end approaches. Rewards will be used sparingly and mostly to reinforce contributors in early/mid rounds; by the final rounds both contributions and rewards will largely collapse.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game (14 rounds) creates a tension between private incentives (keep 20 coins) and group benefits (each 20 contributed is multiplied by 2.6 and shared). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are salient: contributing is costly privately but helps everyone. Punishment and reward let players impose social sanctions or reinforce cooperation, but both are costly to the sender (3 coins per unit). Punishment is particularly powerful (11 coins off the target per unit) and so can deter free-riding, but because punishers pay a cost and cannot be identified by targets, punishment tends to be used sparingly and somewhat indiscriminately. Rewards are milder (4 coins to the target per unit) and also costly to the giver; they are used even more sparingly to signal appreciation.

Given the finite horizon (14 rounds), we expect:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high cooperation as players test each other and use occasional rewards/punishments to enforce norms.
- Middle rounds: some decline in cooperation as free-riding appears; a small number of punishments and rewards are used selectively to influence behavior.
- Final rounds: cooperation tends to drop (backward induction and reduced incentive to sustain norms), and sanctioning becomes rarer because its future benefits vanish.

Uncertainties: who will sustain cooperation, whether punishments will be credible enough to deter defectors, and how costly sanctioning will alter individual payoffs. Overall, expect sparse but targeted punishment and even sparser rewards; contributions decline toward the final rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) over 14 rounds. Each coin contributed yields a group return of 2.6 times the pot split evenly; that implies each additional 20 contributed raises every player's payoff by 4, while it costs the contributor 20. So individual incentive: free-ride (keep 20) because the private return from contributing is negative (-16), but group-benefit incentive favors contribution. In practice we typically see an early period of moderate cooperation (many conditional cooperators hope others will contribute), occasional targeted punishments to deter persistent free-riders, and occasional rewards to encourage cooperators. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 per unit) but inflicts large damage on targets (11 per unit), so it is used sparingly; rewards are also costly (3 per unit) but give modest benefits (+4 per unit to the recipient) and are used even more sparingly.

Uncertainties: players do not observe others' choices before deciding in a round, and they cannot identify who gave/received punishment or reward in summaries. This makes reputational enforcement noisier. Because the game has a known finite horizon (14 rounds), we expect an "endgame" decline: cooperators reduce contributions toward the final rounds since future enforcement or reciprocity is limited. Overall likely pattern: decent cooperation in early rounds, gradual decline as some players start free-riding, few targeted punishments/rewards trying to stabilize cooperation (with limited success), and substantial defection by late rounds.

Final round: the incentive to contribute is lowest because there is no future to influence; nearly universal defection is expected. Punishment and reward are effectively absent.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This public goods game will likely show the familiar pattern from laboratory PGGs: a fair share of players start by contributing (hoping for mutual cooperation), while a handful free-ride. When free-riding persists, a few players occasionally use costly punishment to sanction defectors and occasional rewards to reinforce cooperators. Punishment and reward are used sparingly because both are costly to the sender. Over repeated rounds, cooperation can be sustained for a while if sanctions and rewards are targeted, but the "end-game" effect typically reduces cooperation near the final rounds as incentives to enforce norms drop. Because players cannot see who punished or rewarded them, social enforcement is blunt and selective rather than retaliatory. Expect: moderate-to-high cooperation early (many 20s), some targeted punishments and occasional rewards in the middle rounds to stabilize cooperation, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the last few rounds with few or no sanctions in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses to contribute either 0 or 20 coins to a common pot; each contributed 20 yields 52 coins to the pot which are split equally across the 13 players (so one contributor gives up 20 and each player — contributor and noncontributor alike — gets 4 from that contribution). Individually there is an incentive to free-ride (not contribute) because the private return from contributing is lower than the private cost; socially, contributions raise total payoffs. Because players see round summaries (but not who punished or rewarded them) and the game length is known, we expect the usual pattern: some initial cooperation by conditional or prosocial players, limited and targeted punishment/reward to sustain cooperation, and a gradual decline in contributions over rounds as free-riding spreads and the end approaches. Punishment and rewards are costly to the sender and have asymmetric effects on the receiver (punishment: sender pays 3 per unit to subtract 11 per unit from target; reward: sender pays 3 per unit to give +4 per unit to target), so players use them sparingly. Uncertainty about others' types (conditional cooperator vs. persistent defector) and the finite horizon will produce variation: occasional corrections (defectors switching after being punished), some temporary recoveries in midgame, and a pronounced drop toward the last rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This online public goods game (14 rounds) pits private short-term incentives (keep your 20 coins) against collective benefits (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among 13 players). Typical dynamics: early rounds often see moderate cooperation as players test each other; free-riding (keeping the 20) can pay off immediately unless others sanction defectors. Punishment and reward are available but costly: each sanction or reward unit costs the sender 3 coins; punishment deducts 11 coins from a target and reward adds 4 coins to a target. Because sanctions are costly, players use them sparingly; they tend to punish recurring free-riders early to sustain cooperation, and sometimes reward cooperators. As the horizon approaches, cooperation usually declines (end-game effect), and sanctions become less frequent.

I therefore predict an initial phase with several contributors and a few sparse sanctions to discipline clear free-riders, some occasional positive rewards to reinforce cooperators, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the final rounds. Punishment and reward are used modestly and mostly focused on persistent defectors (e.g., DOG, CROCODILE), with a few one-off rewards to sustain cooperative behavior. In the final round, players expect no future consequences, so contributions and sanctions drop.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players for 14 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their entire endowment (20) to the group pot or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and shared equally; so individual incentives favor free-riding (keeping 20) while group payoff is maximized by contributing. After seeing contributions, players can punish or reward others at private cost: punish costs 3 to the punisher and removes 11 from the target per unit; reward costs 3 and gives 4 to the recipient per unit. Punishment and reward are costly, so they are used sparingly in most experiments. Because the horizon is finite and known (14 rounds), cooperation often declines toward the end (end-game effect), though early and mid-game cooperation can be sustained if players occasionally punish defectors or reward cooperators.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' tendencies leads to mixed behavior — some contributors, some free-riders. Cooperators may punish visible defectors a little and reward cooperative behavior sparsely to sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: if punishments/rewards successfully deter free-riding, contribution rates can rise/stabilize; if not, more players begin to defect. Punishments tend to be targeted and limited — a few actors punished rather than everyone being sanctioned.
- Late rounds: as the known end approaches, incentives to punish decline and contributions typically fall (unraveling). Rewards also taper off. A few stubborn cooperators may persist, but the group often approaches widespread defection by round 14.

Below I simulate a plausible transcript given those incentives and the sparse use of sanctions and rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 14 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20) and a post-contribution stage where players can punish or reward others at non-trivial costs. Key incentives:
- Contributing 20 helps the group: the pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so cooperators increase everyone’s payout but may be personally exploited by free-riders.
- Free-riding (contributing 0) is attractive in the short run, because a defector keeps their private 20 while still getting a share of the public pot.
- Punishment and reward allow conditional cooperators to enforce norms: punishments are expensive to send (3 coins per unit) but harsh on targets (−11 per unit), and rewards are cheaper for targets (+4 per unit) but still costly to send. This typically leads to sparse use of sanctions and rewards — enough to influence behavior but not so much that players overspend.
- Uncertainties: players do not see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who punished or rewarded them in the summary, so sanctioning is anonymous (only aggregate effects are shown). This makes targeted reciprocity less precise and encourages simple heuristics (e.g., punish visible defectors, reward cooperators).
- Dynamics I expect: many players will start by cooperating (first few rounds tend to be relatively cooperative). A small set of persistent defectors (the “SLOTH” type) will quickly emerge and be punished sparingly. Conditional cooperators will use a few punishments or rewards to sustain cooperation, producing some bounce-backs. As the finite horizon approaches (final few rounds), cooperation will decline (endgame effect) and sanctioning becomes less frequent because the deterrent effect weakens. Overall punishment and reward activity will be present but modest each round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players over 14 rounds. In each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly. After seeing the round’s results, players may spend coins to punish or reward others (punishment: source pays 3 per unit, target loses 11 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit, target gains 4 per unit). Players cannot identify who acted on them, and the game length (14 rounds) is common knowledge.

Key dynamics and incentives:
- The direct material incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) each round because you keep your 20 and still get a share of the multiplied pot. But if many free-ride, everyone’s share falls.
- Conditional cooperators may contribute when they expect others to contribute; observed defections usually prompt sparse punishment to deter free-riding. In real experiments punishment and reward are used sparingly because they are costly.
- Rewards are occasionally used to reinforce cooperation when the marginal benefit (friendship-like reciprocity) outweighs the cost of rewarding.
- As the game progresses toward the final rounds, forward-looking players have less incentive to punish (no future rounds to enforce cooperation) and cooperation often decays — especially in the last few rounds. However, some players may continue cooperating out of habit or reciprocity.
- There is uncertainty about who punished/rewarded anyone (no identification), which reduces targeted retaliation but still allows collective norms to form via anonymous sanctions.

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players over 14 rounds. In each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly. After seeing the round’s results, players may spend coins to punish or reward others (punishment: source pays 3 per unit, target loses 11 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit, target gains 4 per unit). Players cannot identify who acted on them, and the game length (14 rounds) is common knowledge.

Key dynamics and incentives:
- The direct material incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) each round because you keep your 20 and still get a share of the multiplied pot. But if many free-ride, everyone’s share falls.
- Conditional cooperators may contribute when they expect others to contribute; observed defections usually prompt sparse punishment to deter free-riding. In real experiments punishment and reward are used sparingly because they are costly.
- Rewards are occasionally used to reinforce cooperation when the marginal benefit (friendship-like reciprocity) outweighs the cost of rewarding.

- There is uncertainty about who punished/rewarded anyone (no identification), which reduces targeted retaliation but still allows collective norms to form via anonymous sanctions.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and may either keep them (contribute 0) or put all 20 into a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players. Individual incentives favor keeping coins (free-riding) because the per-person return from contributing is 1.9/10 = 0.19 of the contributed amount (3.8 total returned to each player when one person contributes), so a lone contributor loses relative to non-contributors. However, if many players contribute, group payoffs rise and contributors can sometimes do well relative to keeping coins. Uncertainty about others' choices and the one-shot incentive to defect typically lead to declining cooperation over repeated rounds, although sparse communication can occasionally sustain or restore partial cooperation.

I therefore predict somewhat high cooperation early (some players experimenting with contributing), a gradual decline as players respond to free-riding, occasional attempted recoveries driven by a few communicative players, and possibly a modest late-round uptick as some players try to "end on a good note." Chat will be sparse and purposeful: short pledges, short reports after seeing results, brief promises or complaints.

This is a 15-round public goods game where each player gets 20 coins each round and can either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) to a shared pot. Every coin contributed is multiplied by 1.9 and then split equally among all 10 players, so each contributor's single-coin contribution returns 1.9/10 = 0.19 coins to themselves (i.e., contributing 20 coins gives them a personal return of 3.8 coins). The private incentive is to free-ride (keep 20 and get whatever share comes from others), while the group optimum is for everyone to contribute. Communication can sustain cooperation early on, but the well-known end-game effect (no future rounds to punish or reward) usually leads to more defection in the final rounds. Uncertainty about who will keep promises and occasional broken commitments will produce a gradual decline in contributions across the 15 rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with ten players, 15 rounds, and binary choices each round (contribute 20 or 0). Each coin contributed increases the group pot only a little for each individual: each 20 contributed raises every player's round payoff by 3.8 (20 * 0.19), so a contributor loses 16.2 relative to keeping the 20 privately if everyone else stays fixed. That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride every round. However, when many contribute the group payoff is large (contributors can end up earning more than 20 in absolute terms), so there is room for conditional cooperation: players may contribute early to test trust or retaliate after seeing free-riding. Communication (sparse here) can help coordinate cooperation, but last-round incentives favor universal defection, and contributions typically decline toward the end. Expect some early cooperation, exploitation by a few free-riders, subsequent retaliation and oscillation, with contributions tapering off in later rounds and collapse in the final round.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game pits individual incentives against the group's best interest. Each round every player is endowed with 20 coins and must either keep it (contribute 0) or put the entire 20 into a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the ten players. A single contribution benefits the whole group (it adds 38 coins to the pot, so each player receives 3.8 more), but the contributor personally bears the full 20-coin cost while only receiving 3.8 back. So individually it's always tempting to free-ride (keep 20 and still receive the shared return); collectively it's best if everyone contributes (everyone would get 38 each).

Typical dynamics: early rounds sometimes see a handful of contributions as players test cooperation and make promises. When players observe that free-riders earn more in a given round, cooperation tends to drop in subsequent rounds. Occasional messages (pledges, calls for fairness, or shaming) can temporarily revive contributions, especially mid-game if a few players coordinate. Over 15 rounds, I expect overall decline in contributions, with sparse attempts to rebuild cooperation and a few short-lived revivals. Chat will be sparse and mostly promises, reactions to payoffs, or attempts to coordinate.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game pits individual incentives against the group's best interest. Each round every player is endowed with 20 coins and must either keep it (contribute 0) or put the entire 20 into a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the ten players. A single contribution benefits the whole group (it adds 38 coins to the pot, so each player receives 3.8 more), but the contributor personally bears the full 20-coin cost while only receiving 3.8 back. So individually it's always tempting to free-ride (keep 20 and still receive the shared return); collectively it's best if everyone contributes (everyone would get 38 each).

Typical dynamics: early rounds sometimes see a handful of contributions as players test cooperation and make promises. When players observe that free-riders earn more in a given round, cooperation tends to drop in subsequent rounds. Occasional messages (pledges, calls for fairness, or shaming) can temporarily revive contributions, especially mid-game if a few players coordinate. Over 15 rounds, I expect overall decline in contributions, with sparse attempts to rebuild cooperation and a few short-lived revivals. Chat will be sparse and mostly promises, reactions to payoffs, or attempts to coordinate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a typical repeated public goods game with a harsh private incentive to free-ride: contributing 20 always reduces your private holding but increases the group pot, which is then shared. A single defector benefits more than a single contributor in every round because contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split across 10 players. That makes defection the dominant individual action in a one-shot game. Because the game is repeated and players can communicate, we expect some initial cooperation driven by goodwill, experimentation, or explicit coordination. Communication can sustain moderate cooperation for a few rounds (especially early), but knowing the game has a finite 15-round horizon creates backward-induction pressure: players have strong incentives to stop cooperating as the end approaches. So a plausible unfolding is: modest cooperation in rounds 1–3 (some players contribute), gradually falling cooperation mid-game as free-riding is observed, and near-universal defection in the final rounds. Chats will be sparse and strategic (promises, conditional proposals, complaints).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). Each round, the total contributed is multiplied by 1.9 and evenly split among the 10 players. Contributing is socially efficient (it raises the group's payout) but individually costly in the short run because a contributor sacrifices 20 private coins while only receiving a fraction (0.19 times their contribution) back through the shared pot. Typical dynamics: some players will try to coordinate cooperation early, but free-riding (defection) tends to appear as players who contribute see higher immediate payoffs from not contributing. Communication can sustain cooperation if trusted, but messages are sparse and promises are sometimes broken. Over many rounds, cooperation often declines as players respond to observed defections and anticipate endgame defection (last-round collapse). Expect moderate cooperation early, a decline in the middle, occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation via pledges, and low contributions toward the end.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits 10 players against the social dilemma: each round everyone can either keep their 20 coins or put them all into a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so each contributor reduces their private holding by 20 but increases every player’s share by 3.8. That makes free-riding (contribute 0) individually attractive in every round, but if many free-ride the group's total payoff falls. With 15 known rounds and cheap communication, expect an initial burst of partial cooperation (some players test and coordinate), then a general decline as free-riding pays off and reciprocity breaks down. Occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation may appear (short messages like “let’s try again”), producing isolated rounds with a few contributors, but most players will drift toward defection mid-to-late game. Chat will be sparse and purposeful: a few proposals, acknowledgements, and complaints after seeing payoffs.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a shared pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and then divided equally among all 10 players. That means a contributor effectively forgoes 20 private coins to increase the group pot; the per-player return from each contributed 20 is 1.9*20/10 = 3.8 coins. If k players contribute in a round, each player (contributor or not) receives 3.8*k coins from the pot; contributors end the round with 3.8*k coins (they gave up their 20), non-contributors end with 20 + 3.8*k.

Incentives: free riding (not contributing) always yields 20 coins more than contributing in that same round, because you keep your 20 and still receive the pot share. However everybody is better off collectively if many contribute (higher 3.8*k for all). Because play is repeated and public payoffs (each player's round net payoff) are shown at the end of each round, players can condition future decisions on others' behavior. Uncertainties include whether promises in chat are sincere, how many others will reciprocate cooperation, and how quickly people will "unravel" cooperation toward the known end (the last round). Typical dynamics: modest cooperation early on, some free riding when it looks profitable, occasional reciprocity that can sustain cooperation for several rounds, and a decline in contributions toward the final rounds as the endgame approaches. Chat can sustain cooperation but is used sparingly; declarations are informative only if they are followed by consistent actions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a finitely repeated public goods game with 10 players, 15 rounds, and a binary contribution choice each round: either keep 20 private coins or contribute all 20 to the shared pot. Each contributed 20 is multiplied by 1.9 then split equally among all 10 players, so each contributor increases every player's round payoff by 3.8. From an individual standpoint contributing loses 20 while returning only 3.8 to yourself — so free-riding (keeping your 20) strictly dominates contributing in a one-shot sense. That makes full defection the unique Nash equilibrium each round.

Nevertheless, in repeated play with communication we commonly see some early cooperation: players may pledge to contribute and some follow through. Early rounds often contain mixed contributions; those who defect earn noticeably more in a round when others contribute, which tends to undermine cooperation. Over time, absent strong enforcement or highly credible coordination, contributions typically decline toward zero (end-game unraveling): as the known final round approaches, incentives to cooperate weaken and cooperation collapses. Messages can slow the collapse for a few rounds, or generate one-off attempts to revive cooperation, but the dominant pattern is declining contributions and sparse chats as players learn that defection pays better.

The transcript below simulates a plausible path: some initial cooperation with a few pledges, quick learning by some players to free-ride, a short attempt to restart cooperation, and then steady collapse to near-universal defection across the middle and end of the game. Chats are sparse and purposeful (promises, brief complaints, calls for coordination).

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public goods game. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and divided equally among the 10 players, so each contributed 20 adds 3.8 coins to every player's return. For an individual, contributing costs 20 but only returns 3.8 to them — the private incentive is to free-ride. The group optimum is everyone contributing (each would get 38), but unilateral deviation pays off (you get 20 + share while contributors get only the share).

Given those incentives and sparse cheap talk, expect some early attempts to coordinate cooperation (a few players commit and ask others to join), which yields modest public returns. Free-riding is tempting and visible through round-by-round payoff summaries, so cooperation is likely to decline over rounds. A few persistent cooperators may keep contributing occasionally (either out of altruism or strategy), and there may be occasional late-round “last-try” contributions, but overall contributions will tend to fall toward zero as the game progresses.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and optional costly punishment. Typical dynamics: players try cooperation early but free-riders can gain short-term advantage (they keep their 20 while sharing the multiplied pot). Costly punishment can deter free-riding by making defection costly, so a few punishers can sustain high contributions. Messaging can help coordinate cooperation. Because the horizon is finite and common knowledge, incentives to defect grow near the end (rounds 13–15), so cooperation often unravels late unless punishers are willing to bear costs to enforce norms. Punishment tends to be sparse and targeted (one or two units aimed at repeat defectors), and chats are infrequent but often used to call out or encourage behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players who each get 20 coins per round and must either contribute all 20 or nothing. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so private payoff-maximizing choice is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, because players can punish others (costing the punisher 4 coins per unit and reducing the target by 8 coins per unit) and the game repeats for a known finite horizon, we should expect a mix of strategies:

- Early rounds: Many players tend to try cooperation or at least test it; defectors earn more short-term, so cooperators may use occasional punishments to deter free-riding.
- Middle rounds: If punishment is used sparingly and credibly, cooperation tends to stabilize — several rounds of everyone contributing are plausible.
- Late rounds (end-game): As the final round approaches, incentives to free-ride grow and cooperation typically unravels; punishment becomes less frequent because its long-term enforcement value disappears and it is costly.
- Chat is typically sparse and used to signal intent or call out defectors; punishment is also used sparsely, usually targeting clear defectors rather than broad retaliation.

Below is a round-by-round transcript consistent with these dynamics, showing contributions, sparse chat, punishment acts (only non-zero), and round summaries (net payoff, coins spent on punishment, coins deducted by punishment) for each player in the exact avatar order.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and costly peer punishment. Each round each player privately decides whether to contribute their entire 20 or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so contributing helps the group but is individually costly (defection gains the private 20 while still earning the same share). Punishment allows cooperators to sanction defectors at personal cost (4 per punishment unit) inflicting larger losses on targets (8 per unit). Players see who punished them in summaries, can message the group, and know the game length.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some initial cooperation driven by norms or pleas in chat; a few free-riders may appear. Because defection is privately profitable, without sanctions contributions tend to drift down.
- Punishment effect: when cooperators use punishment selectively against repeat defectors, average cooperation often rises or stabilizes. Punishments will be sparse because they are costly, targeted at repeated defectors, and usually small (1–2 units).
- Retaliation and noise: punished players sometimes retaliate, causing occasional punitive cycles, but these are limited because punishment is costly and easily identified.
- Endgame: with 15 known finite rounds, cooperation tends to unravel toward the final rounds; punishment persists but often declines because sanctioning late-stage free-riding yields less future benefit.
- Chat: sparse, mostly coordination, short reproaches after punishments, occasional threats early on, and brief wrap-ups at the end.

Transcript below simulates these tendencies: some early cooperation, intermittent punishment targeted at repeat defectors, a few small retaliations, and a gradual decline in cooperation toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20), a group multiplier of 1.9, and costly punishment (each punishment unit costs the sender 4 and reduces the target by 8). Early rounds usually show some mix of cooperation and free-riding. Because players see punishment identities and round summaries, norm-enforcing punishers can increase cooperation over the middle rounds. Punishment is costly, so it will be used sparingly and mainly to signal that defection is costly. Toward the end of a finite game, backward induction makes defection more attractive (last-round free-riding), so cooperation typically unravels in the final rounds even if punishment was effective earlier. Chat messages will be scarce and used mainly to warn, respond to punishments, or signal intentions.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits private incentive (keep your 20) against group benefit (everyone gains if many contribute). Each 20 contributed creates 38 total for the pot, but split among 10 players so each contributor personally recovers only 3.8 — a private loss but a group gain. Expect early rounds to feature conditional cooperation: many will contribute to signal willingness to cooperate, while a few will try free-riding. Punishment is available and typically used sparingly to deter clear, repeated free-riders. Punishment is costly to send and costly to the target, so players tend to punish first-time or occasional defectors lightly, and target repeat offenders more. Communication (chat) will be sparse and mainly used for short appeals, warnings, or thanks. As the finite horizon approaches, expect increasing end-game defection (fewer contributions) because punishment loses future leverage; punishment frequency should decline in the final rounds. Overall: cooperation will be sustained early and midgame through modest, targeted punishments; late-game will show a collapse toward free-riding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players choose to contribute 20 or 0 from a 20-coin endowment; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players. Punishment is available after contributions: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 8 coins. Players see only outcomes after choices, and can identify who punished them. The finite horizon (15 rounds) creates an incentive to defect in later rounds, but social preferences and punishment often sustain cooperation earlier. Expect:
- Early rounds: some cooperation as players "test" each other; a few shirkers may be punished once or twice to induce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: relatively high cooperation supported by occasional, targeted punishment.
- Late rounds: cooperation typically unravels as players anticipate the end (backward induction), so defections increase in the final few rounds; punishment becomes less frequent because it costs the punisher and has diminishing deterrence.
- Chat is sparse and mostly used for short promises, warnings, or complaints after punishments.
Uncertainty: who will be the persistent defectors and who will bear the cost of policing is unclear; punishers sacrifice to enforce norms but may tire out as the game nears its end.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 10 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK) against the classic temptation to free-ride. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must either contribute all 20 or nothing to the group pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Punishment is available but costly: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 8 coins. Messages are allowed and players can see who punished them.

Typical dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: players test each other's willingness to contribute. Many will contribute, some will defect to test enforcement; a few targeted punishments follow.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperation stabilizes if punishment is used sparingly but credibly; a small fraction of defectors keeps being punished.
- Late rounds: end-game unraveling appears. As the horizon shortens, some players reduce contributions and punish less (punishment is costly and deterred when future repercussions are limited), so cooperation declines in the final rounds.
- Chat will be sparse and functional: requests to cooperate, short justifications for punishment, occasional reproaches. Punishment will be used but rarely (one or two units and targeted), because it's expensive.

Below I simulate rounds 1–15 following these expectations: contributions, occasional targeted punishments, and sparse chat. At the end of each round I report each player's net payoff for that round, the amount they spent on punishing others, and the amount deducted from them by punishments they received.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 identifiable players who each choose to contribute either 0 or 20 coins to a shared pot every round. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all players, so contributing helps the group but costs the individual 20 coins immediately. Because players see round summaries (including who punished whom), costly punishment (4 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, 8 coins deducted per unit from the target) can be used to deter free-riding. Common dynamics to expect:

- Early rounds: some exploration. A few players may test free-riding; cooperators sometimes respond with targeted punishment, which can raise cooperation.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is used effectively and not too costly, cooperation tends to stay high as conditional cooperators reciprocate and punish defectors sparingly.
- Late rounds (end-game effect): because the game has a known finite horizon, cooperation typically declines. Players may defect more often in rounds near the end, and punishers may reduce enforcement since retaliation yields less future benefit.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful (announcing intentions or calling out defectors); punishments will be infrequent and targeted, not broad or excessive.

Below is a simulated transcript of all 15 rounds, showing contributions, sparse chat, punishments in tuple form (source, target, unit), and the round summaries showing each player's net payoff, punishment cost used, and punishment lost.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each player effectively chooses between contributing all 20 coins to the public pot (contribution = 20) or withdrawing them privately (contribution = 0). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among the 19 players. Individually, withdrawing is the dominant action: a player who contributes 20 lowers their private take by 20 but only increases the per-player share by (20 * 11.5 / 19) ≈ 12.11, so contributing costs about 7.89 coins to the contributor while helping the group. Group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes (each would then get 230), but individual incentives push toward withdrawal.

Because players do not see others' choices before deciding, the first round typically shows some uncertainty-driven cooperation: some will contribute hoping others do the same. After each round players see net payoffs for themselves and all peers; because contributors end up exactly 20 coins poorer than non-contributors in the same round (everyone receives the same public share, contributors forgo the private 20), players can infer who contributed from the payoff differences. That visibility tends to accelerate unraveling of cooperation. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, cooperation may persist for a few rounds before free-riding spreads; by later rounds we expect many or all players to withdraw.

predicted_rounds.explanation: No prior information is available. Some players (uncertain, conditional cooperators or optimistic types) will contribute to test cooperation; others will withdraw to secure the private 20. Expect a moderate level of cooperation as players explore.

Players who withdrew (and thus earned 20 more than contributors) are visibly better off; some contributors may switch to withdrawing in response, while a subset of conditional cooperators or those who continue to test cooperation may keep contributing. Expect fewer contributors than round 1.

Seeing that withdrawers earn ~20 more than contributors reinforces an incentive to withdraw. Some conditional cooperators may still contribute out of optimism or inertia, but overall cooperation declines further.

By now contributors are visibly worse off than withdrawers. Many have switched to withdrawing; a few persistent cooperators remain. Expect a small group of contributors and a larger majority withdrawing.
The pattern of better payoffs for withdrawers continues. Only a small minority persist in contributing (perhaps due to altruism or experiment curiosity). Cooperation is now low.
Most players have learned withdrawing is better; only a few continue contributing. Expect only a handful of contributors (stubborn cooperators), and modest public returns.
Contributors are now very few. Most players will withdraw consistently. Expect only one or two persistent contributors remaining.

Players see that persistent contributors continue to be disadvantaged. Most players stick with withdrawing; a couple may still test contributing out of principle or habit. Expect contributions to remain very low.
As the pattern persists, almost all players switch to withdrawing. A lone persistent contributor may remain for reasons of altruism or experiment curiosity, but overall cooperation is nearly gone.
By this point cooperation has fully unraveled. All players withdraw their private coins to avoid being exploited, so the public pot yields nothing.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either withdraws 20 coins (keeps them privately, contributing 0 to the group pot) or withdraws 0 (contributes the full 20). The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. Individual incentives: one player's contribution of 20 increases the total distributed amount by 20 * 11.5 = 230, which raises each player's share by 230/19 ≈ 12.11. Because 12.11 < 20, contributing is costly to the contributor in the short run: a single player who contributes gets a lower round payoff than if they had withdrawn 20. The social optimum (everyone contributes) would give each player a very large payoff (if all 19 contribute, each receives ≈230), but free-riding is individually tempting.

Given no pre-play communication, no observation of others' decisions before choosing, and players not knowing how many rounds remain, the typical unfolding is: some initial cooperative gesture(s) by optimistic or conditional cooperators, followed by exploitation by defectors who get higher round payoffs; seeing that defectors earn more tends to reduce cooperation in subsequent rounds. Because players cannot commit or coordinate and the marginal private return per contribution is negative, cooperation usually declines toward universal defection over repeated anonymous rounds—though isolated experiments or a few persistent cooperators can appear briefly.

After seeing that contributing yields lower payoffs than withdrawing in almost every instance, players are overwhelmingly likely to withdraw. Expect universal defection to persist.

game_explanation:
This is a one-shot repeated public goods situation with incomplete information about the total number of rounds and no communication. Each player faces a clear private incentive to withdraw the full 20 coins (contribute 0) because every coin kept gives an immediate private 1-to-1 benefit, while each coin left in the pot returns only 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to each player (the marginal private return is < 1). That makes full withdrawal the dominant action in a single-round sense, but full contribution by everyone yields much higher group payoffs (if everyone contributes 20, each receives 230 that round vs. 20 if nobody contributes).

Because players see round-by-round payoffs for all players after each round, they can infer who contributed and how much total was contributed. That feedback can produce conditional strategies: some players may try to cooperate initially, others may free-ride, and players who see persistent free-riding may stop cooperating in later rounds. Because the number of rounds is unknown, there is no guaranteed "final round" for backward induction, but the typical plausible path is: a few players experiment with cooperation early, free-riders exploit them (earning more that round), cooperation dwindles, maybe occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation appear, and overall contributions tend to fall as the game proceeds.

Below is a plausible transcript (rounds 1–10), showing at each round a brief explanation, the contributions (in the exact avatar order given), and the summary that players would see (each player's net payoff that round). Net payoff for a player = (20 − contribution) + (11.5 * total_contribution / 19). Contributions are either 0 or 20.

game_explanation:
This is a classic voluntary-contribution public goods game with 19 players. Each round everyone starts with a 20-coin endowment in the shared pot and must choose either to leave the 20 coins in the pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split evenly across all 19 players. Because 11.5 < 19, each coin left in the pot yields a per-player return of 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 coin; so a player who contributes 20 coins gets back about 12.1 coins total to the group but only about 12.1/19 ≈ 0.605 per coin — in aggregate they receive less of their own contribution than they give up. Thus contributing is socially beneficial if many do it, but individually costly compared with withdrawing (free-riding). Players do not know how many rounds there will be, and they only see the per-player net payoff for each participant at the end of each round (their own and peers'). That partial feedback makes it possible to infer others' likely choices each round, but it leaves ambiguity (a high payoff could be from free-riding or from many others contributing).

Typical likely dynamics: an early round with a fair number of contributors as players "test" cooperation, then gradual decline as free-riding appears and conditional cooperators reduce their contributions. Without communication and with individual incentives to defect, cooperation often erodes; some steady cooperators may persist and occasional experiments or attempts to re-establish cooperation may create small rebounds. Because the number of rounds is unknown, there is no deterministic endgame cascade, but learning and imitation of profitable free-riding tends to dominate over time.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round the full 20-coin endowment starts in the shared pot; each player either withdraws all 20 for private use (contributes 0) or leaves all 20 in the pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players. Because the private withdrawal is 20 for a defector and 0 for a cooperator, defecting always yields 20 coins more than cooperating in the same round (the public share is identical for everyone in a given round). That makes defection a strictly dominant action in a one-shot view. In a repeated setting (players do not know how many rounds remain), conditional cooperators can sustain some contributions early on, but once players see that defectors earned 20 more in previous rounds, most cooperative behavior typically unravels. Expect moderate cooperation at the start from some optimistic or conditional players, then a steady decline as players learn they get a private advantage by withdrawing. By the final rounds players will likely all withdraw.
