game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 29 rounds. Each round players choose how many of their 20 coins to withdraw (private) so the remainder becomes the public contribution. The group return per contributed coin is 1.8 total, split across four players, so each contributor gets 0.45 back per coin they personally contribute. That creates a private incentive to withdraw rather than contribute, because a withdrawn coin yields 1.0 privately while a contributed coin gives only 0.45 back to oneself. With a known finite horizon, backward induction predicts contributions will tend to fall toward zero as the end approaches.

However, in practice players often start with moderate to high contributions and then gradually reduce them. Punishments and rewards can sustain cooperation early on: punishment (cost 3 to sender, -4 to target) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target) are both costly to the sender, so they are used sparingly. Because players see after each round who punished/rewarded them (but not others' full actions), targeted enforcement and occasional retaliation are possible. Uncertainty about others' current-round choices (simultaneous moves) means players will adjust based on recent summaries rather than real-time signals.

Likely unfolding: early rounds show moderate cooperation with occasional targeted punishment of clear free-riders. Rewards are rare and small, used to reinforce consistent contributors. Over many rounds contributions drift downward as the end nears; punishments become rarer (it is costly to punish) and ultimately most players withdraw fully in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round players decide how much of the 20-coin endowment to contribute to the shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Because contributions benefit everyone but are privately costly, there is a tension between self-interest (keeping coins) and group interest (contributing). Early rounds often see fairly high contributions as players try to establish cooperation. Over time, some players may reduce contributions to free-ride, which can trigger sparse punishment or selective rewards. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per punishment unit) and subtracts substantially from the target (4 coins per unit), so punishments tend to be used sparingly and usually in response to clear under-contribution. Rewards are also costly to give (3 coins per unit) but add only 2 coins per unit to the recipient, so they are also used selectively to reinforce cooperators. Because players know the game ends at round 29, contributions commonly decline toward the end (end-game effect), and punishments become less frequent late in the game. Across 29 rounds I predict an initial phase of relatively high contributions with occasional small rewards/punishments to enforce cooperation, a mid-game decline as some free-riding emerges, and further decline toward the final rounds, with only occasional sanctions or rewards.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 4 players, 29 rounds, and open information after each round (contributions are revealed and players can then punish or reward). Individually, each coin you contribute yields 1.8 total return to the group but only 0.45 to yourself (1.8/4), so the dominant short-run incentive is to withdraw everything (free-ride). The group optimum is full contribution. Punishment (cost 3 to sender, -4 to target) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target) are available but costly; they can sustain cooperation early when reputational concerns matter, but their private cost means they are used sparingly. Because the game has a known finite horizon (29 rounds), we expect some degree of cooperation in early and mid rounds supported by intermittent punishment and occasional rewards, and a gradual decline toward the later rounds as end-game unraveling sets in. Uncertainties include imperfect beliefs about others' responses to punishment/reward and occasional mistakes or experiments; because punishment/reward is individually identifiable, targeted enforcement is possible but will be used conservatively.

# GAME STARTS

This repeated public goods game with 29 known rounds will likely show cooperation early, some attempts to sustain it via occasional rewards and scarce punishments, and a gradual decline toward the end as the finite horizon causes end-game defection. Early rounds: some players test generosity and others reward cooperators or rarely punish clear free-riding. game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round players choose how much to keep privately (withdraw) so their contribution is 20 minus that withdrawal. The group benefit of contributions is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so individual incentives favor free-riding (keep more privately) while group incentives favor contributing. Uncertainty comes from not observing others' choices before deciding; players must form beliefs from past behavior. Over many rounds, cooperative patterns often emerge early (high contributions), sustained by deterrent threats (punishment) or positive reinforcement (rewards). Punishment and reward are costly to send, so they are used sparingly in real games: punitive acts tend to target persistent low contributors and are usually modest; rewards are used occasionally to reinforce high contributors. Anticipation of the finite horizon (29 rounds) typically leads to declining contributions toward the end as backward induction and endgame effects take hold. I therefore predict an initial phase of reasonably high contributions, intermittent targeted punishment of clear defectors by an enforcement-leaning player, occasional small rewards for steady contributors, recovery after punishments, and a gradual erosion of contributions in late rounds with little punishment in the absolute final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with known finite horizon (29 rounds). Each round players decide how much of their 20 coins to keep privately (withdraw) so that the remainder becomes the group's contribution; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Punishment and reward are possible but costly, so we expect cooperation to be sustained early by a mix of reciprocity and occasional rewards/punishments, then to drift downward as the horizon approaches (endgame decline). Punishment is typically used sparingly to discipline clear free-riders; rewards are used even more sparingly to reinforce high contributions. Uncertainties include whether any single player will try to carry cooperation, whether punishers can afford to police repeatedly, and whether players respond to punishment by increasing contributions or by retaliating; with no communication, enforcement is costly and cooperation usually decays over time.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 29 known rounds. Each round players trade off private payoff (withdrawing coins) against group benefit (contributing to the pot). The public return (1.8×sum of contributions) is split equally, so contributing benefits all but is individually costly. Because the horizon is finite and known, cooperation tends to be higher early on and may decay toward the end unless social sanctions (punishment or reward) sustain it. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and hurts the target more (−4 per punitive unit), so it is used sparingly to deter free-riding. Rewards are also costly (3 per unit) but give a smaller benefit to the target (+2 per unit); they are also used selectively to reinforce cooperation. Uncertainty about others’ choices in each round encourages conditional behavior: players often start cooperative, react to observed defections (sometimes with a single punitive action rather than repeated costly retaliation), and may gradually reduce contributions as the end approaches. Below I simulate likely contributions, occasional targeted punishments and sparse rewards, and the round-by-round summaries players would see.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round, players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to withdraw (private payoff) and how much to leave in the shared pot (contribution). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 0.45 to its contributor — making contribution costly for the individual (private incentive to withdraw everything) but beneficial for the group (social optimum is full contribution). Because choices are private each round, early rounds typically involve "testing" — players try moderate contributions to see others' behavior. Conditional cooperation is common: people tend to match peers' average contributions in subsequent rounds. Punishment and reward are available but costly: punishment costs the source 3 per unit and deducts 4 per unit from the target; reward costs the source 3 per unit and gives the target +2 per unit. Because these costs are high, sanctions and rewards tend to be used sparingly — often only when some players repeatedly free-ride or to maintain a norm of cooperation. Over a long horizon (29 rounds), we expect some initial cooperation that drifts downward as endgame approaches, with occasional sanctions or rewards used intermittently to try to sustain cooperation or to retaliate against clear free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) over 29 rounds. Each round every player decides privately how many of the 20 coins to withdraw (so contributions are 0–20). The group benefit from contributions is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so contributing benefits the group but costs the individual relative to private withdrawal. Punishment and reward are available after contributions: punishments are costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) and costly to the target (4 coins lost per unit); rewards cost the sender 3 per unit and give the target +2 per unit.

Typical dynamics: early rounds tend to show relatively high contributions as players test each other. Small deviations by one player can be met with infrequent punishment or occasional rewards to reinforce cooperation. Punishment/reward are used sparingly because they are costly to the sender. Over many rounds, unless punishment or reward successfully enforces cooperation, contributions usually drift downward, especially as the group approaches the final rounds (endgame effect). Here I predict a generally cooperative start, some selective punishment when one player undercuts the group repeatedly (mostly targeted at DUCK in this simulation), occasional small rewards to acknowledge consistent contributors, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the end as incentives to sustain cooperation weaken.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players for 29 rounds. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 tokens to leave in the shared pot (contribution) versus withdraw for private use. The shared contributions are multiplied (×1.8) and split equally, so contributing benefits the group but costs the individual relative to private withdrawal. After seeing contributions, players may pay to punish or reward specific peers; punishments are costly to the sender and reduce the target's payoff, while rewards are costly but boost the target's payoff. Players see who targeted them, and they receive only their own round summary (their net payoff, what they spent on punishment/reward, what they lost from being punished, and what they received from rewards).

Typical dynamics: early rounds often see moderate cooperation as players test each other. Isolated defections (low contributions) usually provoke small, targeted punishments from some others to enforce norms. Rewards are rare but sometimes used to encourage cooperators. Over many rounds, cooperation can either stabilize around a positive level if punishments and rewards sustain norms, or drift down as players learn to free-ride, especially as the end approaches (endgame effect). Punishments are used sparingly because they are costly; retaliatory punishments can appear if punished players can identify and respond in later rounds. Because players only see their own summaries, information is noisy—players know who targeted them but not the full pattern—so uncertainty about intentions persists and behavior fluctuates.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their endowment of 20. The contributions are summed, doubled, and split equally among the three players; each player also keeps their unspent coins. Because the multiplier (2) divided by group size (3) yields a return to the group less than 1 per coin contributed (2/3 per coin to each contributor), the individually dominant strategy is to contribute 0 in a one-shot game. However, with repeated interaction players often begin by contributing some amount (social preferences, optimism, or conditional cooperation). Over rounds we should expect conditional cooperators to slowly reduce contributions in response to free-riding, while selfish players reduce immediately. As the finite horizon approaches, contributions tend to decline further (end-game effect). Uncertainty remains about exact responsiveness: some players may stabilize at moderate contributions; others may jump to zero early.

I predict initial moderate contributions from at least one player, then a gradual decline as free-riding by SLOTH (a slow, low-contribution type) pulls the others down. GORILLA behaves as a conditional cooperator who slowly mirrors the group average downward. DUCK starts relatively cooperative but reduces contributions in response. By the final rounds contributions fall to zero.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 3 players, 16 rounds, and a group multiplier of 2. Individually, each coin contributed yields only 2/3 of a coin back to its giver (the rest returns to others), so the one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0. However, conditional cooperation and reciprocity can sustain positive contributions early on: some players start generous, and others match or punish in subsequent rounds. Over many rounds without communication and with full information about peers' past contributions, a typical pattern is moderate initial cooperation followed by gradual decline as participants learn about free-riding — with a clear risk of end-game defection (near-zero contributions) once the horizon is known. Uncertainties include each player's type (free-rider vs. conditional cooperator), how quickly others react to deviations, and whether anyone tries to buoy cooperation mid-game by increasing contributions. Below I simulate one plausible play path consistent with these incentives.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone privately receives 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot (0–20). The pot is doubled and split equally. Individually, contributing 1 coin returns only 2/3 of that coin back to the contributor (since 2/3 < 1), so the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Socially, full contribution would increase everyone's payoffs, so cooperative behavior can raise group payoffs but is vulnerable to exploitation.

Given the finite, known horizon (16 rounds), typical dynamics are: some initial cooperation from conditional cooperators, exploitation by free-riders who earn short-term gains, and then a gradual unraveling of cooperation as players adjust to observed behavior—often ending in low or zero contributions. Uncertainties include individual types (some players may be steadfast cooperators or stubborn free-riders) and occasional re-testing (a player might try a small contribution later to see if cooperation can restart). I therefore predict: modest positive contributions early, a quick reward for any free-riding player, subsequent reductions in contributions by others, and a drift toward zero contributions for most of the remaining rounds, with occasional small experiments.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their endowment of 20. The sum is doubled and split evenly among the three players, so each player's round payoff = (20 − own contribution) + (2 * total contributions) / 3.

Key incentives and likely dynamics
- Individual incentive: contributing one coin costs you 1 private coin but returns only 2/3 of a coin to you personally (the marginal per-capita return, MPCR = 2/3 < 1). Thus, selfish best response in a one-shot game is to contribute 0.
- Collective benefit: the group is better off if everyone contributes. Full contribution would produce higher total group payoff.
- Repetition and conditional behavior: in repeated play, conditional cooperators may start by contributing moderately; if they see others contribute, they maintain cooperation; if they see free-riding, they reciprocate by reducing contributions.
- Uncertainty and information: players do not observe others' current-round choices before deciding, but after each round they see each player's net payoff for that round (so they can infer, with some algebra, what others likely contributed). No communication is allowed.
- Finite horizon: because the game has a known end (16 rounds), backward induction predicts unraveling toward zero contributions by the final rounds; in practice, contributions often decline over time as cooperation breaks down.

Prediction summary
- Expect modest cooperation early (players try contributing a nonzero amount).
- If one or more players free-ride, conditional cooperators will reduce contributions in subsequent rounds.
- Over several rounds contributions will trend downward, converging to zero well before the last round; by the end most players contribute 0.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), 16 rounds, and a constant end date. Each round everyone privately chooses an integer 0–20 to contribute. The pot (sum of contributions) is doubled and split equally. The private incentive is to contribute 0 (you keep all your 20 minus contribution), while the group incentive is for everyone to contribute more because the multiplied pot returns to everyone.

Typical dynamics and incentives to expect:
- Early rounds often start with some cooperation (positive contributions) because players hope others will reciprocate; conditional cooperators match observed contributions.
- Free-riding is tempting: contributing 0 while others contribute gives a higher individual payoff that round, so if players notice others reducing contributions, they often reduce too.
- Because players see peers' round payoffs and contributions after each round, behavior tends to drift downward over repeated rounds (reciprocal punishment or opportunistic free-riding), especially as the horizon approaches (end-game effect).
- Uncertainties: individual social preferences vary (some stick with cooperation; some exploit), so transient recoveries or fluctuations are possible. With three players, one defector can meaningfully reduce group returns; responses depend on how each player interprets others' motives.

Given these forces, a plausible path is initial moderate cooperation, followed by gradual decline as players test and then follow lower contributions, ending in widespread free-riding by the last rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 16 anonymous rounds. Each round each player has 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20. Contributions are pooled, doubled, and split equally among the three players. The marginal private return on one coin contributed is 2/3 of a coin (you get back 2/3 of the coin via the pot) while the private cost is 1 coin, so the purely selfish dominant action is to contribute 0. However, contributions increase group payoffs: if everyone contributes, all are better off collectively.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation due to uncertainty, social preferences, or conditional cooperation (players willing to contribute if others do). Over time, unless strong norms or enforcement exist, cooperation often decays because free-riders get a higher private payoff, prompting conditional cooperators to reduce contributions. Here players see only per-round net payoffs (their own and peers') at the summary stage—not the raw contribution choices—so they can infer aggregate generosity only indirectly. That creates uncertainty and can slow down or accelerate decline depending on how much players infer about who is free-riding.

Given avatars and likely tendencies: SLOTH is cautious/low contributor (slow to cooperate), GORILLA starts relatively cooperative and reduces contributions if others appear to free-ride, DUCK starts cooperatively but drifts down as the game proceeds. I predict an initially positive contribution level across the group that gradually declines toward zero by the late rounds as free-riding incentives dominate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 3-player repeated public goods game played for 16 rounds. Each round players privately choose how many of 20 coins to contribute; the sum is doubled and split equally. The one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0 (free-ride) because any contributed coin returns only 2/3 of its value to the contributor. However, group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. In practice, with repeated interaction and feedback after each round, we typically see some initial cooperation (at least by more pro-social players), followed by decline as free-riders earn higher individual payoffs and others respond by lowering contributions. Conditional cooperators reduce their contributions when they observe others free-riding; persistent free-riders keep contributing little. Uncertainty comes from not seeing others' current choices before choosing, so players react based on past summaries. Because players cannot send messages, coordination or punishment must happen through contribution choices only. I predict an initial mixed-cooperation phase, then gradual unwinding toward low contributions, and eventual convergence near full free-riding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game played anonymously for a known finite horizon (16 rounds). The social optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20 coins each round (the pot is doubled and split), because the multiplied public pot benefits everyone. However, each player's private marginal return on contributing one coin is 2/3 of a coin (the pot is doubled, then divided among three players), which is less than 1 — so from a pure one-shot, self-interested perspective, the dominant choice is to contribute 0.

Typical dynamics combine these forces: early rounds often show some cooperation (players test each other or start cooperatively), while free-riding incentives and observation of others' behavior lead to gradual decline in contributions. Because players see round-by-round summaries (they can infer peers' contributions from net payoffs), conditional cooperators will reduce their contributions in response to perceived free-riding. In a finite 16-round game, backward induction predicts unraveling toward zero contributions near the end; therefore we expect cooperation to erode over the middle rounds and reach zero before the final rounds, with some players heading to zero faster than others.

Predicted roles:
- SLOTH: initially cooperative but slow to punish; reduces contributions gradually as others free-ride.
- GORILLA: more self-interested; cuts contributions earlier and more steeply.
- DUCK: conditional cooperator, adjusts to the observed group average and gradually reduces as cooperation declines.

Below is a round-by-round transcript with short explanations and the predicted contributions and per-player net payoffs at the summary stage.

game_explanation: This is a standard repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; total contributions are multiplied by 2 and split equally. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) from contributing one coin is 2/3, which is less than 1: individually it is optimal to keep coins, but the group is better off if everyone contributes. Because punishment is costly (1 coin to punish, 2 coins deducted from the target per punishment unit), players may use it sparingly to discipline low contributors and sustain cooperation. With a known 16-round horizon, there is an end-game incentive to defect: rational backward induction predicts declining contributions toward the final rounds. In practice, many groups show moderate-to-high cooperation early, occasional low contributions that are met with small punitive responses, and a gradual fall in contributions as the horizon approaches. Uncertainty about others' types (reciprocal/punisher vs opportunist) and the fact that punishers are anonymous (targets cannot identify who punished them) means punishment signals are noisy; this tends to reduce retaliatory cycles, so punishments are typically limited and targeted when free-riding is clear.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with a multiplication factor of 2 applied to the sum of contributions and then split equally among the three players. Individually it is always optimal to contribute 0, but groups benefit if everyone contributes. Because punishment is available (cost 1 to sender, -2 to target), it can be used to deter low contributions and sustain higher cooperation, though punishment is costly and typically used sparingly. Early rounds are characterized by uncertainty about others' types (cooperative vs. free-riding); conditional cooperators will raise contributions if peers do so, while persistent low contributors may be disciplined with a small amount of punishment. Punishment use is typically low and targeted (small units, few rounds). Toward the final round players may reduce contributions because future retaliation is less effective (backward induction), so we often see a drop in contributions at the end.

Below I simulate a plausible play path over 16 rounds. For each round I explain the expected reasoning, show contributions in the fixed avatar order (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), list any punishment tuples (source, target, units), and then show the summary information that players would see: each player's contribution, how much they spent punishing others, how much was deducted from them by punishment, and their net payoff for the round. Net payoff = 20 - contribution + (2 * total contributions / 3) - (amount deducted from punishment) - (amount spent punishing). I assume shares can be fractional in principle, but I choose contributions such that the redistributed share is an integer.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to put in a common pot; the pot is doubled and split equally. Punishment is available and costly: each punishment unit costs 1 to the punisher and reduces the target by 2. Because contributions are private before choices, players face uncertainty about others' current actions but can condition responses on past behavior via punishment. Typical dynamics: moderate cooperation early, occasional deviations (experimentation or free-riding), and targeted, relatively sparse punishment of clear low contributors to enforce cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher; near the final rounds contributions often decline (“endgame”), but prior punishment behavior can partially sustain cooperation. I therefore predict mostly moderate-to-high contributions around 10–12 with intermittent low contributions met by small punishments; punishment events will be limited and small in magnitude.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), 16 rounds, fixed end point, and a costly punishment stage. The dominant short-term incentive each round is to free-ride (contribute 0) because contributions are privately costly; however, because contributions are multiplied by 2 and split equally, joint payoffs rise with cooperation. Punishment allows players to impose costs on low contributors, at a cost to themselves, which can sustain cooperation early on but tends to be used sparingly because it is costly and noisy (players do not see who punished them). Typical dynamics: some initial cooperation (or at least moderate contributions) supported by occasional targeted punishments by conditional cooperators (who punish low contributors). Over time, contributions often decline as forward-looking incentives to maintain cooperation weaken (especially in later rounds as the end approaches). Punishment frequency usually falls in later rounds: either because players stop investing in enforcement when the game's end is near or because the social norm has already collapsed. Given the avatars, I predict GORILLA will act as the main enforcer (punishing low contributors occasionally), SLOTH will be a steady moderate contributor who rarely punishes, and DUCK will start more selfish and respond to punishment by increasing contributions sometimes, but ultimately drift down toward free-riding as the end nears. Punishments will be sparse and low-intensity (mostly 1 unit each event).

game_explanation:
This three-player repeated public goods game combines a clear collective benefit from high contributions with a direct private incentive to free-ride. Each round a player who contributes more raises total group payoff (the pot is doubled and split equally), so mutual cooperation is payoff-enhancing if sustained. However, any single player can increase their own round payoff by contributing less than others, since they keep their private holding while still receiving a share of the multiplied pot. That creates temptation to defect each round.

Punishment is available and can deter free-riding: one punishment unit costs 1 coin to the punisher and removes 2 coins from the punished player's round payoff. Because punishment itself is costly and its effect is distributed only on the punished target, players tend to punish sparingly in real games—often only when defection is clear, repeated, or norm-violating relative to recent behavior. Uncertainty about others' motives and the fact that punishers are anonymous (players don't see who punished them) add friction: punishers bear private costs, and punished players only see a deduction, not the identity of the punisher, which blunts retaliation chains.

We therefore expect an initial phase of tentative cooperation: moderate-to-high contributions as players test one another. Occasional low contributions will be met with limited punishment from at least one other player (not everyone punishes every defection). After punishment, cooperation often rebounds, because the punished player updates beliefs about enforcement. As the finite horizon (16 rounds) becomes salient, contributions tend to drift downwards—especially in the last few rounds—because the incentive to maintain a reputation declines. Punishment also becomes rarer near the end since punishment costs are less likely to yield future enforcement benefits.

Below I simulate a plausible 16-round trajectory consistent with these incentives: early cooperation, sparse punishment targeted at clear low contributions, some recovery, and gradual endgame unraveling with a low-contribution final round. Each round shows contributions in the order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK and lists only non-zero punishment actions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20; the sum is doubled and split equally. Individually, each coin contributed yields 2/3 coin back to the contributor (shared benefit) but costs 1 coin privately, so the short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Contributing all is group-optimal but individually dominated. Punishment is available after contributions: it is costly for the punisher (1 coin per unit) and harsher for the target (2 coins lost per unit). Because punishment is costly, real players punish sparingly and mainly to deter repeated low contributions or perceived defection.

Typical unfolding: early rounds often start with moderate-to-high contributions as players test cooperation; some players may experiment by reducing contributions, triggering targeted, small punishments from cooperators. Punishment tends to be low-intensity and infrequent. Over middle rounds cooperation can stabilize at intermediate levels if punishments efficiently deter free-riding. As the horizon approaches, backward induction and uncertainty lead many players to reduce contributions (end-game decline), so contributions usually fall in later rounds and punishments taper off. Uncertainties include whether punishers are willing to bear short-term costs to enforce cooperation, who will experiment or defect, and whether retaliation cycles arise (which real players avoid because punishment is costly).

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game among three anonymous players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) is likely to show the usual tension between selfish incentives and group benefits. Each round players can keep up to 20 coins or contribute some to a common pot that pays back less than the full contribution to the individual but benefits the group (pot doubled, split three ways). Absent repetition, contributing 0 is dominant; with repetition, conditional cooperation and the threat of costly punishment can sustain higher contributions.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early uncertainty: some players will try moderate contributions to test others; others may free-ride immediately.
- If free-riding is visible, conditional cooperators often respond with targeted, sparse punishment to discourage it. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it’s used only occasionally.
- If punishment successfully disciplines defectors, average contributions rise and stabilize in mid-game.
- Near the end of the 16-round horizon, contributions tend to fall as future retaliation becomes less effective, so we expect some end-game decline, especially in round 16.

Punishment appears sparsely to discipline persistent low contributors; cooperation improves and stabilizes mid-game. Some short-lived defections trigger targeted punishment. In the final round, anticipating the end, players reduce contributions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 3-player public goods game played for 16 rounds. Each round every player gets 20 coins and simultaneously chooses an integer contribution between 0 and 20 to put into a group pot. The pot is doubled (multiplied by 2) and then split equally among the three players. That means each coin you contribute returns 2/3 of a coin to you (because 1 * 2 / 3 = 2/3) — so the individually rational move is to contribute 0, while the group optimum is to contribute all 20. After contributions are revealed, players can pay to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the punished player. Punishment can sustain cooperation by making free-riding costly, but punishment itself is costly and anonymous (targets don't know which player punished them), which creates uncertainty about retaliation and responsibility.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation (mid-range contributions) as players experiment and try to establish norms. Some low contributions can trigger targeted punishment by others trying to enforce cooperation. Because punishment is costly, it tends to be used sparingly — small units to signal disapproval. Over many rounds, contributions commonly drift downward (especially as the horizon end approaches) unless punishment effectively sustains cooperation. In late rounds, backward induction makes free-riding more attractive and punishments less effective, so contributions often decline further. Because punishments are anonymous but players see how much others spent on punishment overall, players may infer who enforces norms and retaliate or adjust behavior in later rounds.

game_explanation: In this public goods game, each player starts each round with 20 coins that can either be kept privately (withdrawn) or left in the shared pot as a contribution. The pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (private gain) while group payoff favors contributing. Because decisions are simultaneous, early rounds typically show moderate contributions as players test one another. The optional reward stage lets players spend coins to give direct transfers to others; this can sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors or punishing free-riders indirectly (at a cost). In practice, rewards are used sparingly — small, targeted transfers — because they themselves are costly. Over repeated rounds, one often sees initial cooperation or experimentation, some reciprocal rewarding of cooperators, and a gradual decline (end-game effect) as the horizon approaches, unless rewards successfully stabilize contributions. Expect targeted 1- or 2-unit rewards mostly to reciprocate or to signal appreciation for steady contributors; large or frequent reward flows would be atypical.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with four players, 10 rounds, and a reward stage after each redistribution. Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to withdraw (private) versus leave in the public pot (contribution). The total contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so individual incentives push toward withdrawing everything (to free-ride), while group benefit favors contributing. Rewards allow players to transfer coins at a unit cost of 1 to reward others (1 coin sent becomes +1 to the recipient), which can be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation or to signal disapproval.

Likely dynamics: initial uncertainty leads to moderate contributions as players test others (many subjects start around half the endowment). Conditional cooperators will raise contributions if they see others reciprocate; free-riders will lower theirs. Because reward is costly, it will be used selectively—mainly to reward consistently high contributors or to reciprocate favorable treatment. Over repeated rounds, cooperation can be sustained if rewards are used to reinforce contributors; however, with a finite 10-round horizon we expect some end-game decline (participants reduce contributions as the last round approaches), and reward use typically falls in the final rounds. Uncertainty about peers' types in early rounds leads to cautious play; information from the round summaries (who rewarded whom and net payoffs) will shape subsequent choices.

The private return on each contributed coin is 2.1/4 = 0.525, so purely selfish play favors withdrawing everything. However, repeated interaction (10 rounds), observable contributions and rewarding, and social preferences can sustain positive contributions: cooperators can be supported by small positive rewards and social reciprocity, while free-riding tends to be met with omission of rewards rather than costly punishment. Because rewards cost the sender, they are used sparingly and mainly to recognize relatively high contributors or to reciprocate prior generosity. Uncertainty about others’ types and the finite horizon produce some variance: contributions often rise from an uncertain start as cooperative norms form, then drift down toward the end as end-game incentives kick in. Expect mostly modest contributions (not full cooperation), sparse small reward transfers (1–2 units), gradual conditioning on others’ behavior, and a drop in contributions and rewards in the final round.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game creates a tension between private incentives and group benefit. Each coin left in the public pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally across four players, so the marginal per-coin return to each player from contributing is 2.1/4 = 0.525 — less than 1. That means any individual acting purely selfishly prefers to withdraw coins (contribute 0). However, if several players contribute, everyone can benefit collectively; conditional cooperators may contribute in response to others. Because choices are simultaneous and private, early rounds are exploratory: some players test cooperation, others test free-riding. The presence of a costly reward stage lets players reward cooperators (or withhold rewards from defectors), which can help sustain higher contributions by creating social incentives. But rewards are only transfers (cost 1 to giver, +1 to receiver), so they cannot directly punish, only reward desirable behavior; they are typically used sparingly. Over repeated rounds, cooperation often starts moderate and either stabilizes (if rewards and reciprocity sustain it) or drifts down as selfish incentives and uncertainty drive contributions down. With a known finite horizon (10 rounds), we also expect some decline toward the end (end-game effect), since future retaliation or reward is less effective. Uncertainties include each player's social preferences, responsiveness to rewards, and tolerance for short-term loss to build longer-run cooperation.

Toward the last rounds, contributions usually fall (end‑game effect) because future punishment or reciprocity loses leverage.

Rewards will be infrequent and small (mostly single-unit gifts, occasionally a 2-unit thanks). By round 10 contributions will drop sharply (near-zero) and rewards will largely cease.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>
This public goods game pits private incentives against a group benefit. Each player starts with 20 coins; any coins not withdrawn become contributions to a public pot that is multiplied by 2.1 and shared equally among the four players. Because each coin you contribute yields only 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins back to you via the public pot, the individually rational move is to withdraw everything (contribute 0). The socially efficient outcome is full contribution (20 each), but that is fragile because of free-riding incentives.

Given the finite 10-round horizon, standard dynamics are:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others often produces mid-level contributions (conditional cooperation). Some players experiment with contributing more to test reciprocity.
- Middle rounds: if conditional cooperators see others contributing, cooperation may be sustained; otherwise, contributions drift down.
- End game: toward the last rounds, incentives to free-ride strengthen (backward induction), so contributions tend to fall.
- Rewards: because rewarding costs the sender 1 coin per unit to give 1 coin to a recipient, rewards can be used to reinforce cooperative behavior but are costly and thus typically used sparingly. Players tend to direct small positive rewards to higher contributors to encourage them; cross-rewards or reciprocal 1-unit exchanges are common early on. As the horizon approaches, rewards usually decrease or stop.

Uncertainty about others' types (some conditional cooperators, some free-riders) and the visibility of past contributions/rewards will shape behavior. Expect modest positive contributions from at least one or two players early; occasional 1-coin rewards directed at repeat cooperators; and a gradual decline in contributions and rewards toward the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public goods game with 4 players and a multiplier of 2.1 on the joint pot. Each coin one player contributes returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins to each player on average, so the private incentive is to withdraw (free-ride) while the group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. Because players do not see others' current-round choices before they act, the game is one of repeated uncertainty: early rounds are exploratory (testing others' tendencies), and later rounds often show a decline in contributions (an "endgame" effect) unless positive reinforcement (here: voluntary rewards that transfer coins to good contributors and are visible by source) sustains cooperation.

Expectations:
- Early rounds: moderate contributions as players test each other; a few small reward transfers are used to reinforce cooperative behavior (rewarders are selective and sparse).
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators maintain contributions if they observe others contribute and/or if they receive or witness rewards; free-riders get low or no rewards and may be ignored.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to drift down as the shadow of the future shrinks, and rewarding becomes rarer; a few players may still use small rewards to encourage contributors.
- Uncertainties: players differ in social preferences (some are conditional cooperators; some are persistent free-riders). Because rewards cost the giver, reward actions are used sparingly and targeted.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-round public goods game with a multiplication factor of 2.1 divided across 4 players (each contributed coin returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 to each player). Individually, withdrawing (keeping) a coin yields a private payoff of 1 immediately, while contributing a coin yields only 0.525 back to the contributor through the public pot — so the dominant single-round incentive is to withdraw everything (free-ride). However, repeated interaction and the ability to reward others can sustain higher contributions: players who value future rounds or fairness may contribute more, and others can use costly rewards to reinforce cooperation. Because rewards cost the sender 1 coin and give the receiver 1 coin, they are used sparingly in real games — typically to recognize cooperative behavior or (rarely) to reward attempts to sustain cooperation. Uncertainty about others' types and the finite 10-round horizon mean cooperation often starts moderate and then drifts downward as players anticipate end-game free-riding. Below I simulate a plausible trajectory: early conditional cooperation with occasional small rewards to encourage reciprocators, followed by partial unraveling toward the final rounds when incentives to free-ride become stronger.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 rounds. Each round each player effectively decides how much to contribute to the public pot (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so each player faces a private incentive to withdraw coins (free-ride) while the group benefits if people contribute. Because players see past contributions and can identify who punished or rewarded them, behavior is likely to be conditional: players will start somewhat cooperatively, then respond to low contributors with targeted punishment (costly but effective deterrent), and occasionally use small rewards to reinforce high contributors. Punishment and reward tend to be used sparingly in real experiments because punishments are costly and rewards are relatively weak per unit. Because the game has a known finite horizon (10 rounds), we also expect a gradual unraveling toward lower contributions as the end approaches (endgame effect), unless sustained enforcement (punishment/reward) keeps cooperation up. Overall expectation: moderate contributions early, a few targeted punishments to discipline persistent low contributors, some small rewards, midgame stability if enforcement works, and a decline toward the final rounds.

This public goods game pits individual incentives against collective benefit. Conditional cooperators (who increase contributions when others contributed well) are common: they respond to peers' past behavior. Because the game lasts 10 rounds, expect some endgame decline in cooperation (players reduce contributions as the final round approaches), and reduced use of punishment/reward in the final round since there are no future rounds to influence.

Taken together, I predict: an early mix of high and low contributions, a few isolated punishments directed at persistent low contributors, occasional small rewards for consistent cooperators, conditional adjustments by DUCK/CHICKEN, and a modest endgame drop in round 10.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 4 players and 10 rounds. Each round each player decides simultaneously how much of the 20 coin endowment to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The total contribution is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins to each individual. Because 0.525 < 1, any single player has an immediate private incentive to withdraw (free-ride) rather than contribute; but the group as a whole benefits if players contribute more. That creates the classic tension: social optimum calls for high contributions, individual incentive calls for lower contributions.

Punishments and rewards after redistribution allow players to sanction free-riders or reinforce cooperators. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin per unit) but reduces the target by 3 coins per unit, so it can be an effective deterrent even when used sparingly. Rewards cost 1 coin per unit and add 1 coin to the target; they are gentler and typically used sparingly to encourage cooperation. Because players cannot communicate and cannot see others' choices before acting, there is uncertainty about motives and intentions; players respond to observed contribution histories and to punishment/reward signals. Typical dynamics: moderate contributions early on, some free-riding emerges over time, occasional punishments or rewards stabilize cooperation for a while, and contributions often decline toward the end as the “last-round” effect reduces incentives to maintain cooperation.

With 10 rounds, expect (a) initial moderate cooperation, (b) a tendency for contributions to drift down across rounds unless enforcement (punishment/reward) is used, (c) relatively sparse punishment and reward actions (usually 0–2 non-zero edges per round), and (d) some endgame reduction in cooperation in the final rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits individual incentives (withdrawing coins for private use) against group incentives (leaving coins in the pot so everyone benefits after the 2.1 multiplier). Individually, any coin contributed returns only 0.525 to the contributor (2.1/4), so pure self-interest pushes players to withdraw (contribute 0). However, repeated interaction, the ability to punish (costly to sender, strongly harmful to target) and reward (cheaper, smaller benefit to target) introduce mechanisms that can sustain cooperation. Expect heterogeneous behavior: some players initially test cooperation, others start cooperative. Punishment will be used sparingly—mostly to discipline clear defectors—and rewards used occasionally to reinforce cooperators. Because the game is finite (10 rounds), there is uncertainty about whether players will condition their contributions on past behavior; punishment tends to decline near the last rounds because of the end-game incentive to free-ride. Overall, cooperation may rise after early sanctions and supportive rewards, but final-round free-riding is likely.

game_explanation:
This public goods game presents a classic social dilemma: each round every player starts with 20 coins and decides how many to withdraw (private use) so the remainder becomes their group contribution. Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so a player who contributes gives a benefit to the group but obtains less private return than the full cost of contribution. Incentives: selfish best response is to withdraw everything (contribute 0), but if everyone defects the group payoff is low. Uncertainties come from not seeing others' choices before acting and from how others will use punishment or reward.

Typical unfolding in such games: contributions start at a moderate level (some conditional cooperators contribute substantially while a few free-ride), and without strong enforcement contributions tend to drift down over rounds (end-game effect). The punishment/reward stage can sustain cooperation: punishments deter low contributions while rewards encourage high contributors. In practice punishments and rewards are used sparingly because they are costly to the sender; punishers usually act when they perceive clear free-riding, and rewards are occasional gestures to reinforce cooperation.

Given four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), a plausible trajectory is:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions; GORILLA and DUCK are more cooperative, SLOTH begins lower but may respond to sanctions, CHICKEN tends to free-ride.
- Mid-game: occasional punishments from GORILLA (as enforcer) and occasional rewards from DUCK/GORILLA reinforce contributors.
- End-game: a gradual decline in contributions as the horizon approaches, with fewer sanctions because of diminishing future gains.

Below is a round-by-round transcript that follows these tendencies, with sparse punishment and reward actions and a gradual decline in average contributions toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player can withdraw some of their 20 coins privately; the remainder is contributed to the group pot. The total contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. Because the marginal per-capita return (2.1/4 = 0.525) is less than 1, each player gains more privately by withdrawing a coin (keeps 1) than by contributing it (returns only 0.525), so the individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, the group optimum is full contribution (everyone contributes all 20), which yields much higher group payoffs.

Given simultaneous decisions, uncertainty about others' choices, and the finite 10-round horizon, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds often show moderate contributions as players test each other and some hope for cooperation.
- If free-riding appears, conditional cooperators reduce contributions over rounds.
- Punishment and reward are available but costly: punishment costs 1 to the punisher and subtracts 3 from the target; reward costs 1 and gives 1 to the target. Because punishment is costly and risky, players use it sparingly; rewards are also used but infrequently.
- With a finite horizon, cooperation tends to decline over time (endgame effect), especially if punishment does not sustain cooperation.
- Strategic differences may appear: one or two players try to sustain cooperation (higher contributions), others free-ride more. Small, targeted punishments can momentarily curb free-riding but rarely fully reverse a downward trend.

Below I simulate a plausible 10-round transcript consistent with those incentives: modest early cooperation, occasional small rewards for cooperators, occasional sparse punishments directed at persistent low contributors, and an overall gradual decline in contributions toward the end.

This is a repeated public goods game with four players over 10 rounds. Each round every player decides how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution) versus keep privately. The shared total is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly, so individual incentives push toward keeping money privately, while collective welfare is maximized by higher contributions. Because contributions are private at decision time, each player faces uncertainty about others' choices and may condition future behavior on observed contributions and on punishments/rewards they receive. Punishment (costly to impose, more costly to the target) enables enforcement of cooperative norms, while rewards can encourage contributors.

Punishment is costly to the punisher but can increase contributions in subsequent rounds. Rewards are used less frequently but can reinforce high contributors. Toward the final rounds, incentives to punish decline and many players reduce contributions (endgame effect).

I predict an overall trajectory where contributions start reasonably high, dip when a few players test free-riding, followed by some limited punishment that restores cooperation midgame. Toward rounds 9–10 contributions drift down as the end approaches and punishment loses deterrent power. Punishments and rewards will be infrequent and small (mostly single-unit actions).

game_explanation:
This is a finite (10-round) public goods game with 4 players. Each round every player effectively decides how much of a 20-coin endowment to leave in the public pot (their contribution) versus withdraw for private use. The group return on contributions (2.1× total contribution, split evenly) gives a lower private marginal return per coin (2.1/4 = 0.525) than the private return from withdrawing (1). That creates a classic social dilemma: the group does best if everyone contributes a lot, but each individual has an incentive to withdraw more (free-ride). Because the game is repeated but ends at round 10, we expect:

- Early rounds: modest-to-high contributions as players experiment and try to signal cooperativeness.
- Midgame: responses to observed low contributors — occasional punitive acts to discourage free-riding and occasional small rewards to reinforce high contributions. Punishments are costly and typically used sparingly; rewards are also rare but may appear to reinforce cooperation.
- Endgame: contributions typically decline as future enforcement value vanishes; punishment becomes less common because it is costly and has no future deterrent effect, though a small amount of final-round spite or expression punishments can occur.

Uncertainties include how sensitive players are to one another’s actions (how quickly punishments/rewards are used) and how much players shift contributions after being punished/rewarded. Below I simulate a plausible, conservative trajectory with sparse punishment and reward consistent with experimental tendencies.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each receive 20 coins per round. Each round they privately choose an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and divided equally, so collective contributions benefit everyone but each player individually faces an incentive to free-ride. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the source 3 coins and gives the target 2 coins. Messages can be sent at any time.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds are exploratory: players test others' cooperation levels. Cooperative players (who expect reciprocity or value group payoff) will contribute moderate-to-high amounts; defectors contribute little.
- Rewards provide a way to reinforce cooperation. Expect cooperators to reward fellow cooperators selectively and sparingly (1–2 units), while defectors are rarely rewarded.
- Rewarding is costly, so it will be used only when players expect meaningful impact—either to sustain cooperation or to signal approval.
- Over many rounds, patterns usually emerge: clusters of stable cooperators who reward each other and persistent low contributors who receive little reward. Some opportunistic players may increase contributions if rewarded; others stick to low contributions.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior tends to stabilize rather than collapsing abruptly at a known end.

reflection:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each receive 20 coins per round. Each round they privately choose an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and divided equally, so collective contributions benefit everyone but each player individually faces an incentive to free-ride. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the source 3 coins and gives the target 2 coins. Messages can be sent at any time.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds are exploratory: players test others' cooperation levels. Cooperative players (who expect reciprocity or value group payoff) will contribute moderate-to-high amounts; defectors contribute little.
- Rewards provide a way to reinforce cooperation. Expect cooperators to reward fellow cooperators selectively and sparingly (1–2 units), while defectors are rarely rewarded.
- Rewarding is costly, so it will be used only when players expect meaningful impact—either to sustain cooperation or to signal approval.
- Over many rounds, patterns usually emerge: clusters of stable cooperators who reward each other and persistent low contributors who receive little reward. Some opportunistic players may increase contributions if rewarded; others stick to low contributions.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior tends to stabilize rather than collapsing abruptly at a known end.

Below is a full round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 26, showing chat, contributions (in the fixed avatar order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), reward actions (only non-zero tuples), and a brief summary stage each round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with a fixed group (6 players), constant endowment (20 each round), a multiplication of the group pot (×3.8) shared equally, and a one-way costly reward instrument (cost 3 to giver, gives 2 to receiver per reward unit). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect moderate cooperation early on (people try cooperating to see others' responses), and conditional behavior: cooperators will sometimes use the reward instrument to reinforce high contributors, and freeriders will be rewarded little or not at all. Rewards are costly, so they will be used sparingly — mainly to sustain cooperation rather than to fully discipline defectors. Over time, some decline in average contributions is likely if a few players repeatedly free-ride, but occasional positive reinforcement can stabilize cooperation above the one-shot Nash level. Chat will be sparse and mostly to signal intentions or thank/reprimand others. Because players can identify who rewarded them, rewards will be targeted and informational.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each have 20 coins per round. Because the shared pot is multiplied by 3.8 and then split among 6 players, each coin you contribute returns only about 0.63 coins to you personally while benefiting the whole group. That creates a tension: the group does best if many contribute, but individually you usually gain by contributing less. In repeated play, typical dynamics are:
- Some players start cooperative and keep contributing a lot (they try to build the public good).
- Some players free-ride at low contributions. Others respond to free-riding by reducing their contributions unless there are incentives to maintain cooperation.
- Because only positive rewards are available (costly to give), players will tend to use small, targeted rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior rather than punish defection. Rewarding is costly, so it is used sparingly and typically directed at consistent contributors.
- Communication is allowed and can be used to coordinate cooperation or call out low contributors; in real games chat is usually sparse and focused on encouragement or brief calls for fairness.
- Over many rounds without a known end, cooperation often stabilizes at a moderate level sustained by selective rewarding and reciprocal norms; fatigue or perceived exploitation can cause gradual decline late in the sequence.

I will simulate a plausible path through 26 rounds. Expect a core of moderately high contributors (DUCK, GORILLA, DOG at times, PARROT as a social mimicker) who reward one another occasionally; SLOTH behaves like a persistent low contributor and gets largely excluded from rewards; CHICKEN fluctuates. Rewarding is sparse and targeted to sustain cooperation; chat is occasional and focused.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT) against the classic tension: each round everyone has 20 private coins and can contribute any integer 0–20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split evenly, so each coin you contribute returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63 to you — individually costly but collectively beneficial. That creates a temptation to free-ride (contribute little and still enjoy others' contributions) versus a social incentive to contribute more for the group's benefit.

Because players can reward others after seeing contributions, cooperation can be sustained: positive rewards signal appreciation and can tip incentives toward contributing. Rewards are expensive to the giver (cost 3 per unit) and smaller for the recipient (gain 2 per unit), so players will typically reward sparingly, directing scarce reward tokens to those they want to encourage. Communication is allowed but sparse; promises or short appeals may appear, but messages are cheap and unverifiable.

Uncertainty about the total number of rounds typically maintains higher cooperation early on, possibly declining if players suspect an imminent end. Expect some variability: a few steady contributors (leaders), occasional free-riding by DOG, intermittent modest monetary rewards to reinforce cooperation, and short, purposeful chat messages used to praise or nudge behavior. Rewards will be used mostly to thank high contributors or to reciprocate prior rewards, not to punish.

# GAME STARTS

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with voluntary contributions and costly rewards. Typical dynamics start with some exploratory behavior: some players test the waters with moderate contributions while others free-ride. Because contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and shared, group-level efficiency is maximized when everyone contributes more, but each individual's private incentive is to contribute less. Over rounds, players who prefer cooperation tend to establish a norm by signaling in chat and by using rewards to reinforce high contributors; conditional cooperators increase contributions in response, while persistent free-riders may either be ignored or, less commonly, targeted with withheld rewards. Rewarding is costly (3 coins per reward unit) so players use it sparingly and usually only to support consistent contributors or to incentivize recent increases. Uncertainty about the total number of rounds encourages gradual, rather than abrupt, changes; cooperation typically builds slowly if rewards are effective, with occasional slips and small punishments or compensatory rewards. Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests, thanks, short norms).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly rewards and an unknown horizon. Typical dynamics: players start cautiously cooperative (moderate contributions) because contributing benefits the group via the multiplier (3.8) but individual incentive is to free-ride. Because players can reward others (costly to the giver but beneficial to the recipient), rewards tend to be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior and to encourage return to cooperation after defections. Rewards are most effective when given to those who contributed more than the group average; withholding rewards is the main way to sanction free-riders.

Uncertainty about the number of rounds usually supports ongoing cooperation early on; occasional experiments by one player (testing free-riding) trigger targeted non-rewards or small positive rewards to restore cooperation. Chats are typically rare and used to coordinate or call out clear free-riding. Over many rounds we expect a generally cooperative pattern with occasional lapses by one or two players and small, targeted rewards primarily from a few players who act as informal “sustainers” of cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely show a mix of experimentation, reciprocity, and targeted rewarding. Early rounds typically feature moderate contributions as players test others' cooperativeness. Because contributing benefits the group but costs the individual (the MPCR is 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63), there's a temptation to free-ride; absent incentives contributions tend to drift down. Here, players can reward good contributors (costly to the sender, beneficial to the recipient), which supports cooperation if used selectively. Expect a few players to consistently contribute more; they will receive most rewards. A habitual free-rider may exploit the group, prompting occasional messages and targeted rewards to sustain contributors. Chat will be sparse: short strategic signals, praise, or mild calls to cooperate. Rewards will be used sparingly and mostly to reinforce high contributors rather than to punish low ones.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, a contribution stage and a voluntary positive-reward stage. The group multiplier (3.8) makes the group benefit of contributions larger than individual cost (so full contribution raises group payoffs) but the benefit to any single contributor is diluted across the six players, so each player still has an individual incentive to free-ride. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect modest initial cooperation, occasional experiments/defections, and sparse use of rewards to reinforce contributors (not to punish). Rewards are costly to give (3 coins) and only transfer 2 coins to the recipient, so they will be used sparingly and mainly to signal appreciation or to try to sustain cooperation when defection is observed. Chat will be uncommon and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short complaints, or thanks). Over many anonymous rounds we typically see: early moderate cooperation, a few defections or experiments, small reward gestures targeted at cooperators, and some drift in average contributions as players test and respond to others.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each get 20 coins each round. Each round players decide privately how many coins (0–20) to contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players may reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Players can also chat at any time. They do not know how many rounds remain, so they tend to experiment early, try conditional cooperation, and use rewards sparingly to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate kindness. Expect:
- Early rounds: exploration — mixed contributions and a few thank-you rewards for clear cooperators.
- Middle rounds: more coordination — higher, steadier contributions supported by occasional rewards for consistent contributors.
- Occasional free-riding (very low contribution) will appear; others typically withhold rewards from blatant free-riders and sometimes reward those who compensate.
- Chat is sparse and strategic (e.g., short commitments or brief explanations).
- Rewards are infrequent and small (1–2 units) and targeted at those who contributed more, or given in reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public goods setting where each coin contributed benefits the group (3.8×) but returns less than 1× to the individual (3.8/6 ≈ 0.63). That means the private incentive is to keep coins (free-ride), while the group optimum is for everyone to contribute. Because punishment (costly to giver, large cost to receiver) and reward (costly to giver, small benefit to receiver) are available, we expect a mix: early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players "test" each other; when free-riding appears some players will occasionally punish to discourage it; occasional small rewards will be used to reinforce cooperators. Punishment will be used sparingly (because it is costly) and typically targeted at repeat low contributors. Rewards will also be infrequent but used to signal approval. Contributions will likely drift down from initial cooperation unless sanctions sustain them; with limited and uncertain sanctions, we expect fluctuations rather than a monotonic trend. Chat will be sparse and mainly used to call out behavior or thank/plead.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group incentive (contribute to the pot which gets multiplied by 3.8 and shared). Expect moderate cooperation early as players "test the waters" (contributing small-to-moderate amounts), occasional free-riding by players who try to keep their endowment, and a few targeted sanctions (punishments) by players who dislike free-riding. Rewards will be used even more sparingly than punishment — typically to signal approval of cooperators or of those who punish defectors.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we should see relatively stable reciprocal dynamics rather than abrupt end-game collapse. Punishments will be rare (1 unit or so) because they are costly to the punisher but can strongly reduce a target’s payoff; rewards will also be small (1 unit) and used occasionally to reinforce cooperative norms. Chats will be sparse and-purposeful: calling out free-riders, short appeals for cooperation, or brief acknowledgements.

I will simulate 26 rounds. Contributions are integers from 0 to 20 in the order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT. Punishment/reward tuples use (source, target, unit) with positive = reward, negative = punishment. Summaries show each player's net payoff (after sharing and all punish/reward adjustments), how much they spent on punishment and reward, how much was deducted from them by punishment, and how much they received from rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game among six players. Each round players decide how much of their private 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and divided equally. After seeing contributions, players may spend coins to punish (cost 3 per punishment unit, deducts 12 per unit from the target) or reward (cost 3 per reward unit, gives 2 per unit to the target) others. Players see who punished/rewarded them and a round summary with their net payoff and the amounts used/received.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often feature mixed contributions as players test each other. Conditional cooperators (who match others) will try moderate-to-high contributions; free riders will contribute little.
- Because players can identify punishers, a few targeted punishments early on will tend to raise the contributions of frequent low contributors. Punishment is usually sparse (costly to the punisher), so expect only a handful of punishments across the game, typically directed at repeat low contributors.
- Rewards are also used sparingly to encourage cooperators; they cost the source but give smaller benefits to the recipient than punishment inflicts.
- Over many anonymous rounds with no known end, cooperation often drifts downward unless sustained by sanctions. Expect a modest increase in cooperation after early sanctions, then a gradual decline as players discount future enforcement — with occasional rewards and isolated punishments keeping cooperation from collapsing completely.
- Chat messages are rare but may include brief appeals to cooperate, short acknowledgements, or short protests after punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with punishment and reward. Each round, players choose how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and shared equally. Individually, every contributed coin yields less private return than its cost, so free-riding (contributing little or nothing) is the short-run selfish choice. But because contributions benefit the group, conditional cooperation often emerges: many players respond to high contributions by reciprocating, and to low contributions by punishing or withholding rewards.

Expectations about how this particular session unfolds:
- Early rounds typically show heterogeneous contributions: some players test cooperation, some free-ride.
- A few players (enforcers) will occasionally use punishment to discipline persistent free-riders; punishments are costly to the punisher and harsh on the target, so they are used sparingly.
- Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce high contributors, but are slightly costly and less commonly used than punishment in this setting.
- Conditional cooperators will adjust contribution levels based on peers' behavior and on identified punishments/rewards in summaries.
- Over many rounds, cooperation tends to increase if punishments and rewards credibly signal expectations, but occasional lapses occur and provoke further targeted sanctions.
- Players do not know the total number of rounds, so endgame unwinding is less pronounced; occasional defections may still appear late, prompting last-minute sanctions.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>
