game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each coin a player contributes costs that player 1 coin immediately, but every contributed coin is multiplied by 8.1 and then divided equally among all 13 players. The per-player return from somebody's single contributed coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — less than 1 — so each individual has a strict incentive to free-ride (keep coins) even though the group as a whole is best off when everyone contributes. Uncertainty and limited feedback make coordination harder: players do not see others' choices before deciding, and after the round they only see their own net payoff (not others' contributions or payoffs), so conditional reciprocity is blunt and slow.

I therefore predict heterogeneous behavior: some players start cooperative (high contributions from social types), some start low (free-riders), and many will slowly reduce contributions as they realize they personally benefit more from keeping coins than from contributing. Because feedback is private (each sees only their own net payoff), patterns of decline will be gradual and not perfectly synchronized. Near the final rounds the classic "end-game effect" should appear: many players switch to near-zero contributions because there is no longer a future to sustain cooperation. The transcript below simulates one plausible path consistent with these incentives and uncertainties: moderately high initial contributions, gradual decline through the middle rounds, and collapse to defection by the final round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game (16 rounds) pits private incentives (keep 1 coin for yourself) against group incentives (each coin contributed is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally). With 13 players the marginal per-player return from one coin contributed is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, so contributing reduces a player's private holdings by 1 coin while giving ≈0.623 coins back via the public share — a net private loss but a net social gain. Likely dynamics: a mix of persistent cooperators (who keep contributing despite private cost), conditional cooperators (who adjust based on perceived group return inferred from their own payoff), and free-riders (who contribute little or nothing). Because players only see their own net payoff each round (not others' individual contributions), learning and inference are noisy: players deduce group behavior from their payoffs and adjust slowly. Early rounds tend to show some optimism and moderate-to-high contributions from cooperators; free riders exploit that, causing conditional cooperators to reduce contributions over middle rounds. Toward the final rounds, backward induction and endgame effects push many players to defect, producing a collapse in contributions by rounds 15–16. Occasional noisy or idiosyncratic moves (random or stubborn high contributions) can appear, but the general trend is: moderate cooperation → exploitation → decline → near-zero at the end.

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players. Because each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to the contributor in payoff, the private incentive is to free-ride (not contribute), while the group incentive is to contribute (total multiplier 8.1 > 1). Players learn only their own round net payoff at the summary stage, but because they know their own contribution, that net payoff reveals the total group contribution that round (they can infer the aggregate, not individual choices).

Typical likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some positive contributions as players test each other and conditional cooperators contribute when aggregate contributions appear healthy.
- Middle rounds: gradual decline as free-riding pays off for individuals and conditional cooperators reduce contributions in response to lower aggregate contributions.
- End-game: contributions typically collapse toward zero as strategic incentives to free-ride in final rounds dominate and there is little prospect of future reciprocity.
- There is uncertainty about player types (some may be consistent cooperators, some stubborn defectors, and some conditional cooperators) and occasional small random deviations (errors) are possible. Because players can infer only the aggregate each round, responses will be to that aggregate rather than to any individual’s behavior.

Feedback remains limited (only own payoff), and seeing good private returns from low contributions encourages further trimming of contributions. Expect further decline but still some cooperative attempts.
Individual incentives to free-ride and ambiguous feedback push many toward lower contributions. Expect a noticeable drop from early rounds; a few players still contribute moderately.
Seeing that lower contributors often had better net payoffs, more players trim contributions; with only private feedback, coordination on higher contributions is unlikely. Expect further decline.
With repeated evidence that keeping coins tends to raise personal payoff, players steadily reduce contributions. Expect many small contributions and a growing number of zeros.

Contributions continue to fall. Norms of cooperation erode because players cannot reliably identify cooperators and low contributors are repeatedly rewarded. Expect average contributions near the low single digits.
Mid-game decline continues. Many players settle on near-zero contributions; a few still give small amounts but overall the public pot shrinks.
As patterns of low contributions solidify and the end-game approaches, many players choose zero or near-zero contributions. Expect the public pot to be small.
With only six rounds left and predominant defection visible in personal payoffs, most players give 0–2 coins. Expect the public good return to be low.
Now well into the late game, cooperation has largely collapsed. A few players still contribute 1 occasionally, but most contribute 0.
Very late in the game: almost all players choose 0, with perhaps one or two small one-off contributions. Expect the public pot to be negligible.
Near universal defection is expected; with very little to gain from contributing and the end approaching, almost everyone contributes 0.
The same pattern continues: contributions are zero as the dominant strategy is clear and there is no future to sustain cooperation.
With only one round left after this, nearly everyone defects; expectations are for all-zero contributions again.
Final round: theoretically still a one-shot decision, so the dominant action is 0. Occasionally someone may make a small gesture, but most players defect in the last round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game played for a known 16 rounds. Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their endowment. Contributions are added to a group pot, multiplied by 8.1, and then split equally across the 13 players. Because the per-coin return to the group (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623) is less than 1, an individual who cares only about that round's payoff maximizes their private return by contributing 0 (keeping the full coin), while the group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes everything (20).

Given those incentives and the information structure (players do not see others' contributions before choosing, and after each round they only see their own net payoff), typical likely dynamics are:
- Early rounds: some optimism or conditional cooperation leads many players to contribute moderately-high amounts (hoping others will too). High shared returns on average reinforce this.
- Midgame: some players begin to free-ride after observing their own payoff (which can signal that others contributed), but because each player only sees their own payoff, inference about others is noisy and responses are heterogeneous. This produces a gradual decline in average contributions as strategic (and opportunistic) players exploit cooperators.
- Late rounds and endgame: with known finite horizon and learning from payoffs, most players head toward defection (contributions near 0), because future reciprocity cannot be enforced in later rounds. Expect near-zero contributions in the final several rounds.
- Uncertainties remain: with only private payoff feedback there is room for variation—some players persist in contributing at moderate levels (e.g., conditional cooperators who trust the group), while others switch to 0 swiftly. Aggregate behavior will therefore exhibit a downward trend but with heterogeneity.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript consistent with these incentives and information constraints. Each round includes a brief explanation of why the round plays out as shown, the predicted contributions in the exact avatar order, and then the summary stage (each player sees only their own round payoff).

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start without history. Expect initial optimism and some conditional cooperators to contribute moderately high amounts (10–16). A few cautious or opportunistic players contribute less. Because nobody can see others' choices beforehand, contributions are decentralized and heterogeneous.

After a highly rewarding first round, many stay cooperative but a few trim contributions to test free-riding. Because players only observe their own payoff, those who earned relatively more by contributing less may reduce further.
Some players respond to signals from their own payoffs by lowering contributions more sharply. Conditional cooperators still contribute but average contributions decline noticeably.

The downward trend continues as more players experiment with lower contributions and some opportunistic players reduce contributions further. Conditional cooperators still contribute but less than before.
Some players try to maintain mid-level cooperation; others continue trimming contributions. Private payoff feedback still muddles exact beliefs about others' actions, so responses vary.
Free-riding increases as players strategically trim contributions to keep more private coins. Group return per contributed coin remains below 1 for each individual, so continued decline is expected.

The downward drift continues. A cluster of players contributes small positive amounts (conditional cooperators or late reducers), while some set contributions to 0. Payoff feedback gives mixed signals; some who give 0 get relatively high net payoffs and will be reinforced to keep defecting.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 13-player repeated public goods game lasting 16 rounds. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to put into a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 active players. Because the per-coin return to the group from one contribution is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, each player who contributes loses 1 coin privately but only recoups about 0.623 from the pot; the private incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride). However, if many players contribute, everyone benefits collectively. Players do not see other players’ choices before deciding in a round, but at the summary stage they see their own net payoff for that round, which allows them to infer the total contribution (given their own choice). Uncertainty about others’ motives and heterogeneity in types (some conditional cooperators, some persistent cooperators, some free-riders) typically produces mixed behavior: modest cooperation early on, gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding spreads or as conditional cooperators react to inferred low totals, and an end-game “unraveling” toward lower contributions in the final rounds. I therefore predict a pattern with moderate average contributions in early rounds, a steady decline as players learn and respond, and near-zero contributions by the final rounds, with a few players remaining more generous throughout and a few free-riders contributing zero from the start.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players enter round 1 without prior round payoff information. Many start with a fair level of cooperation (testing others), but some distrustful players or selfish types free-ride immediately. Expect heterogeneous but overall positive contributions.

After seeing their own net payoff from round 1, players can infer the total contribution and begin adjusting. Conditional cooperators slightly reduce contributions if they infer others were not fully cooperative; persistent cooperators remain relatively high, free-riders stay low.
Learning continues. The overall group contribution drifts down as some players respond to perceived free-riding. A few players still contribute at decently high levels, but most trim contributions.

The downward trend accelerates: conditional cooperators reduce further in response to inferred totals; free-riders persist at zero. Some players try modest contributions to see if cooperation can be revived, but overall decline continues.

Middle rounds: players start punishing perceived free-riding (by cutting contributions), and reciprocation motives weaken. A core of contributors still gives moderate amounts, but totals fall noticeably.

As players repeatedly observe their own payoffs consistent with lower group sums, cooperation becomes harder to sustain. Some who contributed earlier cut further; a few keep moderate giving out of preference or habit.
The trend toward defection is clearer: many contribute only a little or nothing. A handful of players still make small positive contributions hoping to sustain cooperation, but aggregate totals drop.

By round 8 the strategic incentive to free-ride and the observed low totals push most players to minimal contributions. Some players experiment with tiny contributions; overall the pot is much smaller.
Late-stage decline continues. A few moderately cooperative players still contribute small amounts, but many players have converged to zero or near-zero contributions.

End-game considerations become salient: players anticipate low future returns from contributing and reduce contributions further. Expect mostly zeros and ones, with a small minority still contributing small positive amounts.
As the final rounds approach, further unraveling occurs. Some players hold to a small cooperative stance, but most contribute very little or nothing, expecting others to defect as well.
Contributions are now largely collapsed. A couple of players make token contributions out of intrinsic preference or habit, but group cooperation is nearly extinguished.
With only a few rounds left, most players have fully switched to free-riding. A single player may still give a lone coin, but aggregate is essentially zero.
By round 14, end-game logic dominates. Almost all players choose 0, anticipating no future reciprocation and maximizing private payoff.
Near the very end, cooperation is fully unraveled. Everyone contributes 0 as the standard selfish equilibrium is reached and there is little reason to deviate.
Final round: no future rounds to sustain reciprocity, so all players contribute 0. The game ends with full free-riding as predicted by backward induction in finite repeated games.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to the group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so contributions benefit everyone but are individually costly. That creates the classic tension: full cooperation maximizes group return, but each player has an incentive to free-ride (contribute less) and still enjoy others' contributions.

Because the game runs for a known finite horizon (16 rounds), players face both strategic uncertainty about others' types (cooperators, conditional cooperators, or free-riders) and temporal incentives (early rounds reward cooperation more, late rounds invite defection). Peer punishment can deter free-riding: punishers pay 3 coins per punishment unit to reduce a target's payoff by 7 coins per unit. Punishment is costly, so it tends to be used sparingly and mostly against clear or repeated free-riders. Conditional cooperators will respond to signals (punishment or observed contributions) by increasing or decreasing their contributions. Punishment is most effective early- to mid-game to sustain cooperation; in the final rounds, costly punishment is less attractive because there are fewer future rounds to deter, so cooperation often unravels.

Given standard behavioral regularities, I expect:
- A mix of steady cooperators (high contributors) and a few consistent low contributors.
- Sparse, targeted punishment aimed at obvious free-riders (a few players repeatedly punished).
- Conditional cooperators will adjust upward after being punished or after observing punishment, restoring some cooperation.
- Toward the final rounds contributions will decline and punishment will taper off (endgame unraveling).
- Punishment events are rare and targeted rather than large, because punishers avoid the heavy costs of frequent punishment.

Stable cooperators keep the group pot healthy; those who were punished earlier mostly contribute more. A few players revert to lower contributions causing a few small sanctions.
Players have substantial history; conditional cooperators tend to mirror the average. Punishment is used but restrained — punishers weigh the cost vs. benefit since the game is nearer its end.

Approaching the last third of rounds: contributions hover around moderate-high for cooperators, while some marginal players reduce. Punishment is rarer as players weigh its reduced future benefits.

Final round: almost pure self-interest dominates. Punishment is very unlikely because there is no future to influence; most players contribute little or nothing.

game_explanation:
This is a finite 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. The marginal per capita return (MPCR) is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, which is less than 1, so contributing is privately costly (best individual response is to contribute 0). However, because the group return from others' contributions exceeds the private cost, groups can achieve much higher joint payoffs if many contribute. The availability of costly punishment (3 coins per punishment unit for the punisher; 7 coins deducted per unit from the punished) creates a mechanism to enforce cooperation: some players may punish low contributors to discourage free-riding.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test others' behavior; a few low contributions provoke targeted, sparse punishment by those who value cooperation, which in turn can induce higher contributions. If punishment successfully deters persistent free-riders, contributions can stay elevated through the middle rounds. Because the game has a known finite horizon (16 rounds), cooperation is fragile toward the end: the incentive to defect grows as the shadow of future punishment fades, so contributions typically decline in late rounds and punishment becomes less frequent (punishment is costly and less effective if there is no future to enforce cooperation). There is also uncertainty about who will enforce norms; punishers tend to act sparingly, focusing on clear outliers rather than everyone who contributes below the group mean.

Cooperation trends upward as punished players increase contributions slightly and cooperators continue contributing. A few players who still come in very low draw stronger (but still limited) punishment aimed at deterrence rather than revenge.

The punished player(s) from round 3 increase contributions further. Most contributors remain cooperative; a small number of one-off punishments continue against undercontributors to sustain the cooperative norm.

With cooperation relatively stable, many players continue contributing at high levels. Punishment is rare because contributors are mostly within the cooperative band and punishment is costly.
Midgame reaches a cooperative peak as enforcement and reciprocation establish trust. Players expect others to contribute, so most contribute strongly and few punishments occur.
High cooperation continues but small deviations appear (individual noise or opportunistic testing). Punishment remains restrained and is only used for notable deviations.
Players largely sustain high contributions. Because punishment is costly, most refrain unless they see clear unfairness; with most cooperating, punishment events are minimal.

As the horizon shortens (later rounds), some players begin to reduce contributions—testing whether others will still punish. Punishment may appear but becomes less frequent because the deterrent effect weakens with fewer future rounds.
Cooperation edges down as players anticipate approaching the end. Punishers become more selective because punishment costs can no longer be recouped by future cooperative benefits.

Endgame effects strengthen; several players reduce contributions noticeably. Few punishers invest in costly sanctions since there's little future to enforce, but persistent low contributors may still attract isolated punishment as a fairness signal.
Contributions continue to decline as many players defect more often in the final segment. Punishment becomes rare because the cost of punishing is unlikely to be offset by future gains.

With one round remaining after this, most players act almost purely selfishly; contributions fall further. A small number may still punish as a final expression of fairness or retaliation, but actions are sparse because punishers bear the full cost now.

Final round: the threat of future punishment is gone, so many revert to pure self-interest; contributions are low. Punishment is rare because it only reduces payoffs in the terminal round and cannot change future behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so an individual’s marginal return on each contributed coin (8.1/13 ≈ 0.62) is below 1. That creates the classic tension: contributing benefits the group but is individually costly, so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. In practice, many experiments show moderate cooperation early on, some norm enforcement via punishment (targeted at clear low contributors), and a gradual decline in contributions over time as incentives and retaliation dynamics take their toll — especially as the end approaches and “last-round” logic reduces incentives to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to both sides (source pays 3 per unit, target loses 7 per unit), so it tends to be used sparingly and mostly in early-to-mid rounds to deter persistent low contributors; retaliation can occur but is also relatively rare because punishment is expensive.

Below I simulate a plausible 16-round sequence. Expect initial moderate contributions, occasional targeted punishments of low contributors, some short-lived improvements after punishment, and an overall downward trend toward low contributions in the final rounds with punishment largely disappearing at the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players over 16 rounds. Each round players choose a private contribution (0–20) from 20, then the group sum is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, and afterwards players may spend costly punishment (3 coins per unit inflicted, 7 coins deducted per unit from the target). Typical dynamics: many players start by contributing moderately high amounts (norms of cooperation), while a few test the waters with low contributions or free-riding. Punishment is used sparingly to sanction clear low contributions; punishers incur a cost, so punishment tends to appear only when some players are seen as persistent defectors or when someone is surprised by a drop. Because players see who punished them, limited retaliatory or reciprocal punishment can appear in subsequent rounds. Over repeated rounds, punishment can stabilize cooperation for a while, but toward the known final rounds contributions often decline (endgame effect) as incentives to sustain cooperation fade. There is uncertainty about individual types (some unconditional cooperators, some conditional, some opportunistic defectors), so expect modest fluctuations, a few targeted punishment events (not mass punishment), some retaliations after being punished, and a gradual decline in contributions near the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players who each get 20 coins per round, decide privately how much to contribute (0–20), then the group pot is multiplied and split. After seeing contributions, players can punish others at personal cost (3 coins per punishment unit) that removes 7 coins per unit from the punished. Key incentives:
- Contributing helps the group (multiplied return) but is individually costly; free-riding (contributing little) raises private payoff.
- Punishment can deter free-riding, but it is costly to the punisher and thus tends to be used sparingly and selectively.
- Because punishers are identifiable, targeted punishment will be used mainly against clear low contributors or repeated defectors.
- Knowledge of a finite horizon (16 rounds) makes defection more attractive in late rounds (backward induction), so contributions tend to decline toward the end; punishment intensity typically drops in the final rounds as costs outweigh future benefits.
- Uncertainties include heterogeneity in preferences (some are strong cooperators or strong free-riders), how much others will punish, and whether occasional low contributions are mistakes or intentional.

Predicted unfolding in summary:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high contributions with a few free-riders; targeted punishing of obvious low contributors to signal that defection will be costly.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators increase contributions after punishment; average contributions stabilize at a higher level, with punishment used rarely to discipline occasional lapses.
- Late rounds: gradual decline in contributions; punishments become rare as the incentive to enforce cooperation weakens near the end. Final round sees the biggest decline in average contributions.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group benefit (every coin contributed is multiplied by 8.1 and shared). Because the marginal per-coin return to the group (8.1/13 ≈ 0.62) is less than 1, the dominant individual strategy is to contribute 0, but groups often sustain positive contributions through conditional cooperation and the threat or use of punishment. Punishment is costly (3 coins per unit to the punisher) but effective (7 coins deducted from the target), so players tend to punish sparingly and mainly target clear free-riders or retaliators. Players cannot coordinate verbally, so uncertainty about others' types (cooperators, free-riders, retaliators) drives learning across rounds.

Typical unfolding: contributions start at a moderate level as players test one another. When someone under-contributes relative to the group expectation, they may receive limited punishment from a few peers; this encourages higher contributions later. Retaliation is possible because punishers are identifiable, but full-scale vendettas are rare because punishment is costly. With a finite horizon (16 rounds), contributions often rise during mid-game if punishment sustains cooperation and then decline toward the very end as the incentive to build future reputation disappears. I simulate those plausible dynamics: modest initial contributions, selective low-volume punishment to discipline defectors, gradual increase and stabilization in mid-game, and an endgame collapse in the last one or two rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game (16 rounds) creates incentives to balance private holdings with the group return: contributing reduces your private coins now but raises the pot that is multiplied by 8.1 and split among all 13 players. Because the per-player share of each contributed coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, contributing is socially efficient but individually costly. Players start uncertain about others' behavior; if many contribute, cooperation can be sustained. Punishment offers a way to enforce cooperation by imposing a larger cost on defectors (7 per punishment unit) at a cost of 3 to the punisher, but punishment is costly and is therefore used sparingly in real games. Over 16 rounds we expect an initial phase of moderate cooperation with occasional punishments for low contributors, a midgame where cooperation can be sustained if punishment is used, and a late-stage decline as backward induction and endgame thinking reduce incentives to pay costs to enforce cooperation. Punishment will appear intermittently—mainly directed at clear low contributors—but will be limited because punishers also pay a cost. Toward the final rounds contributions tend to fall and punishment declines.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 identifiable players, 16 rounds, and costly peer punishment. Each coin contributed returns less to the contributor (8.1/13 ≈ 0.62) than it adds to the group's total, so the individual incentive each round is to free-ride (contribute 0), while the group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes all 20. Because contributions are private before decisions, players cannot condition their contribution on others' current moves; they only learn about others' contributions after each round and can respond with punishment. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 per unit) and much harsher to the target (7 per unit), so punishment is effective but used sparingly in real games.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start reasonably cooperative (moderate contributions) as trust and social norms matter.
- Middle rounds: some low contributors appear; a few cooperative players sometimes punish blatant free-riders to enforce cooperation, which causes punished players to raise contributions for a while. Punishment remains rare because it's personally costly.
- Late rounds: as the end approaches, forward-looking players reduce contributions (backward induction), so average contributions decline. Punishment also declines because its future-return motive weakens; occasional punitive acts may still occur out of spite or norm enforcement, but overall punishment frequency falls.
- Final round: most players anticipate no future and contribute little or nothing; punishment is unlikely since it only reduces the punisher's payoff with no long-run benefits.

The transcript below follows these expectations: modest initial cooperation, occasional one-off punishments targeted at repeat low contributors, temporary recoveries after punishment, and a gradual decline in cooperation toward the final rounds. Punishments are sparse and small (1 unit most times).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and costly peer punishment. Each coin contributed to the public pot yields 8.1 coins total and is split equally among 13 players, so a contributor receives about 0.623 coins back per coin they contribute (8.1/13). That means contributing is socially efficient but individually costly: the group benefits if people contribute, but each player loses on every coin they give relative to keeping it privately. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per punishment unit) and much harsher on the target (7 coins lost per unit), and punishers can be identified by targets. Because punishment is expensive, players typically use it sparingly and mainly to discipline clear free-riders or to enforce emerging norms.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' cooperativeness leads to moderate contributions (not full 20, not zero). A small number of clear free-riders will invite punishment from a few norm-enforcers.
- Middle rounds: reputations form. If punishers are willing to pay the cost, contributions tend to rise toward high levels because defectors are disciplined and adjust upward.
- Late rounds: the "endgame" incentive kicks in — with known final rounds, some players reduce contributions because future retaliation cannot be leveraged; punishment also tends to fall off because it's costly and less effective. That can cause declines in contributions in final rounds.
- Uncertainties: individual preferences (social vs. selfish), error/mistakes, and varying willingness to pay for punishment create heterogeneity. Punishment is expected to be selective (target low contributors) and sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 rounds, private endowments (20 coins each round), a high multiplier on the group return (8.1) and a costly punishment stage. The incentives: contributing to the public pot increases group payoffs (and everybody's share) but is individually costly; free-riding (giving little or nothing) can raise a player's private round payoff while lowering others' returns. Punishment lets players impose a larger damage on a low contributor (−7 per punishment unit) at a smaller personal cost (−3 per unit), so punishment can be used to discourage persistent free-riding. However punishment is itself costly, so players weigh enforcement against its cost, and because the game has a known finite horizon (16 rounds), end-game incentives tend to push contributions down toward the final rounds.

Likely unfolding: early rounds typically see moderate-to-high contributions as many participants try cooperating or "testing" the group. A few low contributors (defectors) usually appear immediately; cooperators sometimes respond with a small amount of targeted punishment to signal that free-riding is costly. If punishment is credible and not overused, contributions tend to stabilize at intermediate-high levels through mid-game. As the horizon approaches, contributions generally drift down (the end-game effect); punishment becomes rarer because it's costly and because threats of future retaliation lose force. Throughout, punishment will be sparse and targeted — typically 1 unit from one or two players per round directed at persistent low contributors rather than large, frequent sanctioning.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 10 players against a standard social dilemma: each round everyone can contribute some (0–20) of a private endowment to a group pot that is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. Individually, each coin kept gives you +1 to your private holding, while each coin contributed returns only 0.66 to you via the shared pot — so the short-run dominant action for a self-interested player is to contribute 0. But if many players contribute, the group gets a much larger payoff overall. Because players do not see peers' choices before deciding and they only see their own net payoff at the summary stage, information is limited. Messages can be used to signal intentions, reveal one’s own contribution and payoff (which lets others infer the total), or to try to coordinate.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty breeds a mix of modest contributions and cautious free-riding. A few players may "test" cooperation by contributing more, others will partially cooperate or hedge.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators respond to perceived generosity; if they infer that total contributions are falling, they downgrade their own contributions. If a visible leader attempts renewed cooperation and shares their payoff/contribution, others may temporarily follow.
- Late rounds: because the number of rounds is unknown, there's no forced end-game unraveling, but fatigue and repeated evidence of free-riding generally push contributions down. Occasional attempts to restart cooperation appear, producing temporary rebounds.
- Messages are sparse and purposeful: short appeals to contribute, short announcements of one’s own contribution and payoff to influence others, or a complaint about free-riding.

This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins per round. Because the group-level multiplier (6.6) divided by 10 gives a per-player return of 0.66 per coin contributed, contributing to the public pot increases total group earnings but is individually dominated: any one player benefits more privately by contributing less when others contribute. Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation (players testing each other's willingness to contribute). Communication can sustain cooperation temporarily, but without a known end point players tend to drift toward lower contributions as free-riding pays off and reciprocity breaks down. Uncertainty about the number of rounds encourages caution: some players will attempt to maintain cooperation with messages; others will exploit cooperation or revert to low contributions when others defect. Chat will be sparse and mostly about coordination, calls-out after defections, and occasional appeals to fairness or reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a standard linear public goods game with ten players. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all players. Since each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each contributor (6.6/10) but costs 1 coin privately, the individually rational choice is to contribute 0. Group rationality would have everyone contribute everything, but the private incentive to free-ride typically causes contributions to fall over time. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect some initial cooperation (experimentation and norm-establishment), then a drift downward as free-riding pays off. Occasional messages can sustain or briefly revive cooperation if enough players coordinate, but such revivals are often fragile. game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with ten anonymous players, 20 private coins each round and a group return that gives 0.66 coins back to each player for every coin contributed (6.6 total multiplied and split across 10). Individually, contributing costs 1 coin while returning only 0.66 to yourself, so the short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). The group optimum, however, is high contributions because the group return per coin (6.6 total) exceeds the cost.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will try to cooperate or propose target contributions (coordination attempts). Because players can compute the group's total contribution from their own payoff and their own choice, they will learn aggregate behavior each round even though they don't see individual contributions.
- Midgame: conditional cooperators may adjust contributions in response to the inferred group total. Without individual-level accountability, persistent free-riders (who contribute little or nothing) can be hard to punish; this tends to push average contributions down over time.
- Communication can help coordinate and temporarily sustain cooperation, but because peers' individual choices remain private, messages are less powerful than in fully transparent settings.
- Uncertainty about the total number of rounds makes endgame effects weaker — players cannot be certain when (if) to defect for a final one-shot gain — which tends to sustain some cooperation relative to a known finite horizon, but without strong feedback cooperation usually erodes slowly.
- Expectation: a moderate start with a few cooperative signals, some free-riding emerges and pulls down the group total; occasional coordination attempts produce temporary rebounds, but the long-term trend is gradual decline in contributions, with sparse but meaningful chat.

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, private endowments of 20 each round, and a group multiplier of 6.6 that is split equally. Each coin a player contributes yields 0.66 to each player (6.6/10), so an individual loses 1 coin from private holdings but only gains 0.66 back — a net personal loss per coin contributed. That makes free-riding (contributing 0) the individually dominant choice in one-shot play, but group payoff is higher when people contribute.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we should expect some initial cooperation: some players will try to signal or reciprocate, and a few may contribute high amounts early. Chat is allowed and can help sustain cooperation, but in laboratory and online settings chat is typically sparse. Over rounds, as players experience private payoffs and little visible information about peers (they only see their own net), conditional cooperators will withdraw if they perceive others are defecting; free-riders will persist. So the likely unfolding is: modest-to-high contributions in the first few rounds driven by optimism or persuasion; then a gradual decline as incentives to free-ride assert themselves and the group fails to coordinate on sustained cooperation. Occasional small attempts to rekindle contributions may appear through chat, but by the middle-to-late rounds contributions will be low or zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 each round, a large multiplier (6.6) on the group pot, and equal sharing across the 10 players. The dominant individual incentive in any one-shot choice is to contribute 0 (free-ride) because each coin kept yields 1 to the chooser but only 0.66 to each player on average (0.66 per player from each contributed coin). However, because the group return per coin is 6.6 total (so 0.66 per person) the social optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20 coins. Uncertainty and anonymity create tension: players can't see others' current contributions when choosing, and they only learn their own payoff at the summary stage each round. Communication can promote coordination but is cheap and non-binding, so promises may be broken.

Likely dynamics: early rounds often start with moderate cooperation as players test each other's responsiveness and signal intent. A few free-riders can quickly erode trust and pull average contributions down; conditional cooperators will reduce their contributions in response. Because players do not know how many rounds will be played, there is no guaranteed final-round unraveling, which can help sustain cooperation longer than in a known finite-horizon game. Still, over many anonymous rounds we usually see a gradual decline in average contributions, punctuated by occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation via messages. Chat will be sparse and mostly about proposals, disappointment at free-riding, or short promises.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among the 10 players, so each coin you contribute increases your own payoff by 0.66 but costs you 1 — a private net loss of 0.34 per coin. That creates a strong private incentive to free-ride (contribute 0), while the group's total welfare rises if people contribute. Players can send messages to the group, but contributions are made without seeing others' choices that round. Also players do not know how many rounds the game will have, and at the end of each round they only see their own net payoff (not others').

Likely dynamics: initial rounds often start with moderate-to-high contributions due to cooperation norms, confusion, or people making commitments in chat. But because private incentives favor free-riding and feedback is noisy (you only see your own payoff, not others' contributions), contributions tend to drift downward over time. Chat can sometimes coordinate or temporarily boost contributions, especially if a few players are committed cooperators; but unobserved defections and personal payoff signals usually push many toward lower contributions. Expect a mixture: a few persistent cooperators who keep contributing some amount, some conditional cooperators who try to match perceived group behavior (but have limited information), and several players who free-ride (often contributing 0). Chat will be sparse and purposeful — occasional promises, short appeals, or complaints — producing modest temporary increases but not reversing the long-term decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, each receiving 20 coins per round and deciding how many to contribute to a public pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed yields 0.66 coins back to every player (including the contributor). Individually, contributing one coin reduces your private holding by 1 but increases your payoff by 0.66 — a net loss of 0.34 — so the dominant individual incentive is to contribute 0. Socially, however, everyone contributing their full endowment maximizes group welfare.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often show some cooperation (players try contributions in the teens) because players are uncertain, want to establish reciprocity, or hope to coordinate. Once players observe their own payoffs and infer others' behavior, contributions often drift downward as free-riding pays off, unless credible communication or norms emerge. Chat tends to be sparse but can briefly encourage cooperation or call out low contributors; such messages can temporarily raise contributions. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no clear final-round collapse, but contributions commonly decline toward low levels over time with occasional bumps when a few players try to re-establish cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with incomplete information about the total number of rounds and private payoffs shown only to each player at the end of each round. Each round players choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among the 10 players. The dominant individual incentive is to contribute nothing (keep 20) because every contributed coin returns only 0.66 to the contributor (0.66 < 1), so free-riding is payoff-maximizing given others' contributions. However, the social optimum is everyone contributing their full endowment, because the group receives the multiplication benefit.

Typical dynamics expected here:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test others and try to establish norms or reciprocal strategies. Communication can support cooperation but is not fully credible because contributions are private.
- Because players do not observe others’ contributions, responses are based on their own payoffs and any messages. This uncertainty makes conditional strategies (like “I’ll contribute more if others do”) harder to sustain.
- Over repeated rounds without a known end, cooperation commonly drifts downward: some conditional cooperators reduce contributions when they suspect free-riding; some unconditional cooperators keep contributing high; others free-ride consistently.
- Sparse, short, and sometimes persuasive chat messages are typical; they can temporarily boost coordination but usually do not fully stop the decline in contributions.
- Near the later rounds (even though players don't know when the game ends), you often see more variability: some players give up contributing, some make last-ditch cooperative efforts, and some exploit those efforts.

Given these incentives and uncertainties, I predict moderate initial contributions with a gradual decline over the 19 simulated rounds, punctuated by a few brief pledges and appeals in chat. A few players (e.g., CHICKEN, SLOTH) behave as relatively steady cooperators; others (e.g., GORILLA, DOG, OWL) act as persistent free-riders. CHAT is sparse and directed — announcements, pleas, and occasional self-reports of own payoff at summary.

However, the social optimum is everyone contributing their full endowment, because the group receives the multiplication benefit.

- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test others and try to establish norms or reciprocal strategies. Communication can support cooperation but is not fully credible because contributions are private.
- Because players do not observe others’ contributions, responses are based on their own payoffs and any messages. This uncertainty makes conditional strategies (like “I’ll contribute more if others do”) harder to sustain.
- Over repeated rounds without a known end, cooperation commonly drifts downward: some conditional cooperators reduce contributions when they suspect free-riding; some unconditional cooperators keep contributing high; others free-ride consistently.
- Sparse, short, and sometimes persuasive chat messages are typical; they can temporarily boost coordination but usually do not fully stop the decline in contributions.
- Near the later rounds (even though players don't know when the game ends), you often see more variability: some players give up contributing, some make last-ditch cooperative efforts, and some exploit those efforts.

Given these incentives and uncertainties, I predict moderate initial contributions with a gradual decline over the 19 simulated rounds, punctuated by a few brief pledges and appeals in chat. A few players (e.g., CHICKEN, SLOTH) behave as relatively steady cooperators; others (e.g., GORILLA, DOG, OWL) act as persistent free-riders. CHAT is sparse and directed — announcements, pleas, and occasional self-reports of own payoff at summary.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group contribution is multiplied by 6.6 and divided equally among all players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each player. That means a coin contributed raises a player's own return by only 0.66 (less than the private value 1), but it raises total group surplus. The standard tension applies: individually optimal play is to contribute 0, but the group's payoff is higher when people contribute.

Typical dynamics to expect here: an early phase where some players experiment or try to signal cooperation (promises, modest contributions). Because players don't know the horizon, there is less strict end-game collapse, but reputation and conditional cooperation often lead to a gradual decline in contributions over many rounds as players respond to observed returns and to messages. A few players may be consistently cooperative (high contributors) and some consistently defect (near-zero contributions). Sparse chat will include occasional promises, calls for coordination, and short reactions to one's own round payoff. Uncertainty about others' contributions and the private nature of decisions will produce cautious behavior and gradual adjustment rather than sudden all-or-nothing shifts.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with ten players who each get 20 coins per round and choose an integer contribution 0–20. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 6.6 and shared equally (so each player gets 0.66 per coin of the group's total). Individually, a coin you contribute costs you 1 but returns 0.66 to you via the public pot — so free-riding is the dominant short-term incentive. Group-optimal behavior is full contribution by everyone, but individual incentives and uncertainty about others' choices normally produce declining contributions over time.

Because players only see their own round payoff (not each other's contributions) they can still infer the group's total contribution from that payoff, but not who contributed. That makes conditional strategies (e.g., "I'll match the average next round") possible but noisier. Also, players do not know how many rounds there will be, which lowers explicit end-game collapse but tends to produce gradual erosion of cooperation as suspicions about others' free-riding increase. Chat is allowed but typically sparse; a few proposals and occasional signals (promises, complaints, or payoff reports) will appear. I predict an initial moderate level of cooperation with a few free-riders, followed by a gradual decline toward low contributions, with a few players intermittently trying to revive cooperation via messages or small contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK). Each round everyone privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split evenly. After seeing contributions, players can pay to punish specific peers (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the punished player). Players can also send chat messages at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with moderate cooperation (midpoint contributions), but a few players may free-ride. Because the multiplier is large relative to a single player's return, free-riders can look tempting: a low contributor can still profit from others' contributions.
- Punishment is the only enforcement tool here. Players who repeatedly contribute little are likely to be targeted, but punishment is costly so it will be used sparingly and mostly by a few enforcement-minded players.
- Communication (chat) will be sparse and usually aimed at encouraging cooperation or calling out low contributions.
- Over many rounds, contributions may rise if punishers successfully deter free-riding. But when punishments are too weak or too infrequent, defection can persist or re-emerge. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no guaranteed end-game collapse, but some gradual drift downwards is possible toward later rounds.
- Expect a small number of recurrent targets (here, DUCK shows up as a repeat low contributor in this simulated history), occasional one-off defections (e.g., CHICKEN or OWL once), and limited retaliation (some punished players may retaliate once or twice).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives against group welfare. Each player gets 20 coins per round and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot. Every coin contributed returns 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins to each player — so contributing is costly privately (you lose 1 coin to gain ~0.66), but the group benefits if many contribute. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we should not expect simple endgame unraveling; cooperation can be sustained through reputation, messaging, and the possibility of costly punishment.

Punishment is a blunt enforcement tool: it lets players reduce others' payoffs (5 coins deducted per punishment unit) at a private cost (2 coins per unit). That makes punishment effective but also costly, so in practice players use it sparingly — usually to sanction clear free-riders or persistent defectors. Messages are typically few but used to coordinate or call out behavior. Early rounds often feature probing behavior (some low contributions to test tolerance), followed by mild sanctions that bring most players toward a cooperative norm. Occasional deviations and occasional retaliations can occur, but with identified punishers the group tends to converge on a relatively high contribution level if punishments are credible and not too costly to maintain.

Based on these incentives and typical patterns, I predict:
- Early rounds: a mixture of moderate contributions and a few low contributions; selective punishment of clear undercontributors.
- Middle rounds: higher, more stable contributions as norms form and punishments deter extreme free-riding.
- Late rounds: possible small fluctuations but no systematic collapse because players don't know when the game ends. Punishment remains sparse and targeted.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields 1 to the player) against a group return (each contributed coin returns 6.6 total, i.e. 0.66 to each of 10 players). The dominant selfish choice is to contribute 0, since every coin contributed loses the contributor 0.34 on net. However, with costly punishment available (2 coins cost per punishment unit, 5 coins damage to the punished player per unit), a subset of players can discipline low contributors and sustain higher contributions. Expect early rounds to show heterogeneous behavior: some automatic cooperators contribute a lot, free riders contribute little, and a few enforcers punish. Over repeated rounds, conditional cooperators will tend to increase contributions if punishment is used, producing higher group contributions mid-game. Punishment will be used sparingly — mostly directed at persistent low contributors — because it is costly to punish. Toward the unknown end, some players may reduce contributions (endgame-style) or attempt to free-ride if punishers ease off; retaliation is possible but limited. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to threaten, explain, or chastise defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a common pot that is multiplied and divided equally. Contributing is socially efficient but individually costly; free-riding (contributing little or nothing) can pay off unless others punish it. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect some gradual pattern: early rounds are experimental (people test how others behave), mid-game cooperation tends to rise if punishment deters free-riding, and contributions stabilize at moderate-to-high levels. Punishment is costly to send (2 coins per punishment unit) and powerful against the target (5-coin deduction per unit), so players punish sparingly and primarily to deter repeated low contributions. Messages are few but purposeful: warnings, promises, and occasional calls for fairness. Occasional provocations (one person testing the group) will be met with small, targeted punishments by enforcers (players like GORILLA, RABBIT, DOG) and verbal reminders. Over many rounds this dynamic tends to support moderately high cooperation, with small setbacks when individuals test limits.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and an unknown horizon. Typical dynamics:
- Early rounds usually show a mix of contributions as players test others' willingness to cooperate. Some will free-ride to see if they can benefit.
- Because punishment is allowed and punishers are identifiable, a few players (often those who care about group outcomes or prefer norms) will use small punishments to discipline clear free-riders. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it tends to be used sparingly and targeted.
- Once a norm of cooperation is established and enforced, contributions typically rise and stabilize at higher levels. Occasional lapses (short-term defections) can occur; these are usually met with a small punitive response that restores cooperation.
- Identification of punishers creates a risk of retaliation, but retaliation is usually limited because it is costly and reduces overall earnings. Expect a short, limited retaliation episode if someone is punished and then retaliates, then a return to cooperative contributions.
- Because players do not know the end, end-game collapse is less pronounced than in fixed-horizon games; small declines may appear late but overall cooperation often persists if punishment enforcement is effective.

Below I simulate rounds 1–19, showing likely chats, contributions, punishments, and the summary stage each round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, a private endowment of 20 each round, a group return of 6.6 times total contributions split evenly, and a costly punishment stage. The private marginal return from contributing is 0.66 per coin (6.6/10), which makes free-riding individually attractive but collectively harmful. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect conditional cooperation early on (many contribute positive amounts), occasional targeted punishment of clear free-riders to enforce cooperation, and a gradual erosion of contributions as the game progresses if punishments are either too costly to sustain or yield insufficient compliance. Chat messages will be sparse and used mainly to signal intentions, call out low contributions, or threaten punishment. Punishment will be used sparingly (often 1–2 units) and typically directed at repeated low contributors. Over 19 rounds we should see an initial relatively cooperative block, intermittent punishment episodes to curb clear defectors, and a slow drift toward lower contributions as punishment becomes costly and some players experiment with free-riding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep coins) against a socially optimal outcome (everyone contributing). With a 6.6 multiplier on the pot and equal split, group payoffs rise sharply with contributions, but each player individually gains more by free-riding given the split. Punishment is costly to send (2 coins per unit) but strongly harmful to the recipient (5 coins per unit), so punishment is an effective but expensive enforcement tool. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they are likely to treat the game like an indefinite-horizon interaction: initial rounds will be exploratory (some low contributions), messages and occasional punishments will be used to deter persistent free-riding, and conditional cooperators will raise contributions over time once they see punishment credible. Chat will be used sparingly to coordinate norms; punishments will be relatively infrequent and targeted at repeat or blatant low contributors. Over 19 rounds I expect cooperation to rise from mixed/low early rounds toward higher, more stable contributions in later rounds, with isolated punishments when defection occurs.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with an unknown horizon and costly peer punishment. Typical dynamics: some players start cooperative (high contributions), some free-ride early (low contributions), and conditional cooperators adjust to the group average. Punishment is costly to the punisher but can deter persistent free-riding; because punishment also reveals who punished whom, it creates reputational/retaliatory risk. Expect moderate cooperation from the start (many contribute positive amounts), occasional low contributors (SLOTH and CHICKEN are likely to slip), and sparse targeted punishment by a few enforcement-minded players (GORILLA, OWL, maybe MOOSE or DOG) intended to discourage repeat free-riding. Chats will be few and used to coordinate norms or react to punishments. Over time contributions may stabilize at a moderate-to-high level, with occasional lapses and intermittent punishment to enforce norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players privately decide how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the pot is multiplied and split equally, so individual incentives push toward free-riding while group incentives push toward contribution. Because players can punish after seeing contributions (and can identify who punished them), punishment serves as a local enforcement mechanism: occasional punishments against low contributors can sustain higher contributions across rounds. However, punishment is costly to both sides, so it tends to be used sparingly — typically only when someone undercuts cooperation repeatedly or very blatantly (e.g., contributing near 0). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, they have less incentive to defect only at the end; this uncertainty supports more stable cooperation than a known finite-horizon game. Expect moderate-to-high average contributions with some fluctuations: occasional low contributors will be punished a bit, leading to temporary increases in their contributions. Chat messages will be sparse and functional (requests, warnings, thanks).

# GAME STARTS

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player 20 coins each round and asks them to decide how many to contribute to a shared pot. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally across the 10 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each contributor—less than the private cost of 1. That creates a tension: group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes a lot, but individual incentive is to free-ride. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, they face an indefinite-horizon environment where reputation and the threat of punishment can sustain cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and much more costly to the punished (5 coins per unit), so players tend to punish sparingly and only to correct low contributors or deter persistent free-riding. Chat can be used to coordinate or signal intentions, but real players typically use it sparingly.

I therefore predict an initial round with mixed contributions and light punishment targeted at obvious free-riders. As rounds proceed, conditional cooperators will increase contributions if low contributors are sanctioned, producing a rise to near-high cooperation in the middle rounds. Punishments will be occasional and targeted (1-unit or small), not widespread. Occasional dips in contribution will be met with brief punitive responses to restore cooperation. Because the horizon is unknown, the group has an incentive to maintain cooperation rather than unravel at the end, so cooperation remains relatively high into the later rounds with only a few corrective punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with voluntary contributions and costly punishment. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied and split equally. Because individual benefit from one coin contributed is less than the cost (the multiplier divided by group size is 6.6/10 = 0.66), unilateral free-riding is privately tempting. However, since the game repeats and players can punish others (at a private cost of 2 per punishment unit that deducts 5 from the target), punishments serve as a social enforcement mechanism: cooperators may punish low contributors to raise future contributions. Players do not know the total number of rounds, which tends to sustain cooperation relative to a known finite horizon.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds start with relatively high contributions (conditional cooperation and optimistic signals), but a few players test by contributing little (free-riders).
- Cooperators will punish visible low contributions but do so sparingly because punishment is costly. Punishment is targeted at repeat or extreme low contributors.
- Punished low contributors often raise their contributions in subsequent rounds; occasional retaliatory punishment is possible but limited.
- Over time, contributions will hover around moderately high levels (not full contribution) with intermittent drops and occasional small punishments to keep cooperation in range.
- Chat is sparse and used for short coordination, warnings, or calls for fairness.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with punishment, ten anonymous players, and an unknown number of rounds. Each round each player decides how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all players, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to the contributor—less than the private cost—creating a tension between individual incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) and group incentive to contribute more. Because players can punish after seeing contributions, costly punishment can support cooperation: players who punish bear a cost (2 coins per punishment unit) to reduce a target's payoff (by 5 coins per unit), making defection less attractive. Messages let players coordinate and signal intentions.

Typical dynamics: early rounds often see modest-to-moderate contributions, especially if players signal cooperation. A few players may test by contributing little; these are sometimes punished to enforce norms. Punishment is typically sparse and targeted: a few players punish clear defectors rather than wide-scale retaliation. Over many rounds contributions often stabilize at a moderate level among conditional cooperators, with occasional lapses corrected by punishment. If many players start to defect or if punishment is perceived as excessive, contributions can decline. Because players do not know the horizon, end-game collapse might be less pronounced than in known finite games, but some gradual fatigue or strategic experimentation may still lower contributions late in the sequence.

Below is a plausible transcript of rounds 1–19. At the start of each round I give a short explanation for the expected choices in that round. Contributions follow the avatar order given in <PLAYERS>. Punishments are listed as tuples (source, target, unit) and only non-zero actions are included.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to each contributor (less than the private value of 1). That creates a tension: contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly. Because the game lasts only two rounds, standard "end‑game" incentives push toward lower contributions (especially in round 2), but players may still contribute in round 1 if they expect reciprocity or want to signal cooperativeness.

The reward stage allows players to spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins to another player. Rewarding is costly and typically sparse; it can be used to reward high contributors (encouraging cooperation) or to punish (by withholding rewards), but anonymity of reward receipt (players don't see who rewarded them) reduces direct targeted retaliation. Expect moderate contributions from several players in round 1, with some free-riding. Few players will use small rewards in round 1 to signal approval; in round 2 contributions will likely decline overall, but those who received (or gave) rewards in round 1 may slightly adjust their behavior.

game_explanation:
This 2-round public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 1 coin) against group incentives (each coin contributed is multiplied by 2.2 and shared among six players, so each contributor gets back only about 0.367 of a coin per 1 contributed). The dominant self-interested action in a one-shot is to contribute 0 (free-ride). Because the game lasts only two rounds, backward induction makes full cooperation unlikely in theory, but two real-world factors push some players toward positive contributions in practice: (1) players can send rewards after seeing contributions, and (2) players can send non-binding chat messages (announcements or promises). Rewards are costly to the giver (4 coins per reward unit) and help the recipient (3 coins per unit); they are therefore an expensive way to incentivize cooperation and are used sparingly in actual experiments.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here: players do not observe others’ choices before making their own, so contribution decisions are based on beliefs, announcements, and social preferences. Rewards are anonymous at the recipient side (players cannot identify who rewarded them), which weakens direct reciprocity but still provides feedback. Expect a mix of behavior: some conditional cooperators or prosocial players will contribute nonzero amounts and occasionally reward high contributors; some players will free-ride. Rewarding will be sparse and targeted mainly at clear cooperators, and second-round behavior will respond to the pattern of rewards and payoffs seen after round 1.

Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 1 coin but the group returns only ~0.37 coins to each member), so the dominant incentive is to free-ride. However, because the game lasts two rounds and players can reward others afterwards (at a cost to themselves), we should expect some conditional cooperation: players who care about reciprocity may contribute some amount in round 1 and use sparse rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior. Rewarding will be used sparingly (each reward unit is costly: 4 coins from the sender, 3 coins to the receiver), mostly to thank high contributors or reciprocate in round 2. Chats are likely to be few and strategic (announcements of intentions or short responses). Free-riders who earn well without contributing may continue to do so unless they are directly targeted by group norms; because rewards are anonymous to recipients, targeted retaliation is less precise, so punishment is unlikely to be costly and frequent.

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with optional costly rewards. Each player gets 20 coins each round and decides privately how many to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally: because 2.2/6 < 1, each coin contributed yields less back to the contributor than it costs privately, so pure selfish incentives favor contributing 0. However, contributions raise everyone's payoff, so conditional cooperators may give positive amounts, especially early. With only two rounds, there is an “end-game” pressure to reduce contributions in round 2, since future reciprocity is limited. Rewarding allows players to shift payoffs (cost 4 per unit, gives 3 to the target) and can be used to reinforce cooperative contributions or reward helpful players, but rewards are costly and typically used sparingly. Because players cannot be identified as reward sources in their private summaries, rewards are a weak but visible instrument to signal norms; expect moderate cooperation in round 1 with a few targeted rewards, then lower contributions and fewer rewards in round 2.

game_explanation: This PGG has the standard tension: each coin a player contributes benefits the group (it is multiplied by 2.2 and shared), but each contributor loses more privately than they gain from that coin (the private return per contributed coin is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 < 1). So the individually rational move is to contribute 0, while the group-optimal move is to contribute all 20. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds and identities/reward givers are anonymous, we expect somewhat higher contributions in round 1 (players test others and try to encourage cooperation) and generally lower contributions in round 2 (endgame effect). Costly rewards provide a mechanism to reward cooperators and punish free riders, but they are themselves costly (giver pays 4 per reward unit to give the target +3), which makes reward use sparse and strategic. Uncertainties include whether players trust announced intentions, whether some will exploit others once rewarded, and how much players are willing to pay to shape behavior given only one future round.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After contributions are revealed and payoffs computed, players can spend coins to give positive rewards to others (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins). Messages may be posted at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1 will be exploratory. Some players will signal cooperative intent in chat and contribute a lot; others will test the waters or free-ride. Because reward is costly, only a few reward actions are typical, targeted at clear cooperators to encourage them.
- Round 2 will reflect reciprocity and strategic adjustment. Players who received rewards or positive signals are likely to maintain or increase contributions; unreciprocated low contributors may either keep free-riding or raise contributions to avoid being isolated. Rewarding remains sparse and is used to reinforce cooperation rather than punish.
- Uncertainties remain: players cannot identify reward sources, so reputational signals come only from contributions and messages. The limited horizon (2 rounds) reduces long-run incentives to build reputation, but direct reciprocity across the two rounds still matters.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with optional costly rewards. Each round, players decide simultaneously how much of 20 coins to contribute to a pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among all six players, so contributions help the group but each coin you keep privately yields 1 coin to you while contributing yields 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to yourself and 0.367 to each other player. That creates the classic tension: the privately optimal move is to contribute zero, but the group does better if people contribute.

Because the game is only two rounds and contributions are observable after choices, expectations and a small number of targeted rewards will shape round 2. In round 1 we should expect a mix: a couple of players try to signal cooperation (moderate contributions), some free-ride, and a few small rewards aimed at the clearest cooperators. Players tend to reward sparingly because rewards are costly (4 coins spent per unit) and deliver only 3 coins to the recipient. In round 2, players who were rewarded or saw others cooperate are somewhat more likely to maintain or increase contributions, while persistent free-riders (if unpunished) typically continue to contribute little.
