game_explanation:
This is a standard linear public goods game with 6 players, endowment 20 each round, a group multiplier of 2.2 and an equal split. The private return to one coin contributed is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367, well below 1, so the dominant incentive for a selfish player is to free-ride (keep the coin). However, the group optimum is to contribute more, because each coin benefits the group more than its private return. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, reciprocity (rewarding cooperators) and reputation-building are limited but still meaningful: contributors who get rewarded may be encouraged to contribute more in round 2; free-riders who receive no sanction or reward are likely to continue free-riding. Rewarding is costly (4 coins per reward unit), so players tend to reward sparingly—typically 0–1 units to signal appreciation or to reciprocate. Chat is usually sparse and used to coordinate or to call out behavior.

Expected unfolding: in round 1, absent history, many players will under-contribute or experiment; a couple of players may contribute moderately/highly to test cooperation. A few sparse rewards follow: cooperators get small positive reinforcement from some peers, which encourages them to stay cooperative in round 2. Free-riders mostly remain unrewarded and continue low contributions. Round 2 sees modest conditional increases by those who were rewarded and continued defection by the consistent free-riders. Rewards remain few and targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a multiplication factor (2.2) and a costly, pro-social reward stage. Each player starts each round with 20 coins and chooses a private integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.2, and split equally. After payoffs are calculated, players can spend 4 coins to send 1 reward unit that gives the recipient 3 coins. Rewards are anonymous in summaries (players see only amounts received, not who sent them). Because the game lasts only two rounds, behavior will mix motives:
- Round 1: uncertainty about others' types encourages moderate contributions. Some players may signal intentions via chat. Rewards are used sparingly to encourage cooperation or to reciprocate observed contribution levels.
- Round 2 (final round): endgame effects often reduce contributions, but players who were rewarded or who prefer to "reward cooperators" may still contribute and send small rewards. Because rewards are costly and anonymous, players tend to send only a few units.

Uncertainties include not knowing whether chat statements reflect actual contributions, and anonymity of reward sources reduces direct reciprocity (you can reward cooperators you observed, but you can't directly repay an anonymous reward). Expect contributions to be moderate but possibly decline in round 2, and relatively few, small reward transfers in each round.

This is a two-round public goods game with costly rewards. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players, so each coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to the contributor personally — less than the private cost of 1. That creates a strong incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) even though the group is better off if many contribute. Because the game lasts just two rounds and contributions are anonymous, forward-looking incentives to cooperate are limited. Typical dynamics: in round 1, expect heterogeneous behavior — some players (conditional cooperators or signalers) make moderate contributions, others free-ride or contribute little. A few targeted, small rewards are likely given to signal a norm or to encourage reciprocation in round 2. In round 2, if rewards successfully signal reciprocity, cooperative contributors may increase their contributions; persistent free-riders who are not rewarded are likely to remain low. Chat is available but usually sparse; players sometimes use it to announce intentions or to justify actions, which can slightly influence behavior in round 2.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and rewards. Each round, players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so contributions benefit the group but reduce the contributor's private holdings immediately. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 per punishment unit; each unit deducts 10 from the target) or reward (cost 4 per reward unit; each unit gives the target +3). Because punishment is expensive but very harmful to the target, players will use it sparingly — typically to sanction clear free-riding. Rewards are also used sparingly since they are costly to the giver and give relatively small benefits to recipients.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- In round 1, without prior history, contributions tend to be mixed: some conditional cooperators contribute moderately or highly, while others hedge or free-ride. High contributors may threaten or actually punish clear free-riders, but will do so sparingly because of the personal cost.
- Messages can influence behavior but are imperfect signals; players may state intentions but cannot force others.
- Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives to build reputation are limited. After round 1 punishments, some players may try to adjust in round 2 (either reduce contributions if they expect free-riding to persist, or increase to avoid sanctions), but overall contributions typically fall in the final round. Punishment and reward activity usually remains low across rounds.

This is a repeated (2-round) public goods game with 6 anonymous players. Each coin a player contributes returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 coins to that player (personal marginal per-capita return < 1), so contributions are individually costly but socially beneficial (group return per coin = 2.2 > 1). Because the game is short (only two rounds) and players cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, there's tension: forward-looking players may reduce cooperation in the final round (no future to enforce cooperation), but punishment and rewards can still be used to shape behavior. Punishment is relatively harsh on targets (−10 per punishment unit) while costing the punisher 4; rewards cost 4 and give the target +3. Given these payoffs and typical experimental behavior, I expect:
- Round 1: moderate overall contributions (some strong cooperators, some free-riding). A small number of targeted punishments against clear free-riders, and one or two sparse rewards for high contributors.
- Round 2: punished low contributors tend to increase contributions; some high contributors persist. With only two rounds, a mild decline in cooperation is possible, but the immediate sanctioning in round 1 often raises contributions in round 2 relative to the individual punished players. Chat will be sparse and focused on encouragement, apology, or brief moralizing. Punishment will be used sparingly.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot that is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Individually, each contributed coin returns only about 0.367 to the contributor (2.2/6), so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Collectively, however, full contribution is profitable. Because the game lasts only two rounds, strategic considerations differ across rounds: players often try some cooperation in round 1, hoping to use punishment and rewards to sustain cooperation in round 2, while expecting more defection in the final round.

Expectations and likely dynamics:
- Round 1: Mixed contributions — some conditional cooperators give moderate amounts, some selfish players give little. A few players may send short messages promising cooperation or threatening sanctions. Punishment and reward are used sparingly: a couple of players may punish clear zero contributors to deter free-riding, and a couple of small rewards may be given to encourage high contributors.
- Round 2: Conditional cooperators who were hurt by free-riding in round 1 may raise contributions after seeing their own payoffs and any punishments/rewards they experienced. However, because this is the final round, some players will defect (especially those who were not punished or who benefit from free-riding). Overall, expect a modest uptick in contributions by those responsive to sanctions, but persistent low contributions from a few players.
- Uncertainty: Players cannot see others' choices directly and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, so communication and inferred patterns matter but are noisy. Punishments are costly to the sender and severe to the target; rewards are costly and generate smaller benefits, so both tend to be used sparingly and strategically.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with 6 players. Each round players decide privately how much of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. The private marginal return on each contributed coin is less than 1 (2.2/6 ≈ 0.37), so the one-shot incentive is to contribute 0. Still, with two rounds and the possibility to punish or reward afterward, players often show some cooperation in round 1 (either hoping others will reciprocate, or because some are conditional cooperators). Punishment is effective at reducing a target's payoff (−10 coins per punishment unit) but costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit), so players punish sparingly—usually only severe free-riding triggers punishment. Rewards are cheaper for targets (+3 per reward unit) but also costly to the giver (4 coins), so rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.

Typical unfolding:
- Round 1: a mixed pattern — a few high contributors, some moderate contributors, a couple of low contributors. A small number of punishment and reward actions follow (punish obvious low contributors, reward high contributors), because players want to enforce norms but avoid heavy costs.
- Round 2: players who were punished in round 1 tend to raise contributions; cooperators often keep contributing. Punishment is reduced; a few rewards acknowledge improved behavior. Final payoffs reflect contributions plus redistributed pot plus the sparse punishment/reward effects.

The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so contributions benefit the group but cost the individual. With only two rounds, incentives to free-ride are strong: in the final round especially, the usual incentive to build or maintain a reputation is weak. However, the punishment (costly to the punisher but steep for the punished) and reward (cheaper for the giver but smaller benefit to the receiver) allow cooperators to sanction free riders or reward cooperators, which can sustain some contributions in round 1 and influence behavior in round 2.

Expectations: some players will contribute moderately or fully in round 1 (either because they prefer cooperation or hope to shape others' behavior), while others will free-ride. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly — a few targeted punishments against clear zero contributors and a few small rewards to recognize high contributors. After round 1 punishments/rewards, some punished players may retaliate or reduce contributions in round 2; others who were rewarded may remain cooperative. Overall, contributions may decline slightly in round 2, but not collapse entirely if a subset of players value cooperation or want to avoid being punished again.

This is a two-round public goods game with punishment and reward options. The marginal private return on each contributed coin is less than 1 (2.2 total return split among 6 players ≈ 0.37 to each), so purely selfish players have an incentive to free-ride. But social preferences and conditional cooperation are common: many players contribute moderately in round 1, then adjust in round 2 based on what they see. Because the game is only two rounds, players may try to signal cooperation early (to avoid being punished) or exploit the other side if they expect others to contribute.

Because players cannot trace punishments/rewards to specific senders, retaliation is less targeted; adjustments tend to be in contributions rather than in reciprocal punishment. Expect modest contributions in round 1, a small amount of punishment targeted at the weakest contributors, and some reciprocal rewarding among high contributors. In round 2, players who were punished or who observed others' generosity will typically raise contributions; punish/reward activity often decreases.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly punishment and reward. Each coin a player contributes costs them 1 privately but increases the group pot by 2.2 in total; divided among six players, each contributed coin returns about 0.367 to every player. That means any single coin contributed reduces the contributor's private holdings by about 0.633 (1.00 − 0.367), so the individual incentive is to free-ride, while the group incentive is to contribute. Because the game is only two rounds, forward-looking incentives for cooperation are limited but still present for the second round: players can reward or punish after round 1 and may adjust contributions in round 2 in response.

Punishment is costly for both sides (source pays 4 per punishment unit; target loses 10 per unit) so it can be an effective deterrent even when used sparingly. Rewards are also costly for the source (4) but give only +3 to the recipient, so they are less powerful per coin spent but can signal goodwill. Messages (cheap talk) may coordinate cooperation but are not binding.

Uncertainties include beliefs about others' intentions (do they plan to cooperate or freeride?), how much players will punish or reward, and whether reciprocity will be used. In practice we should expect: moderate contributions overall, a few targeted rewards for noticeable cooperators, and sparse punishment aimed at clear free-riders. After round 1, those who were punished are likely to raise contributions in round 2; those who received rewards may maintain or increase contributions. Because players cannot identify who punished/rewarded them in the summaries, retaliation is less precise, but behavior can still change based on observed contributions and public messages.

game_explanation: This is a 2-round public goods game with costly punishment and rewarding. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After seeing outcomes, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 to the sender, -10 to the target) or reward (cost 4 to the sender, +3 to the target) others, and they may send messages. Because the game lasts only two rounds, classic end-game incentives push toward lower contributions in the final round (no future to incentivize cooperation), while the possibility of punishment/reward can shift behavior if players expect enforcement or reciprocity. Uncertainty comes from not seeing others' contributions or who punished/rewarded you: punishers and rewarders are anonymous. In practice, contributions in early rounds are often mixed (some conditional cooperators give moderate amounts, some free-ride), with relatively sparse use of punishment and reward (because they are costly). In the second (final) round, many players tend to reduce contributions, but targeted punishment of obvious free-riders (if inferred) or reciprocal rewards between cooperators can still occur — though overall punishment is generally used sparingly.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with simultaneous contributions each round (0–20). The multiplier (2.2) makes each coin socially valuable but privately costly (each coin returns ~0.3667 to the contributor), so the dominant individual incentive is to free ride. Because contributions are revealed before punishment/reward, players can use costly punishment (source pays 4, target loses 10) or costly reward (source pays 4, target gains 3) to influence behavior. Punishment can deter low contributions but is itself costly and anonymous (targets don't see who acted). Rewards are less powerful per unit but can encourage cooperators.

Uncertainties and likely dynamics:
- Round 1: Without prior history, contributions typically mix: some players try to signal cooperation, some free ride. Cheap talk (pre-round messages) can encourage cooperation but is not binding. Observing a clear free rider (very low contribution) often triggers sparse punishment from a few cooperators, because punishment is costly and used sparingly.
- Round 2 (final round): Because the game ends after round 2, backward-induction suggests defection, but with experienced punishment from round 1, conditional cooperators may raise contributions to avoid punishment. Expect modest adjustment: punished players tend to increase contributions somewhat; cooperators may continue to punish low contributors but more sparingly. Overall, some increase in contributions is plausible but not full cooperation.

Transcript follows the rounds and keeps chat sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with a built-in rewarding stage. Each round each player privately has 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all 15 players, so each coin you contribute returns only about 0.247 to yourself through the public good (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247). That makes unilateral contribution costly to the individual but beneficial to the group as a whole: socially profitable contributions require others to contribute too.

Because rewards are available after contributions, players can direct costly positive incentives toward contributors: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins. That makes rewarding a fairly costly way to boost others' payoffs, and in practice people use rewards sparingly and selectively to encourage cooperators. Several uncertainties shape behavior: players do not know the total number of rounds, they cannot identify who gave them any reward in the summary, and there is no communication. Typical dynamics in such settings are moderate contributions in early rounds (some experiment-specific optimism), selective rewarding of high contributors, and a gradual drift downward in contributions over time unless rewards successfully sustain cooperation.

Predicted high-level pattern across the simulated 9 rounds:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions (some high cooperators, some free riders), a few targeted rewards to high contributors.
- Middle rounds: cooperation drifts slightly downward on average; rewards remain present but sparse and mostly directed to consistently high contributors (and occasionally to players who increase contributions).
- Later rounds: without punishment and with the costliness of rewarding, contributions tend to decline further; rewards still appear but remain limited in number and size.

Now the round-by-round transcript.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with limited information about others. Some try a relatively high contribution to test reciprocity; others hedge by contributing moderately or not at all. Early rewards are rare and typically directed at clearly generous contributors to signal appreciation.

Players observe aggregate outcomes from round 1 (their own payoff, amounts used/received in rewards, and peers' totals) and update beliefs. Those rewarded in round 1 (or who saw benefits) may keep contributing highly; persistent zeros (e.g., PARROT, SNAKE) draw little reciprocal reward. Rewarding remains selective.

Some players try to signal cooperation to attract rewards; high contributors tend to be rewarded again. Free riders remain visible and typically do not receive rewards. Rewarding continues to be sparse because each unit is costly.
Midway through the sequence, some cooperators reduce contributions slightly if they feel others free-ride; consistent cooperators (or visibly generous players) still attract a few selective rewards.

As patterns become clearer, average contributions drift downward for some players who do not receive rewards. Players who have been consistently generous remain focal points for rewarding, but senders remain cautious due to the 4-to-5 cost-benefit of rewards.

Some players experiment by increasing contributions again to try and attract rewards; persistent zeros still receive none. Rewarding is used to acknowledge attempts to cooperate and to reinforce reliable cooperators.

With experience, some players settle into lower but stable contribution levels; a few continue to contribute near the top of the range and receive intermittent rewards. Rewarding reinforces those patterns without being widespread.
As rounds progress, average contributions trend modestly downward overall; high contributors are still visible and occasionally rewarded. Reward senders remain frugal and selective.

In later rounds players often show some erosion of cooperation unless sustained by rewards. Rewards remain available but are still used sparingly to acknowledge sustained or renewed cooperative attempts.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This online public goods game gives each player 20 coins per round to allocate between a private holding and a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so cooperating (contributing) raises group returns but reduces the individual's private holding immediately. Because players cannot see choices before deciding and do not know the total number of rounds, behavior typically reflects a mix of heuristics: some players consistently contribute (intrinsic cooperators), some free-ride or test the waters (low contributors), and many are conditional cooperators who adjust their contributions in response to observed group averages and rewards. Costly rewards (4 coins cost per unit, 5 coins benefit to recipient) let players positively reinforce contributors, but rewards are used sparingly because they are costly. Given anonymity of who rewarded whom in player-facing summaries and no group chat, expect small, infrequent reward transfers targeted at visibly high contributors rather than direct punishment of low contributors. Over repeated rounds we typically see modest drift downward from initial optimism, with occasional stabilization when cooperators reciprocally reward one another.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with voluntary rewarding. Each round each player has 20 coins and can contribute some to a public pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly among all 15 players. The private return from contributing one coin is only about 0.2467 coins (3.7/15), so pure self-interest favors free-riding (contribute 0). However, many people start with some cooperation (positive contributions) out of conditional cooperation, reciprocity, or social preferences. The reward stage lets players spend 4 coins to give 5 coins to another player; each reward unit is costly to the giver but produces a net social gain (+1) and can be used to sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors or to reward perceived generosity.

Uncertainties that affect behavior: (1) Players do not know the total number of rounds, so end-game defection is less certain — this often helps sustain cooperation somewhat. (2) Rewarding is costly and anonymous in the summary (players can't identify who rewarded them), which reduces direct tit-for-tat but can still support norms if people generally reward cooperators. (3) Because reward cost is large relative to coin returns, rewards tend to be used sparingly and targeted at clearly cooperative players.

Predicted dynamics across the nine rounds: initial moderate contributions (near half the endowment) as players test each other, with a slight decline across rounds as some start free-riding or reduce contributions in response to observed average behavior. Rewarding will be infrequent and targeted: a few players who consistently contribute more (here PARROT and a few others) will be occasional recipients. Only a small number of single-unit rewards per round are likely because of the high cost (4 coins per reward unit). These sparse rewards may slow the decline of cooperation but won't fully prevent it.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players. Each round everyone decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied (by 3.7) and split equally. After seeing revealed contributions they may spend remaining coins to reward other players (each reward unit costs the source 4 coins and gives the target 5 coins). Players don't know how many rounds remain and cannot identify who sent rewards.

Likely dynamics:
- Many players start with moderate contributions; some specialize as high cooperators (contribute a lot each round) while a few free-ride persistently (contribute little or nothing).
- Because the multiplier is greater than 1 per capita (3.7 split across 15), group payoff increases when many contribute; but individual incentives still tempt under-contribution.
- Rewards are costly to send, so they are used sparingly and directed at visible cooperators to sustain or encourage cooperation; rewarding low contributors is unlikely.
- Over rounds we expect small adjustments: those who received rewards or saw others rewarded may slightly increase contributions; steady free-riders may remain low. Reward patterns remain sparse—usually 0–3 small rewards per round—from players who want to foster cooperation.
- Uncertainties: which peers are conditional cooperators vs. persistent free-riders cannot be known initially; because players don't know remaining rounds, some maintain cooperation to avoid being punished later, but others may experiment.

Because contributions are multiplied by a factor >1 but split among many players, the socially efficient move is high contribution, while the individually rational single-shot move is to contribute nothing. The uncertainty here (players do not know the total number of rounds) tends to support more sustained cooperation than a known finite horizon. The reward stage provides a costly way to reward (or, implicitly, not reward) others: each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins. In practice rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperative contributors rather than to punish defectors (punishment is not an option here), so expect a few modest positive transfers each round directed toward those who consistently contribute more. Some players will be consistent cooperators (contribution high each round), some consistent free-riders (contribute near 0), and several conditional cooperators who adjust upward if the group average looks cooperative. Over nine rounds we expect overall contributions to rise from a mixed start and then remain at moderate-to-high levels, with small, targeted reward flows concentrated on the high contributors (especially MOOSE, OWL, and steady contributors like COW).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players, 20 private coins each round. Each player's contribution to the public pot yields a multiplied return (3.7×) that is split equally among all players, so individual incentives favor free-riding (keep your 20 privately) while group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes more. Because players also have a costly reward instrument (4 coins cost to giver → +5 to recipient), small positive transfers can be used to reward cooperators and indirectly encourage contributions. Rewards are visible only as amounts received/sent in the summary (not who sent them), and players do not know the total number of rounds — this increases uncertainty about end-game behavior and typically stabilizes cooperation better than a known final round.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show heterogeneous behavior: some players start moderately cooperative (near half or more: 10–18), some moderately, and a few free-ride (0–3). Players test the environment for norms.
- Sparse reward use: because rewards are costly, most players reward only occasionally and usually with 1 unit (4 coin cost) to signal approval of high contributors. Expect only a few reward edges per round.
- Conditional adjustments: players who receive rewards tend to keep or slightly increase contributions; those who are ignored or see many free-riders may reduce contributions. Over several rounds, contributions typically cluster around moderate-to-high levels among reciprocating cooperators, while a core of persistent low contributors remains.
- Uncertainty about the horizon makes steep end-game free-riding less likely; instead we expect moderately stable contributions with some noise across rounds.

Below I simulate nine rounds consistent with these principles: modest, persistent cooperation by a plurality, a handful of free-riders, and sparse, targeted rewarding of higher contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments (20 coins each round), simultaneous contributions, a group multiplier (3.7) and a voluntary, costly reward stage. Because players do not know the total number of rounds and cannot condition their first-round play on future termination, early rounds typically show heterogeneous behavior: some players contribute a lot (altruists or unconditional cooperators), some contribute little or nothing (free riders), and many conditional cooperators choose intermediate contributions. The multiplier (3.7 split among 15 players) yields a private marginal per-coin return far below 1, so individually it usually pays to contribute 0. However, group returns create an incentive to cooperate if others do so.

Uncertainty about others' types and the difficulty of identifying rewarders means rewards will be used sparingly and mainly to reinforce standout cooperators (not to punish). Because rewards are costly (4 coins per unit) and produce modest gains for recipients (5 coins per unit), players will typically send only one or a few small reward units and only when they observe clear above-average contributions. Over rounds we expect modest decline in average contributions as free riding persists and conditional cooperators adjust downward after observing others' behavior, with only occasional small rewards directed at persistent high contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with voluntary monetary rewards. Each round players choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot that is multiplied (3.7×) and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may spend private coins to give discrete reward units to specific peers; each unit costs 4 to the giver and adds 5 to the receiver. Players do not learn identities of rewarders and do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early uncertainty typically produces moderate contributions: many players hedge, contributing neither full endowment nor zero.
- Conditional cooperators respond to observed group behavior: if many others contribute, they tend to raise contributions; if many free-ride, they lower contributions.
- Rewards are typically sparse and used selectively to reinforce cooperative contributors (not to punish), because rewarding is costly. Expect a few 1-unit rewards directed at high contributors each round rather than broad rewarding.
- A handful of persistent low contributors (free-riders) often emerge and receive no rewards; this may reduce average contributions over time. Because players do not know the horizon, endgame collapse may be muted compared with a known-final-round game.
- Overall, expect contributions to rise modestly if rewarders successfully reinforce cooperation, but with variability driven by a few low contributors and occasional fatigue.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with anonymous contributions and a separate costly reward stage. Each coin a player puts into the group raises the public pot by 3.7 coins but is then shared equally among all 15 players: the marginal per-capita return (MPCR) on one contributed coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, well below 1. That makes contributions privately costly: each coin you keep yields 1 to you, whereas contributing yields ≈0.247 to you through the public good. So the dominant one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0).

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, and because they learn aggregated information after each round (their own payoff, what they spent on rewarding others, and how much reward they received, and the peers' corresponding info), we expect conditional cooperation and reciprocity to arise in a limited way: some players will contribute moderately early on (testing cooperation), and others will respond by reducing contributions if they see low group returns. The separate reward stage (4 cost for the sender → +5 to the target) creates a mechanism to selectively reward cooperation, but rewards are costly to send and only slightly increase total group payoff (net +1 across source+target), so in practice players reward sparingly and usually target visibly higher contributors. Because reward sources are not identified by recipients (they see amounts received but not who sent them), rewards encourage generalised or reputational reciprocity rather than precise bilateral tit-for-tat.

Putting these forces together, a typical unfolding is:
- Round 1: moderate, heterogeneous contributions (players “test the water”), very few small rewards to top contributors.
- Middle rounds: some conditional cooperators sustain contributions for a while, but average contributions drift downward as free-riding emerges; sparse targeted rewards attempt to prop up cooperators.
- Later rounds: contributions decline further; rewards remain rare and targeted; without strong enforcement cooperation slowly decays.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods setting with 15 players per round. Each coin a player contributes is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally, so each contributed coin returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.25 to the contributor personally — less than the private cost of 1 coin. Pure selfish incentives favor contributing 0, so any cooperation must come from altruism, conditional cooperation, or incentives created by rewarding. The reward device is pro-social in aggregate (each reward unit costs 4 to the giver and adds 5 to the recipient, so total group wealth rises by 1 per reward unit), but it is individually costly to give. Because players do not see others' choices before contributing and cannot identify who rewarded them later, strong direct reciprocity is limited. Players also do not know the total number of rounds, which tends to sustain cooperation (no definite end-game drop), but uncertainty and the costliness of rewarding usually mean rewards are used sparingly.

Predicted unfolding: early rounds typically show heterogeneous behavior — a handful of consistent cooperators contribute moderately, many others free-ride. Occasional small rewards are used to acknowledge above-average contributors; those who get rewarded sometimes increase or sustain contributions in subsequent rounds. Because rewarding is costly, reward events remain few and targeted rather than widespread. Overall, contributions may fluctuate around a modest positive level rather than converge to full cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players each round. Each player gets 20 coins and decides a private integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so the social return to contributing is positive but individual temptation to free-ride remains (you keep your full 1 coin cost but only receive a fraction of its multiplied value). After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others: punish costs 4 per unit and removes 7 per unit from the target; reward costs 4 per unit and adds 5 per unit to the target. Because punishment/reward is anonymous (players don't know who acted), incentives are to signal norms through costly actions but direct retaliation is limited.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as norms are unestablished and many try to cooperate. Some players may free-ride immediately.
- Sparse punishment tends to be used to discipline clear free-riders; sparse rewards tend to reinforce cooperators. Both are costly, so actions are limited.
- Punishment can stabilize cooperation, but because it's costly and anonymous, it will be used sparingly and targeted at repeat low contributors.
- Over time, contributors may adjust upward if punished or rewarded; persistent free-riders may be targeted more, but not everyone pays the cost of sanctioning.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds in advance, endgame collapse is less certain; behavior tends to evolve gradually.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly costly punishment and rewarding. Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation (many players contribute some fraction of the 20-coin endowment) because people start optimistic and try to build public provision. Over time, if some players free-ride (contribute little), others reduce contributions in response, producing a gradual downward drift. Because players do not know the horizon, downward drift may be slower than in a known-final-round game, but decline is still common.

Punishment is costly to send (4 coins per unit) but quite harmful to the target (-7 coins per unit), so it is used sparingly and usually only against persistent low contributors. Rewards are also costly (4 coins to give, +5 to target) and are typically used to reinforce high contributors. Expect a few low-intensity punishments targeted at repeated free-riders and occasional small rewards to sustain cooperators. Because actions are anonymous in summaries, players can't see who targeted them, which limits direct retaliation; this tends to reduce large-scale punishment wars.

Given these forces, I predict moderate initial contributions with a slow decline across nine unannounced rounds, with sparse, low-unit punishment and reward actions concentrated on persistent low contributors and on signaling approval for high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game where each player gets 20 coins each round and decides privately how many to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally among the 15 players, so each unit contributed yields a return of 3.7/15 ≈ 0.2467 to each player — less than 1 for the contributor but potentially beneficial to the group. The dominant private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because you keep the full private value of the coin, while the group benefits from others' contributions. However, the game allows costly punishment and rewarding after contributions are revealed. Punishing costs the source 4 coins and reduces the target by 7 coins per punishment unit; rewarding costs the source 4 coins and gives the target +5 coins per reward unit. Because punishments and rewards are costly, they are typically used sparingly to enforce norms or encourage cooperation. Uncertainty about total rounds (players don’t know when the game ends) encourages more stable patterns of reciprocity and norm enforcement: early free-riding may be met with occasional punishment, prompting others to increase contributions later; conversely, excessive punishment is rare because it is costly and can provoke retaliation. Over many rounds we expect an initial low-to-moderate contribution level, a modest use of punishment to discipline clear free-riders, some targeted rewards to reinforce cooperative contributors, and gradual movement toward higher average contributions, with persistent outliers (e.g., repeated zeros) occasionally targeted by sparse punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and costly reward. Each round players choose how much of their private 20-coin endowment to put into a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally. Because the multiplier is greater than 1 but the share each individual receives is less than 1 times their own contribution, the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) while the group benefit is maximized by larger contributions. The punishment/reward stage lets players try to enforce cooperation or reward helpful behavior, but both actions are costly to the actor (4 coins per unit) and deliver asymmetric effects to the target (punishment removes 7 per unit; reward gives +5 per unit).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertain behavior and a mix of moderate contributions and a few free-riders. Some players try to sanction clear free-riders or reward cooperators, but sanctions are typically sparse because they are costly.
- Middle rounds: cooperation tends to decay as players see that free-riders can still benefit, and punishers pay costs to punish with limited deterrent effect. Rewards are used occasionally but sparingly.
- Later rounds: contributions typically stabilize at a lower level. Punishment is used rarely because of the costliness and limited identifiability of who punished whom. Occasional targeted punishments (against persistent zeros) and occasional small rewards (to encourage cooperative miners) can appear but are infrequent.

Uncertainties:
- Who will take on the costly role of punisher or rewarder is unknown and often rotates or drops out over time.
- Players cannot see who targeted them at the time of summary, which reduces direct reciprocity against punishers/rewarders.
- Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they may keep contributing some positive amounts early on but tend to reduce contributions over time.

Below is a simulated transcript from Round 1 through Round 9 showing likely contributions, sparse punishment/reward actions, and the round summaries players would see.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private interest and group benefit. Each player gets 20 coins and can contribute any integer 0–20 to a common pot. Every coin in the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and then split equally among the 15 players, so the private return from contributing one coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins — far less than the 1-coin cost. That means the individually rational move is to free-ride (contribute 0) even though the group maximum payoff comes from everyone contributing more. The punishment and reward stage provides a decentralized enforcement mechanism: players can pay to punish (cost 4 per unit to the source, -7 per unit to the target) or reward (cost 4 per unit to source, +5 per unit to target). Because both sanction types are costly, in practice they are used sparingly — often only to discipline clear defectors or encourage reliable cooperators.

Given players do not know how many rounds will be played, typical dynamics begin with relatively high contributions (hoping others cooperate), then drift downward as free-riding appears. Sparse punishments of obvious defectors can raise their contributions in subsequent rounds, but the costliness of sanctions and the anonymity of who imposed them limits how much cooperation can be sustained. Rewards are rarer than punishments and used selectively to reinforce consistent contributors. Expect: early-round cooperation, occasional targeted punishments of clear low contributors, a mixture of modest rewards to steady cooperators, and a gradual average decline in contributions across rounds.

The availability of costly punishment and rewarding after seeing contributions creates an institutional mechanism to enforce cooperation: low contributors are likely to be punished (they lose more than the punisher spends), and high contributors may be rewarded. However, punishments and rewards are costly to send, so players use them sparingly. Early rounds typically show heterogeneous contributions (some low, some medium), followed by escalation of norms: punishments target obvious free riders, rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation, and contributions tend to rise and then stabilize at a higher level. Because players do not know the number of rounds, cooperation supported by punishment/reward can be sustained across many rounds. I therefore predict: (1) moderate contributions in round 1 with a few punishments to the most egregious low contributors; (2) a gradual increase in average contribution across rounds as punishments and rewards establish norms; (3) punishment and reward remain sparse — used mainly to signal social norms rather than to punish everyone.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start without history. Expect a mix of modest cooperative contributions and a few low free-riders. After seeing contributions, a few players will punish the most obvious free-riders and a small number of rewards will be used to encourage mid-to-high contributors. Punishment and reward are kept sparse.

Players update beliefs based on round 1. Those who were punished (SNAKE, CROCODILE, SLOTH) are likely to increase contributions; cooperators may raise theirs slightly. A few targeted punishments will continue for persistently low contributors and a couple of rewards will reinforce cooperative choices. Punishments/rewards remain limited in number.

Cooperation continues to rise as punishments have signaled that low contributions are costly. Many players increase their contributions. Punishment is targeted and sparse (a continued negative action for the occasional low contributor); reciprocal rewards may appear between mutual cooperators.

Contributions are now relatively high. Players continue to use rewards to reinforce cooperation and reserve punishments for the occasional low contributor. Expect few punitive acts and a couple of reinforcing rewards.
Cooperation stabilizes at a high level. Punishments become rare; rewards are used occasionally to reinforce key contributors and maintain the cooperative norm.

High cooperation continues. One player who temporarily dropped contribution in the previous round (CHICK) is likely to be sanctioned lightly; a few rewards continue as signals supporting leading cooperators. Punishments stay targeted and modest.

Having re-aligned contributions, players largely maintain cooperative levels. Small reinforcing rewards continue and there may be a final sanction to a player slightly below the common norm (e.g., CROCODILE). Overall punishment stays light.
Contribution levels remain high and stable. Players mainly send small rewards to reinforce cooperation; punitive acts become rarer as norms are entrenched.

In the last simulated round here, norms are entrenched and contributions remain high. A final small punishment may target a player slightly below the norm; occasional rewards continue for standout cooperators.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players per round. Each coin you keep yields a private benefit of 1, while each coin contributed returns 3.7 coins to the group, split equally; that means each coin you contribute raises everyone’s payoff by about 0.247 but costs you 1 — so the individually dominant choice is to contribute 0. However, if many players contribute, the group gets higher total payoffs. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, conditional cooperation often emerges: many players start by contributing moderately (hoping others do too), then adjust based on observed behavior.

Punishments (costly to sender and costly to target) and rewards (costly to sender and beneficial to target) give players tools to sanction free riders and encourage cooperators. In practice, punishments and rewards are used sparingly: a few targeted punishments against persistent low contributors and occasional small rewards for high contributors are enough to stabilize intermediate cooperation levels. Expect: initial mid-level contributions, a few punishments targeted at clear free riders, some rewards to high contributors, then modest convergence to a middle-range pattern (not full cooperation, not zero contributions). Because players can’t identify who punished/rewarded them in summaries, actions serve as general social enforcement rather than personalized tit-for-tat.

predicted_rounds.explanation: With no history, many players contribute a fair share (around half) to test others. A few free riders appear; visible low contributors invite a small number of targeted punishments. Generous contributors receive a few small rewards. Punishment and reward use is modest.

Punished low contributors increase their contributions somewhat; most cooperators maintain or slightly adjust contributions. A small additional punishment addresses one persistently low player, and a couple of small rewards continue to recognize high contributors.

Contributors who were rewarded or punished tend to adjust toward the group mean. A few players who remain below the norm get a light sanction. Rewards remain selective and infrequent, mainly to reinforce reliable high contributors.

The group is settling into a middle-range cooperation level. Persistent very low contributors (or those who have not adjusted) may get another light punishment. Generous players continue to receive occasional small rewards.

After several rounds of feedback, most players stabilize. A few who remain below the informal norm receive isolated punishments. Rewards continue to be a small part of enforcement, aimed at keeping cooperators contributing.
Some mild drift downward appears among marginal contributors; a couple of light sanctions are used to deter further free riding. Rewards are still used selectively to reinforce steady contributors.

Occasional lapses occur (one player drops to 0); the group responds with a light punishment to signal that very low contributions are costly. Rewards continue to shore up reliable contributors. Punishments remain targeted and not excessive.

After a clear sanction, the previously zero contributor partially returns to the fold. Contributions are modestly higher on average. The group keeps using single, targeted punishments for very low contributors and occasional small rewards.

With nine interactions of feedback, the group has converged to a stable middle-range cooperation pattern: many players contribute around 8–12, a few remain slightly lower, and punishments and rewards are still used sparingly to maintain norms.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game typically starts with some moderate-to-high contributions as many players try cooperation when the game begins and their horizon is unknown. Because contributions are multiplied (3.7) and shared, group payoff is maximized by high contributions, but individual incentives favor free-riding. Conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if they see too much free-riding; a small number of players may punish persistent defectors to sustain cooperation, and occasionally players reward consistent cooperators. Punishment is costly to the punisher and harsh on the target, so it is used sparingly and typically in small units targeted at obvious under-contributors. Rewards are also used selectively to encourage contributors. Over rounds, contributions may drift downwards as free-riding appears, but targeted punishment and occasional rewards can stabilize cooperation at an intermediate level. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, endgame behavior (sharp collapse) is less pronounced than in known-final-round settings.

predicted_rounds.explanation: At the start players are uncertain and often give a fair amount to test others; a few experimenters contribute little to see payoffs. Punishment is unlikely in round 1, but one player may send a small reward to signal reciprocity to a high contributor.

Players observe revealed contributions (some low contributors stood out). Conditional cooperators slightly reduce contributions; a couple of cooperators may punish clear free-riders to discourage persistent defection, and one reward may appear to reinforce strong contributors.

After seeing punishment of low contributors, some defectors reduce their free-riding slightly but others still contribute little. Punishment continues but remains targeted and sparse; a cooperative player may again reward another cooperator.

Conditional cooperators partially recover their contributions where punishments appeared effective; persistent low contributors may still be targeted with isolated punishment. A cooperator may send a small reward to reinforce a reliable contributor.

Mid-game adjustments continue: some cooperators slightly lower their contributions seeing uneven behavior, while punishments remain infrequent but are used against repeat defectors. Occasional rewards keep incentives for contributors.

Some stabilization occurs: punishers pay the cost and may reduce how often they punish; rewards are used selectively to sustain cooperation among trusted contributors. Overall contributions hold near intermediate levels.

As patterns are clearer, a few players who repeatedly contribute little get targeted again; rewards continue to be given sparingly to reliable high contributors. Overall slight downward drift continues among marginal cooperators.

Uncertainty about future rounds and accumulated small losses from punishment make some reduce contributions further; targeted punishments continue against evident low contributors, and occasional rewards attempt to sustain cooperation among core contributors.

By this later stage (players still unaware of total rounds while playing), contributions remain around an intermediate-low level. Punishment is still used sparingly against persistent low contributors, and small rewards may still be given to reinforce steady cooperators.
